phases.name: Most training-relevant — energy systems, the lifts & sled
Advanced applied — your training model, beyond the textbook

meta.ordering: training-relevance priority (reordered 2026-05-23 from David's chapter ranking)
meta.edition_note: Aligned to the NSCA CSCS exam blueprint (Detailed Content Outline) effective July 1, 2025. The Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning 5th edition (Feb 2026) is now the current text; the exam is blueprint-based, not edition-based, so 4th- and 5th-edition study both map to the tested content.

1.title: Anaerobic training adaptations
1.page_refs: 5E Ch 5 — Neural, Muscular, Connective Tissue, Endocrine & Cardiovascular Adaptations; Detraining
1.key_concept: Anaerobic training adaptations follow a time course: neural gains dominate the first ~6-10 wks (↑high-threshold recruitment, ↑firing/rate coding, ↑synchronization, ↓GTO inhibition/antagonist coactivation), then structural hypertrophy takes over as strength driver (>10 wks). Muscle: ↑fiber CSA (Type II preferential), IIx→IIa MHC shift, ↑CP/ATP/glycogen stores, ↑buffering. Connective tissue lags — tendon stiffness, collagen, BMD respond slowly (bone ~6 mo). Endocrine change is mostly an altered acute response, not resting levels. Cardiac: concentric LV wall thickening, minimal chamber change vs aerobic.
key_facts: Neural drive dominates early (~6-10 wks); structural/hypertrophy drives gains >10 wks (Sale)
Size principle (Henneman): low-threshold/Type I recruited first, then high-threshold/Type II
Fiber CSA detectable ~3-4 wks (9-12 workouts); Type II hypertrophies > Type I
IIx→IIa MHC shift is early; IIx dropped ~18%→7% over 8 wks (Staron); detraining reverses w/ IIx overshoot
CP +28%, ATP +18%, glycogen up to +112% after ~5 mo RT (MacDougall)
MES ≈ 1/10 of fracture force; bone time course ~6 mo; ~30-35 reps enough for osteogenic stimulus
Tendon stiffness ↑ only w/ heavy loads (80% 1RM), not light (20%); Achilles +15-19% / 8 wks
Resting hormones ~unchanged chronically; adaptation = altered ACUTE response + AR receptor content
Cardiac: ↑LV wall thickness/mass (concentric) but little chamber-volume change unlike aerobic
Detraining: strength held ~4 wks; neural loss first, then atrophy; muscle-memory speeds re-gain
1.training_link: Your strength block has run for months — you're well past the neural-only window and into structural gains, so treat progression by adding load, not just reps. Connective tissue and bone lag muscle badly (tendon stiffness needs heavy 80%+ loads, bone ~6 mo), so when you swap Hyrox station work or ramp sled/lunge volume, jump load conservatively to protect tendons that haven't caught up. Keep multijoint axial lifts (squat, clean, deadlift) in — they drive the BMD and stiffness that carry the wall balls and farmer's carries.

glossary.def: Early strength gains from the nervous system: ↑motor-unit recruitment, ↑firing (rate coding), ↑synchronization, ↓Golgi tendon organ inhibition and antagonist coactivation. Dominates before hypertrophy is visible.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="15" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M 21 20 L 35 20 M 21 20 L 35 10 M 21 20 L 35 30" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="10" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="3"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Enlargement of muscle fiber CSA via net accretion of actin/myosin and added myofibrils. Detectable ~3-4 wks; Type II fibers grow more than Type I with heavy load.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="22" cy="20" r="8" fill="#a33344" opacity="0.7"/><line x1="38" y1="20" x2="48" y2="20" stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a2)"/><defs><marker id="a2" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="5" refY="5" markerWidth="4" markerHeight="4" orient="auto"><polygon points="0,0 10,5 0,10" fill="#ffd84a"/></marker></defs><circle cx="60" cy="20" r="13" fill="#a33344"/></svg>

glossary.def: Henneman's rule that motor units are recruited in ascending order of soma size — low-threshold (Type I) first, high-threshold (Type II) last as force demand rises.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="16" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M22 20h10M22 20l10-7M22 20l10 7" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="13" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="3"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Training-driven MHC shift along the continuum from IIx toward IIa (more oxidative). Occurs early; detraining reverses it, sometimes with an IIx overshoot.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="12" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="10" y="22" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><g stroke="#0b1220" stroke-width="1"><line x1="22" y1="12" x2="22" y2="28"/><line x1="40" y1="12" x2="40" y2="28"/><line x1="58" y1="12" x2="58" y2="28"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Threshold bone strain — about 1/10 of fracture force — that must be exceeded to trigger osteoblast activity and new bone formation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Force transmitted per unit of tendon elongation. Rises with heavy resistance training (80% 1RM) but not light loads, improving force transfer to bone.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Loss of accumulated adaptations when training load is removed/reduced. Neural losses come first, atrophy later; strength rarely falls below pretraining.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Quantity of mineral per area of bone; a key strength indicator. Rises with heavy multijoint loading correlated to muscle strength/mass, over a long (~6 mo) time course.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

1.notes_5e: 5E (Roberts & Collins) expands molecular hypertrophy signaling (Akt/mTOR, AMPK, MAPK, myostatin), reframes endocrine adaptation as an altered acute response rather than resting-level change, and adds QCT bone imaging and muscle-memory detraining detail.

2.title: Bioenergetics I — ATP and the phosphagen system
2.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — Phosphagen System; Control of the Phosphagen System; Substrate Depletion & Repletion
2.key_concept: ATP is the immediate energy currency, but muscle holds only ~80-100 g — seconds of work. The phosphagen system rebuilds it fastest: creatine kinase catalyzes CP + ADP → ATP + Cr, a single-step near-equilibrium reaction driven by the law of mass action. Resting CP is 4-6x muscle ATP, so it buffers ATP, which itself drops only ~50-60% even at fatigue. Adenylate kinase, also called myokinase (2 ADP → ATP + AMP), adds a second fast path; its AMP strongly stimulates glycolysis. Highest rate but lowest capacity — dominant in short, high-intensity work.
key_facts: Phosphagen = fastest ATP rate, lowest capacity (Table 3.3 rank 1 rate)
Creatine kinase: CP + ADP → ATP + Cr (single-step, high rate)
Adenylate/myokinase: 2 ADP → ATP + AMP; AMP stimulates glycolysis
Muscle ATP store ~80-100 g; falls only ~50-60% even at fatigue
Resting CP is 4-6x muscle ATP concentration — an ATP reserve
Type II fibers hold more CP → faster phosphagen replenishment
Extremely-high intensity 0-6 s = phosphagen (Table 3.2)
CP repletion: ~50% in 30 s; ATP full ~3-5 min; CP full up to ~8 min
Reactions are near-equilibrium, direction set by law of mass action
2.training_link: Your heavy PM compound lifts and sprint-start work are phosphagen-dominant — highest power, smallest tank. Rest 3-5 min between top strength sets: that is the ATP/CP repletion window, and cutting it to ~60s leaves CP unrestored so Type II force drops on the next set. In Hyrox, short all-out surges (sled push, wall-ball burst) also lean on CP, and ~30s of easy movement already buys back ~50%.

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Mitochondria & Energy Systems

glossary.def: Adenosine triphosphate, the cell's immediate energy currency. Hydrolysis of its terminal bond to ADP + Pi releases the energy that powers contraction.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="9" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="20" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="8" font-weight="700">A</text><circle cx="40" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="40" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><circle cx="55" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="55" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><circle cx="70" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="70" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><g stroke="#a37a08" stroke-width="1"><line x1="29" y1="25" x2="33" y2="25"/><line x1="47" y1="25" x2="48" y2="25"/><line x1="62" y1="25" x2="63" y2="25"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Creatine phosphate (CP), a high-energy phosphate stored in muscle at 4-6x ATP concentration. Donates its phosphate to ADP to rapidly rebuild ATP.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="35" cy="25" r="12" fill="#ff8a3a" opacity="0.85"/><text x="35" y="22" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a1a00" font-size="7" font-weight="700">Cr</text><text x="35" y="32" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a1a00" font-size="7" font-weight="700">~P</text><path d="M 50 25 L 65 25" stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><defs><marker id="arr" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="5" refY="5" markerWidth="4" markerHeight="4" orient="auto"><polygon points="0,0 10,5 0,10" fill="#ffd84a"/></marker></defs><circle cx="78" cy="25" r="10" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="78" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">ATP</text></svg>

glossary.def: Enzyme catalyzing CP + ADP → ATP + Cr. A single-step reaction, so it supplies energy at a very high rate.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="18" fill="#ffd84a" opacity="0.3"/><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="14" fill="#ffd84a" opacity="0.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="10" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="50" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">CK</text></g></svg>

glossary.def: Enzyme catalyzing 2 ADP → ATP + AMP. A second fast ATP-replenishing path; its AMP product is a potent stimulant of glycolysis.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Principle that reactant/product concentrations drive reaction direction. Rising ADP/Pi speeds ATP resynthesis; rising ATP slows or reverses it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A reversible reaction whose direction is dictated by reactant concentrations. The phosphagen reactions run this way, shown by two-way arrows.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

2.notes_5e: 5E frames the phosphagen reactions explicitly as near-equilibrium reactions governed by the law of mass action and gives full CP repletion up to ~8 min (half in ~30 s); it does not cover creatine-supplement dosing in this section.

3.title: Bioenergetics II — Glycolysis & lactate
3.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — Glycolysis; Formation of Lactate; Energy Yield of Glycolysis; Lactate Threshold & OBLA
3.key_concept: Glycolysis breaks down carbohydrate (blood glucose or muscle glycogen) into pyruvate in the sarcoplasm — slower ATP rate than the phosphagen system but far greater capacity. Pyruvate has two fates: reduced to lactate (fast/'anaerobic' glycolysis, regenerating NAD+ for a high but brief flux) or shuttled into mitochondria to acetyl-CoA → Krebs (slow/'aerobic' glycolysis). Fate is set by energy demand, not oxygen availability. Lactate is not waste — it's a fuel and gluconeogenic substrate; ATP hydrolysis, not lactate, drives the H+ accumulation of metabolic acidosis.
key_facts: Net substrate-level ATP: 2 (from glucose) or 3 (from glycogen) — glycogen skips the hexokinase ATP cost
Gross 4 ATP produced; glucose burns 2 (hexokinase + PFK), glycogen burns 1 (PFK only)
Full oxidation via Krebs+ETC: ~32 ATP (glucose) / ~34 (glycogen, malate-aspartate shuttle)
Fast vs slow glycolysis is decided by ENERGY DEMAND, not O2 — glycolysis itself is O2-independent
Lactate is a fuel (type I + cardiac fibers) and Cori-cycle gluconeogenic substrate — NOT the cause of fatigue
Metabolic acidosis = H+ from ATP hydrolysis; LDH reaction actually consumes protons ('lactic acidosis' is a misnomer)
Resting blood lactate 0.5-2.2 mmol/L; type II lactate production 0.5 vs type I 0.25 mmol/g/s
LT (LT1): abrupt rise above baseline; 50-60% VO2max untrained, 70-80% trained; ≈ ventilatory/anaerobic threshold
OBLA (LT2): 2nd inflection ≈ 4 mmol/L; peak blood lactate ~5 min post-exercise; training shifts LT/OBLA right
3.training_link: Your indoor LT1=147 and LT2=158 bpm are your personal versions of the LT and OBLA breakpoints on the blood-lactate curve — steady Zone-2 Hyrox runs sit under LT1, threshold work rides near 158. When your 4x8 intervals average HR 170-175 you're supra-MLSS: lactate production outruns clearance, so the session is intentionally time-limited and drives the rightward shift of your thresholds. Cue easy active recovery after hard pieces — it clears lactate faster than standing still.

glossary.def: Multi-step sarcoplasmic breakdown of glucose or glycogen to 2 pyruvate. Net 2 ATP (glucose) / 3 ATP (glycogen); slower than phosphagen but higher capacity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="20,12 30,5 40,12 40,24 30,31 20,24" fill="none" stroke="#ffcc4a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="30" y="22" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffcc4a" font-size="6">glc</text><line x1="42" y1="18" x2="55" y2="18" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5"/><g fill="#ff8a3a"><circle cx="62" cy="18" r="4"/><circle cx="78" cy="18" r="4"/></g><text x="70" y="38" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">2× pyruvate</text></svg>

glossary.def: Fast: pyruvate → lactate, regenerating NAD+ for rapid but limited flux. Slow: pyruvate → mitochondria → Krebs. Determined by energy demand, not oxygen.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Product of pyruvate + NADH via lactate dehydrogenase. A fuel and gluconeogenic (Cori-cycle) substrate, not waste; its formation consumes protons rather than causing acidosis.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="8" fill="#ff8a3a"/><line x1="28" y1="25" x2="48" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="56" cy="25" r="10" fill="#a04040"/><text x="56" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fff" font-size="6">muscle</text><line x1="66" y1="25" x2="80" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="88" cy="25" r="6" fill="#ff5e5e"/><text x="50" y="45" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">shuttle to heart/liver</text></svg>

glossary.def: Exercise-induced drop in intracellular pH from H+ produced by ATP hydrolysis outside the mitochondria — not from lactate. Impairs glycolytic enzymes and excitation-contraction coupling.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intensity where blood lactate first rises sharply above baseline; ~50-60% VO2max untrained, 70-80% trained. Marks increased glycolytic reliance; tracks ventilatory/anaerobic threshold.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Onset of blood lactate accumulation — second inflection in the lactate curve, ≈ 4 mmol/L. Reflects recruitment of larger (type II) motor units.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Highest intensity where lactate production = clearance. Closely tracks LT2/OBLA; the ceiling of sustainable steady-state work.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="40" x2="90" y2="40" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="10" y1="40" x2="10" y2="5" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><path d="M 10 38 L 25 36 L 40 32 L 50 28 L 58 18 L 70 8 L 85 5" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="55" y1="5" x2="55" y2="40" stroke="#ff5e5e" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/><text x="55" y="50" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ff5e5e" font-size="5">LT2 / MLSS</text></svg>

3.notes_5e: 5E stresses that fast/slow (not aerobic/anaerobic) glycolysis is set by energy demand not O2, and explicitly reframes 'lactic acidosis' as a misnomer — acidosis stems from ATP hydrolysis, and lactate is a fuel.

4.title: Bioenergetics III — Oxidative phosphorylation
4.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — The Oxidative (Aerobic) System: Glucose/Glycogen Oxidation, Fat Oxidation, ETC, Energy Production & Capacity
4.key_concept: The oxidative system is the primary ATP source at rest and in low-intensity work. Pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → Krebs cycle strips H⁺ onto NADH/FADH₂, which feed the electron transport chain; H⁺ pumped down cytochromes builds a proton gradient, and O₂ is the final electron acceptor, forming water. NADH yields ~2.5 ATP, FADH₂ ~1.5, so complete glucose oxidation nets ~32 ATP (glycogen-start 30–36 by shuttle) vs 2 net from glycolysis alone. Oxidative phosphorylation supplies >90% of ATP. Fat via β-oxidation yields far more (>100 ATP/palmitate) but demands more O₂.
key_facts: Net ATP/glucose (blood-glucose start): ~32; glycogen start 30–36 depending on shuttle
ETC yields: 1 NADH ≈ 2.5 ATP, 1 FADH₂ ≈ 1.5 ATP (older texts: 3 and 2)
Per glucose the oxidative path makes: glycolysis 2 NADH, pyruvate ox 2 NADH, Krebs 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ + 2 GTP(→ATP)
O₂ = final electron acceptor → water; oxidative phosphorylation = >90% of ATP synthesis
Fat: hormone-sensitive lipase → FFA → β-oxidation → acetyl-CoA; palmitate >100 ATP each, ~300+ per triglyceride
Fuel at rest ~70% fat / 30% CHO; ↑ intensity shifts toward CHO (near-100% CHO at high-intensity aerobic)
Prolonged steady work: gradual shift CHO → fat as glycogen depletes; protein 3–18% only in long efforts
Rate-limiting Krebs step: isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate (isocitrate dehydrogenase); stimulated by ADP, inhibited by ATP
Rate vs capacity are inverse: phosphagen = fastest rate; fat oxidation = greatest capacity
4.training_link: Your Z2 work (indoor HR cap ~132) sits below the crossover point, so you're burning mostly fat and driving mitochondrial content up — exactly the 5E adaptation that lets you hold a higher % VO₂max before lactate climbs. That bigger aerobic engine spares glycogen for the running stations and compressions on Hyrox day, when CHO oxidation dominates. Long easy volume isn't junk miles — it's remodeling the oxidative machinery.

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán on Zone 2 & Mitochondrial Function

glossary.def: ATP resynthesis in the ETC driven by the proton gradient, with O₂ as final electron acceptor. Supplies >90% of total ATP; ~32 per glucose vs 2 from glycolysis alone.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><ellipse cx="40" cy="20" rx="22" ry="12" fill="#67e8b0" opacity="0.3" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/><path d="M 20 14 Q 40 30 60 14 M 20 26 Q 40 10 60 26" fill="none" stroke="#a8efb8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Mitochondrial reaction series (citric acid / TCA cycle) that oxidizes acetyl-CoA, yielding NADH, FADH₂, and GTP. Two turns per glucose; rate-limited by isocitrate dehydrogenase.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Cytochrome chain that passes H⁺/electrons to build a proton gradient for ATP synthesis. NADH ≈ 2.5 ATP, FADH₂ ≈ 1.5 ATP; O₂ accepts electrons → water.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Breakdown of free fatty acids in mitochondria into acetyl-CoA plus H⁺ carriers. Feeds Krebs directly; huge capacity (>100 ATP per palmitic acid) but O₂-costly.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intensity (~65% VO₂max) where CHO overtakes fat as dominant fuel. Below = fat-dominant (Z2); above = CHO-dominant. Concept beyond the 5E text but consistent with its intensity-driven substrate shift.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: VCO₂/VO₂ used to estimate fuel mix: 0.7 = pure fat, 1.0 = pure CHO. Not defined in 5E Ch 3 but tracks its 70/30 rest split and intensity shift.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

4.notes_5e: 5E updates ETC accounting to NADH ≈ 2.5 / FADH₂ ≈ 1.5 ATP (netting ~32/glucose, 30–36 by shuttle) rather than the older 3/2 → ~36–38; RER and the ~65% crossover point are not in the chapter and are flagged as extensions.

5.title: Energy system interaction & substrate use
5.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — Energy Production & Capacity; Substrate Depletion & Repletion; Oxygen Uptake / EPOC; Metabolic Specificity & Interval Training
5.key_concept: All three systems (phosphagen, glycolytic, oxidative) run at once; contribution is set primarily by intensity, secondarily by duration. Rate and capacity trade off inversely: phosphagen = fastest rate/lowest capacity, fat oxidation = slowest rate/highest capacity. Fatigue tracks depletion of phosphagens and glycogen — not FFAs, lactate, or amino acids. At exercise onset an O₂ deficit is covered anaerobically; afterward EPOC (excess post-exercise O₂ consumption) repays it, restoring PCr/ATP, reloading O₂ in blood and muscle, and clearing metabolites. EPOC scales mainly with intensity. Manipulating work:rest ratios lets you 'select' a target energy system.
key_facts: Duration→primary system: 0-6s phosphagen; 6-30s phosphagen+fast glycolysis; 30s-2min fast glycolysis; 2-3min slow glycolysis+oxidative; >3min oxidative
Rate rank (fastest→slowest): phosphagen > fast glycolysis > slow glycolysis > CHO oxidation > fat/protein oxidation; capacity is the inverse order
PCr can be near-fully depleted by intense exercise; ATP holds up (may drop ≤50-60%) because CP + myokinase + oxidation defend it
PCr repletion: ~half back in 30s; full ATP in ~3-5min; full CP by ~8min — chiefly aerobic, so hard efforts need long work:rest
Muscle glycogen ~300-400g total; liver ~70-100g; glycogenolysis rate rises with intensity (0.7 / 1.4 / 3.4 mmol·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ at 50/75/100% VO₂max)
Glycogen repletion: 0.7-3.0 g CHO/kg every 2h → 5-6 mmol/g wet muscle/h for 4-6h; full replenish ~24h (longer if eccentric damage)
O₂ deficit = anaerobic energy at onset while aerobic system lags; EPOC = O₂ above rest to restore homeostasis (the two are related but NOT equal)
EPOC is intensity-, duration-, and mode-dependent; intensity has the greatest effect — heavy resistance > circuit training
Cycle ergometry: anaerobic dominates efforts up to ~60s; beyond ~60s aerobic progressively becomes primary (roughly 50/50 at ~60s)
5.training_link: Your Hyrox stations tax all three systems at once — sled push/pull sits on phosphagen+fast glycolysis, the 8×1 km runs on oxidative-glycolytic, wall balls and burpees on a mixed floor. Because full PCr rebuild is aerobic and takes up to ~8 min, program true max-power work (heavy sled, sprints) with long work:rest and never let glycogen bottom out — feed 0.7-3.0 g CHO/kg post-session for 24h refill. On race day the effort is continuous, so your ceiling is aerobic capacity and lactate clearance, not raw phosphagen power.

video.title: Most of Your Training Should Be EASY! Polarized Training With Dr. Stephen Seiler

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Energy Systems

5.notes_5e: 5E keeps the same phosphagen/glycolytic/oxidative model but sharpens the recovery numbers (PCr ~30s half / ~8min full; CHO 0.7-3.0 g/kg refuel) and frames EPOC as intensity-primary with an explicit interval work:rest table for metabolic specificity.

glossary.def: The always-on blend of phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative ATP supply, with the dominant contributor shifting by intensity (primary) and duration (secondary).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Inverse trade-off: the faster a system makes ATP, the less total ATP it can make. Phosphagen is highest-rate/lowest-capacity; fat oxidation is the reverse.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Selective using-up of fuels during exercise. Phosphagen and glycogen depletion cause fatigue; FFAs, lactate, and amino acids rarely limit performance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The anaerobically-supplied energy at the start of exercise, before aerobic ATP production ramps up to meet demand.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption — O₂ uptake above resting used to restore homeostasis (PCr/ATP resynthesis, O₂ stores, temperature, circulation). Scales mainly with intensity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Aerobic rebuild of phosphocreatine after intense work: ~50% in 30s, full in ~8min — the reason short maximal efforts need generous rest.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="7" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".8"/><circle cx="40" cy="14" r="5" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="5" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="58" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M27 20h7M45 16l8 3M45 25l8-3" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Interval prescription that biases a target system: ~1:12-1:20 for phosphagen (5-10s work), ~1:3-1:5 for fast glycolysis (15-30s work), and low ratios (near ~1:1) for the oxidative system (>3min work).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#5ec8ff"><rect x="10" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="30" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="50" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/></g><g fill="#3a4a5e"><rect x="20" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/><rect x="40" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: The total energy the anaerobic mechanisms can supply during an all-out effort — the anaerobic contribution to short maximal exercise.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

6.title: Resistance technique — squat
6.page_refs: 5E Ch 16 — Fundamentals (grip, stable positioning, breathing, spotting) + Back Squat checklist
6.key_concept: Back squat is a structural, closed-pronated-grip lift with the bar balanced on the upper back in one of two spots: low bar (across posterior delts at mid-trap, wider grip) or high bar (above posterior delts at base of neck, slightly wider grip). Set feet shoulder-width or wider, toes slightly out. Descend by flexing hips and knees while holding torso-to-floor angle roughly constant, back neutral, elbows high, chest up, heels down, knees over feet. Depth = thighs parallel to floor, or stop earlier if the trunk rounds or heels lift. Extend hips and knees at the same rate to return. Two spotters at the bar ends; use Valsalva only briefly under heavy loads.
key_facts: Two bar positions: low bar = across posterior delts at mid-trap (wider grip); high bar = above posterior delts at base of neck (slightly wider grip)
Grip = closed, pronated; feet shoulder-width or wider, toes slightly out, parallel under the bar
Depth stop = thighs parallel to floor, OR trunk begins to round, OR heels rise — whichever comes first
Descend/ascend keeping torso-to-floor angle constant; hips and knees extend at the SAME rate
5E verbal cues (exact): 'Chest up, head neutral' · 'Spread the floor with your feet' · 'Drive through your heels'
Common errors: heels lift, torso flexes further forward / upper back rounds, knees drift in (hip adduction) or out (hip abduction)
Back squat = structural exercise → warrants spotting; power exercises are NOT spotted
TWO spotters at opposite bar ends, hands 2-3 in (5-8 cm) below bar ends, thumbs crossed, move in unison — NOT one spotter behind
Valsalva under heavy structural loads only; breath-hold kept transient (~1-2 s) — BP can hit ~3× resting
Weight belt indicated for lower-back-stressing lifts at near-max/max loads; not needed for light-load squats
6.training_link: Your low-bar back squat is the anchor of your strength PM block — hold the torso-to-floor angle constant so hips and shoulders rise together and load stays hip-dominant for Hyrox carries and lunges. Coach depth to thighs-parallel or the first sign of a round/heel-lift, not below. Save the Valsalva for your top singles and keep the brace to 1-2 s; on volume back-off sets breathe normally and drop the belt.

video.title: Human Kinetics — How to perform a barbell back squat

audio.title: Iron Culture — Schoenfeld: Mechanisms of Hypertrophy

glossary.def: Knees drifting inward via hip adduction during the squat; a listed common error. Cue 'spread the floor' to keep knees tracking over the feet.
Expiring against a closed glottis with braced abs/rib cage → 'fluid ball' that stiffens the torso and supports the spine. Keep it transient (~1-2 s) as BP can triple.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="30" y="10" width="20" height="22" fill="none" stroke="#c9c9c9" stroke-width="2" rx="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="21" r="6" fill="#ffb86b" opacity="0.7"/></svg>

glossary.def: 5E squat depth: descend until the tops of the thighs are parallel to the floor — or stop earlier if the trunk rounds or the heels rise.
A lift that loads the vertebral column (e.g., back squat, deadlift). Justifies Valsalva under heavy loads and warrants spotting.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Low bar sits across the posterior delts at mid-trapezius with a wider grip; high bar sits above the posterior delts at the base of the neck with a slightly wider grip.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Most strenuous part of a rep, just after the eccentric-to-concentric transition. Exhale through it, inhale on the easier phase (default breathing).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

6.notes_5e: 5E specifies TWO explicit bar positions (low/high) and a three-condition depth stop (parallel OR trunk rounds OR heels rise), and mandates two end-of-bar spotters for the back squat rather than a single spotter behind.

7.title: Resistance technique — deadlift
7.page_refs: 5E Ch 16 — Deadlift, Stiff-Leg Deadlift, Romanian Deadlift, Good Morning (sumo/trap-bar extend 5E Ch 16)
7.key_concept: The deadlift is a hip-and-knee-extension pull from the floor. Set up with feet hip-to-shoulder-width, toes slightly out, bar ~1 in (3 cm) ahead of the shins over the balls of the feet, hands just outside the knees in a closed pronated (or alternated) grip, back neutral/slightly arched, scapulae depressed and retracted, chest up, shoulders over or just ahead of the bar. Drive by extending hips and knees together — keep the torso-to-floor angle constant so hips don't outrun the shoulders — while the bar stays close to the shins. Once past the knees, drive the hips through to a fully erect lockout. Lower under control, spine neutral throughout.
key_facts: Bar starts ~1 in (3 cm) in front of shins, over the balls of the feet; feet hip-to-shoulder-width, toes slightly out
Grip: closed pronated, hands just outside knees; switch to alternated (mixed) grip if load is too heavy to hold pronated
Set-up: back neutral or slightly arched, scapulae depressed + retracted, chest up, shoulders over/slightly ahead of bar, heels down
Pull: extend hips + knees together — keep torso-to-floor angle constant; hips must NOT rise before shoulders
Prime movers: glute max, hamstrings (semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris) + all four quads
Common errors: bar drifting away from body; failing to lock out fully
Stiff-leg DL: knees fixed in slight/moderate flexion; hip hinge only; back must not round, knees must not fully extend, heels stay down — targets hamstrings/glutes + erector spinae
RDL: soft-knee hip hinge, hips pushed back, bar slides down thighs to ~patellar-tendon height (torso ≈ parallel); keep bar on thighs, don't hyperextend at top
Good morning: bar on upper back, hinge to torso ≈ parallel, neutral spine — knees stay soft (over-flexing turns it into a squat)
7.training_link: For your Hyrox demands, the deadlift and its hinge variants build the posterior-chain strength that drives sled push/pull and keeps your back safe under a heavy farmer's carry. Program the conventional pull for max force, and use RDLs and stiff-leg DLs to bulletproof hamstrings against the fatigue-driven form breakdown that shows up late in a race. Cue 'push the floor away' and 'hips and shoulders rise together' — the same brace you want when you deadlift a sandbag with a jelly-legged, redlined heart rate.

video.title: Human Kinetics — How to perform a barbell deadlift

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Strength Protocols

7.notes_5e: 5E gives precise deadlift set-up numbers (bar ~1 in/3 cm ahead of shins, over the balls of the feet, grip just outside the knees) and treats stiff-leg DL, RDL, and good morning as distinct hip-hinge checklists; sumo and trap-bar variants are not in the Ch 16 technique checklist.

glossary.def: Natural lordotic alignment of the spine held rigid under load; the non-negotiable deadlift position — rounding it loses the neutral spine and raises injury risk.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overhand grip with the thumb wrapped around the bar; the default deadlift grip, placed just outside the knees.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: One hand pronated, one supinated; used when the load is too heavy to hold with a double-overhand grip so the bar doesn't roll out.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Movement pattern of flexing/extending at the hips with a near-fixed knee angle and neutral spine; the basis of stiff-leg DL, RDL, and good morning.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The trunk's angle to the ground; keeping it constant off the floor ensures hips and shoulders rise together rather than the hips shooting up first.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Deadlift variant with knees held in slight/moderate flexion; hip hinge lowers the bar with a neutral back — knees never fully extend and heels stay planted.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Top-down hip hinge with soft knees; hips push back and the bar slides down the thighs to ~patellar tendon while the torso reaches parallel, emphasizing hamstrings/glutes.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

8.title: Resistance technique — bench & overhead press
8.page_refs: 5E Ch 16 — Flat Barbell Bench Press; Seated Barbell Shoulder Press; Spotting Free Weight Exercises
8.key_concept: Both presses start from the five-point body contact position (head, upper back/shoulders, buttocks on the bench; both feet flat) with a closed pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder width. Bench: lower to touch chest at ~nipple level, forearms vertical, press up and very slightly back to lockout — no arching or chest-raising to meet the bar. Shoulder press: lower to touch clavicles/anterior delts, extending the neck to let the bar pass the face, then press straight overhead without arching the low back. Both are over-the-face/overhead lifts requiring a spotter with a closed alternated grip inside the athlete's hands.
key_facts: Five-point contact: head, upper back, buttocks on bench + both feet flat on floor
Grip: closed, pronated, slightly wider than shoulder width (both lifts)
Bench: bar touches chest at ~nipple level; press up + very slightly back to lockout
Bench errors: bouncing off chest, lifting hips, no thumb wrap (false grip)
Shoulder press: bar to clavicles/ant. delts; extend neck so bar passes face
Both lifts: forearms perpendicular to floor (bench) / parallel to each other; wrists stiff
Do NOT arch back or rise off seat/bench during either press
Over-the-face + overhead lifts require a spotter; power lifts (push press) are NOT spotted
Spotter uses closed ALTERNATED grip, inside the athlete's hands; assists liftoff then follows bar
DB variation: spot the forearms near the wrists, not the bar
8.training_link: For Hyrox you rarely max these, but they build the pressing durability that wall-balls, burpee-broad-jumps and sled push all draw on. Own the five-point setup and a stiff, vertical forearm on the bench, and drill the neck-back-then-through path on overhead press so you never hunt for low-back arch to finish a rep. When you press overhead loaded, keep ribs down and glutes tight — same bracing you want under the sandbag lunge.

video.title: Human Kinetics — How to perform a bench press

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Maximizing Hypertrophy

8.notes_5e: Stable — 5E keeps the five-point contact model, closed pronated grip, and alternated-grip spotting; it explicitly warns to spot DBs at the wrists (not elbows) and to NOT spot power lifts like the push press.

glossary.def: Stable supine/seated posture: head, upper back/shoulders, and buttocks on the bench plus both feet flat on the floor, held throughout the set for spinal support.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overhand grip, palms down and knuckles up; the standard grip for both the bench and shoulder press.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Thumb wrapped around the bar; opposite of an open/false grip, which risks the bar slipping.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: One hand pronated, one supinated. Used by the spotter (inside the lifter's hands) on over-the-face barbell lifts for a secure catch.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Spotter-assisted move of the bar off the supports to the start position; used in bench and shoulder press.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Hardest part of the rep, usually just after the eccentric-to-concentric transition; exhale through it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Explosive overhead lift where hip/knee drive sends the bar ~1/2 to 2/3 up, then the arms press it out; a power exercise — not spotted.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

9.title: Olympic lifts — power clean & derivatives
9.page_refs: 5E Ch 16 — Power Clean / Power Snatch / Mid-Thigh Pull
9.key_concept: 5E frames the power clean as ONE continuous pull from floor to front-rack, broken into FOUR phases: first pull (floor→knee, extend hips+knees, constant torso angle, hips don't outrace shoulders), transition (bar above knee, hips extend + knees slide forward under bar into the power position — RDL-like), second pull (rapid triple extension of hip/knee/ankle ending in a vertical shrug, heels down as long as possible), and catch (pull under, whip elbows, rack across clavicles/front delts in a quarter-squat, then stand). Arms stay long until the shrug finishes. Bar received with thighs above parallel.
key_facts: 4 phases (5E): first pull → transition → second pull → catch — not 'six positions', 'scoop', or 'double-knee bend'
Triple extension = hip + knee + ankle, capped by a vertical shrug; arms stay straight until then
First pull: keep torso-to-floor angle constant; don't let hips rise before shoulders
Transition = RDL-like: hips extend, knees slide forward under bar → power position, bar stays on thighs
Second pull: heels stay on floor as long as possible to maximize force transfer; shrug BEFORE elbows bend
Catch: quarter-squat, upper arms parallel to floor, bar on clavicles/anterior delts, torso near-erect, feet flat
Grip: pronated, slightly wider than shoulder-width, outside the knees; hook grip if grip is limiting
5E common errors (power clean): letting the bar drift forward off the shins in the first pull, dropping the hips during the second pull, and flexing the elbows before the triple extension is complete
Mid-thigh pull starts in power position (knee ~125-145°, hip ~140-150°); can load to ~140% of clean 1RM
9.training_link: For your Hyrox training the full catch buys little — no sport skill demands racking a bar in a quarter-squat under fatigue. Steal the second pull instead: a clean-grip mid-thigh pull or hang high-pull trains triple-extension power at low technical cost and lets you overload (up to ~140% of clean 1RM). If you're time-boxed, a loaded jump or heavy KB swing delivers similar hip drive with almost no learning curve.

video.title: Human Kinetics — How to perform the power clean

audio.title: Iron Culture 236 — Galpin: Muscle Fiber Types & Training

9.notes_5e: 5E standardizes the clean on FOUR named phases (first pull, transition, second pull, catch) and drops the older 'scoop/double-knee-bend/six-position' language; it also details the mid-thigh pull with explicit power-position joint angles (~125-145° knee, ~140-150° hip) and ~140% 1RM loading.

glossary.def: Floor-to-knee phase: hips and knees extend while the torso-to-floor angle stays constant and the bar tracks close to the shins.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Bar-above-knee phase where hips extend and knees slide forward under the bar into the power position; movement pattern mirrors the RDL.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Torso upright, bar at mid/upper thigh, knees re-bent under the bar — the loaded launch point for the second pull and the start of the mid-thigh pull.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: The explosive core of the lift: rapid triple extension (hip, knee, ankle) finished by a vertical shrug, generating peak power.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Simultaneous forceful extension of hip, knee, and ankle that launches the bar upward.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Athlete pulls under and racks the bar on the front delts/clavicles in a quarter-squat with elbows high, then stands.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Thumb wrapped around the bar and pinned by the fingers — a stronger grip used when grip strength limits the pull.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Derivative starting in the power position (from blocks/rack); trains second-pull power without a catch and tolerates supramaximal loads (~140% 1RM).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

10.title: Alternative modes — kettlebell, sled, strongman
10.page_refs: 5E Ch 17 — Strongman, Weighted Carries, Kettlebell & Sled Training (technique)
10.key_concept: Nontraditional implements (kettlebell, sled, strongman) add specificity, variety and odd-object stability demands. KB swing is hip-hinge driven — momentum floats the bell to eye level, NOT a squat; evidence backs KBs for general fitness, not maximal strength vs weightlifting. Sled push/pull is concentric-dominant → high metabolic and lactate cost but minimal muscle damage, so it recovers fast and suits high frequency. Strongman carries (farmer's, yoke, sandbag, Zercher) demand a braced neutral spine against large, exercise-specific spinal loads. Match implement to sport demand and athlete experience.
key_facts: KB swing = hip hinge; hip/knee extension + momentum floats bell to ~eye level, not arm-lifted; back stays neutral
KBs: good for general fitness; weaker for max strength/jump vs weightlifting (WL +4% VJ / +13.6% squat vs KB +0.8% / +4.5% over 6 wk)
Sled is ~purely concentric → big lactate/metabolic hit (~12.4 mmol/L) but no muscle damage (CK flat) → fast recovery, high frequency
Sled technique: low horizontal trunk/shin, braced spine, short fast steps → longer strides; front foot stays behind hip (push)
Heavy sled loads improve acceleration (≤10 m) but not max velocity; PAPE 15 m sprint gains shown specifically with 66-75% BM loads
Yoke walk = largest spinal compression of the strongman carries; ranked 2nd most dangerous — brace hard
Weighted carries build total-body/trunk stability + grip; keep spine neutral-vertical, no lateral flexion (esp. suitcase carry)
Tire flip = push, not lift; forceful triple extension, drive tire forward; hands-slip biceps tear is the #1 injury
Each strongman lift loads trunk/hip uniquely → variety builds broad spinal stability; reserve for experienced athletes
10.training_link: Sled push/pull is the most Hyrox-transferable implement here — it's concentric-dominant, so you get a big lactate hit with almost no muscle damage; slot heavy sleds in frequently without wrecking recovery, using a low trunk/shin angle and short, fast, driving steps. Farmer's carries build the grip and braced-trunk endurance that carries and the sandbag station demand — keep the spine stacked, no lateral lean. Treat kettlebell swings as conditioning and hinge patterning, not your main strength driver — that stays your barbell work.

video.title: Human Kinetics — How to perform the trap bar deadlift (alternative implement)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Strength Building Protocols

10.notes_5e: 5E expands nontraditional/strongman coverage with detailed technique for sled push/pull, weighted carries (yoke, sandbag, Zercher) and kettlebell swings, and stresses implements are general-preparation/variety tools rather than primary max-strength drivers.

glossary.def: Ballistic hip-hinge in which explosive hip/knee extension swings the bell in an upward arc via momentum, back neutral, arms passive — a hinge, not a squat.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Odd-object lifting (tire flip, log, carries, sled) that imposes high, awkward, exercise-specific trunk/hip loads and instability beyond traditional barbell work.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Loaded walk (farmer's, yoke, sandbag, Zercher, suitcase) that trains grip and total-body/trunk anti-flexion stability while keeping the spine neutral and vertical.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Carrying a heavy frame across the shoulders; produces the greatest spinal compression of the common strongman carries.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Concentric-dominant horizontal drive against a loaded sled; high metabolic/lactate cost with minimal muscle damage, enabling high-frequency use.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Non-barbell training tool (tire, log, stone, sandbag, kettlebell, sled) used to add specificity, variation and stability demand.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Unilateral load carried at one hip; an anti-lateral-flexion trunk task that trains obliques/quadratus lumborum and lateral spinal stabilization.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

11.title: Resistance program design — needs analysis
11.page_refs: 5E Ch 18 — Step 1: Needs Analysis (Evaluation of the Sport; Assessment of the Athlete)
11.key_concept: Needs analysis is program-design variable 1 of 7 and a two-stage process. Stage 1 = evaluate the sport: movement analysis (body/limb patterns, muscles, actions), physiological analysis (strength/power/hypertrophy/muscular-endurance priorities), and injury analysis (common sites + mechanisms, esp. non-contact). Stage 2 = assess the athlete: training/injury status, exercise-technique experience, physical testing, then set ONE primary training goal. Testing goes beyond 1RM — isometric mid-thigh pull (max/rapid force), countermovement jump (slow SSC), rebound jump (fast SSC) on force plates expose whether weak jumps stem from poor strength. Outputs drive every downstream variable.
key_facts: 7 program-design variables; needs analysis is #1 → selection, frequency, order, load/reps, volume, rest follow
Sport eval = movement analysis + physiological analysis + injury analysis (non-contact focus)
Athlete assessment = training/injury status + technique experience + testing → primary goal
Training status tiers (Table 18.1): beginner ≤2mo/none-low, intermediate 2-6mo/basic, advanced ≥1yr/high
Classified on training age, technique/skill, current program, training stress — a continuum, not hard bins
1RM alone misses rapid force; add IMTP (max/rapid force), CMJ (slow SSC), rebound jump (fast SSC) on force plates
Prolonged time-to-takeoff + high jump = adequate impulse but poor force profile → emphasize strength
One primary goal per block (emphasis/de-emphasis); maintain other qualities with retaining loads
80-90% of exercises overlap between athletes; only 10-20% individualized, but loads always individualized
Excess hypertrophy hurts endurance athletes (carry extra mass) but aids collision sports (↑ momentum)
11.training_link: Run your own Hyrox needs analysis first. Sport eval: mixed-metabolic, hip-dominant, high muscular-endurance demand at the 8 stations plus 8x1km running — so cap hypertrophy (extra mass = carrying cost over 60+ min) and prioritize strength-endurance + running economy. Athlete assessment: you're advanced training-status, so 4-6 sessions/wk and heavier loads are fair game — but test with IMTP/CMJ, not just 1RMs, to catch whether a slow sled push is a max-strength gap vs a rapid-force gap, then pick ONE goal per block.

video.title: Galpin — Optimal Protocols (needs analysis discussion)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Maximizing Hypertrophy (programming)

11.notes_5e: 5E reframes needs analysis around modern force-plate testing (IMTP + CMJ/rebound-jump SSC profiling) and updates status tiers via McKay et al. — richer than older editions' 1RM-centric assessment.

glossary.def: Two-stage evaluation (sport requirements + athlete assessment) that is the first step of resistance-program design and dictates all subsequent variables.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Mapping a sport's body/limb movement patterns, joints, muscles, and muscle actions to guide exercise choice.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Ranking a sport's strength, power, hypertrophy, and muscular-endurance priorities.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: An athlete's current preparedness — set by training age, technique skill, and prior program stress — classified as beginner, intermediate, or advanced.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Isometric mid-thigh pull — isometric test of maximal and rapid force production, complementing dynamic 1RM data.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Modified reactive strength index = jump height ÷ time-to-takeoff (CMJ); low mRSI with long TTT flags a strength deficit.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Targeting one primary quality per block while providing maintenance stimulus for others.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Degree to which a gym exercise transfers to sport performance via matching movement patterns and demands.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

12.title: Resistance program — exercise selection
12.page_refs: 5E Ch 18 — Step 2: Exercise Selection (core/assistance, structural/power, movement analysis, muscle balance)
12.key_concept: Exercise selection (Step 2) classifies lifts by muscle size and joint count. Core = multi-joint, large muscle areas (chest/shoulder/back/hip/thigh), high sport transfer (dynamic correspondence) → prioritized. Assistance = single-joint, small areas, weak-link/rehab work. A core lift loading the spine directly (squat) or indirectly (power clean) is structural; a structural lift done explosively is a power exercise. Pick lifts from the sport's movement analysis (joint ROM, muscle actions), keep agonist/antagonist muscle balance, and respect the athlete's technique, equipment, and time. Order: power → other core → assistance.
key_facts: Core = ≥2 primary joints + large muscle area; receives priority (dynamic correspondence)
Assistance = single-joint, small muscle area; used for weak links, injury prevention, rehab
Shoulder complex counts as ONE primary joint; spine counts as one → crunch/back-ext = assistance
Structural = loads spine (directly e.g. squat, or indirectly e.g. power clean) w/ postural stabilization
Power = a structural lift done explosively; use for velocity/RFD (loaded jumps, weightlifting derivatives)
Sport-specific: more similar to sport movement → greater positive transfer (specificity/dynamic correspondence)
Muscle balance = proper agonist:antagonist RATIO, not equal strength; fix imbalances w/ added lifts
Order: power → other core (most demanding first) → assistance; earlier lifts get best strength adaptation
In weak/low-training-age athletes, heavy strength work can raise power as much as ballistic training
12.training_link: For Hyrox, treat squat, deadlift, and heavy sled as your structural core lifts and weightlifting derivatives (hang power clean, jump shrug) as your power tools — program them first while fresh. Balance your hinge-heavy posterior chain against knee-dominant volume, and reserve single-joint accessories (calf raise, hamstring curl, rotator-cuff work) for weak links and durability, not the main event.

video.title: Mike Israetel — How Much Training Volume is Best? (Mesocycle Design)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Hypertrophy Programming

12.notes_5e: 5E keeps the core/assistance + structural/power taxonomy but adds explicit structural/power distinction with deceleration caveats and a weightlifting-derivative loading continuum (fig 18.6) plus squat progression/regression (fig 18.7); the 'tier system' is not 5E terminology.

glossary.def: Multi-joint lift recruiting large muscle areas; prioritized for its direct transfer to sport performance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Single-joint lift working a small muscle area; targets weak links, injury prevention, and rehab.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Core lift that loads the spine directly or indirectly and demands postural stabilization (rigid torso, neutral spine).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A structural exercise performed explosively to emphasize velocity and rate of force development.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Degree to which a training exercise transfers to improved sport performance; higher movement similarity → more transfer.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Proper strength/power ratio between opposing (agonist/antagonist) muscle groups across a joint — not necessarily equal strength.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="12" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="10" y="22" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><g stroke="#0b1220" stroke-width="1"><line x1="22" y1="12" x2="22" y2="28"/><line x1="40" y1="12" x2="40" y2="28"/><line x1="58" y1="12" x2="58" y2="28"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Agonist actively drives the movement (quads in leg extension); antagonist sits on the opposite side (hamstrings).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

13.title: Resistance program — intensity & volume
13.page_refs: 5E Ch 18 — Step 5: Training Load & Repetitions; Step 6: Volume
13.key_concept: Load and reps are inversely related: heavier load → fewer reps. Set intensity as %1RM or as an xRM, then pick reps to hit the goal. RM continuum: strength ≤6 reps at ≥85%; power (multi-effort) 3-5 at 75-85%; hypertrophy 8-12 at 67-80% (but a wide load span, even <60% to failure, grows muscle); endurance ≥12 at ≤67%. Assign 1RM by direct test, estimate from an xRM, or test the goal-rep xRM directly. Volume = sets × reps; volume-load = sets × reps × load (a work proxy). Multiple sets beat single for trained lifters; progress via the 2-for-2 rule.
key_facts: Load/rep by goal (Tbl 18.11): strength ≥85%/≤6; power multi-effort 75-85%/3-5; hypertrophy 67-80%/8-12; endurance ≤67%/≥12
1RM assignment: direct 1RM test → estimate from xRM (e.g. 10RM) → or test the goal-rep xRM directly
Assistance exercises capped at ≤8RM load, even inside a heavy strength block
Volume-load = sets × reps × load (proxy for mechanical work); rep-volume = total reps
Hypertrophy: light loads (<60% to failure) ≈ heavy loads for growth; heavy is more time-efficient + adds strength
Volume (Tbl 18.13): trained strength 2-3 sets; hypertrophy 3-6 sets; multiple sets > single for intermediate/advanced
2-for-2 rule: +load when athlete beats goal reps by ≥2 on the last set for 2 consecutive sessions
Load increase ~2.5-10% (upper body smaller, lower body larger); avoid habitual training to failure
13.training_link: Your strength blocks should live in the ≤6 rep / ≥85% zone — 5x5, 4x4 on squat, deadlift, press — high stimulus, low rep-volume so it stacks cleanly on Hyrox conditioning. Keep assistance work capped at ~8RM to spare joints, and use RIR (leave 1-2 in reserve) instead of grinding to failure so next-day run and erg quality survive. Progress load by the 2-for-2 rule, not by chasing a rep PR every session.

video.title: Dr. Mike Israetel — How Much Training Volume is Best? (MEV/MAV/MRV)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Volume & Frequency for Hypertrophy

glossary.def: One-rep max — most weight liftable once with proper technique. Intensity is often prescribed as a % of it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="20" x2="70" y2="20" stroke="#c9c9c9" stroke-width="3"/><rect x="14" y="10" width="14" height="20" fill="#404040" stroke="#c9c9c9"/><rect x="52" y="10" width="14" height="20" fill="#404040" stroke="#c9c9c9"/><text x="40" y="14" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ffd84a" font-size="9" font-weight="700">1RM</text></svg>

glossary.def: The most weight liftable for exactly x reps to true failure (e.g. a 10RM). Used to assign loads or estimate 1RM without maximal testing.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Load-vs-goal map: low reps/heavy → strength, mid → hypertrophy, high reps/light → endurance. Effects blend at any given RM.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Sets × reps × load — a proxy for mechanical work done in a session. Distinct from rep-volume (total reps).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Progression cue: if the athlete exceeds the goal reps by 2+ on the last set in 2 consecutive workouts, add load next session.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Reps-in-reserve and rating of perceived exertion — subjective, autoregulatory intensity gauges best paired with %1RM. Prescribe as ranges (RIR 2-3, RPE 7-8).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: ~8-12 reps at 67-80% 1RM classically, but growth spans a wide load range (even <60% to failure); mechanical tension is the driver.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#67e8b0"><rect x="14" y="12" width="6" height="20" rx="1"/><rect x="24" y="14" width="6" height="18" rx="1"/><rect x="34" y="10" width="6" height="22" rx="1"/><rect x="44" y="16" width="6" height="16" rx="1"/><rect x="54" y="12" width="6" height="20" rx="1"/><rect x="64" y="14" width="6" height="18" rx="1"/></g></svg>

13.notes_5e: 5E reworks intensity around volume-load, RIR/RPE and set-repetition-best autoregulation, an updated RM continuum showing hypertrophy across a wide load span, and explicit 'avoid habitual failure' guidance — a shift from the older fixed %1RM-only model.

14.title: Resistance program — rest & frequency
14.page_refs: 5E Ch 18 — Step 3 Training Frequency, Step 4 Exercise Order, Step 7 Rest Periods
14.key_concept: Rest length scales with relative load, not just goal: strength and power both use 2-5 min; hypertrophy 2-3 min multijoint, 60-90 s single-joint; endurance ≤30 s. Assign rest per exercise by its actual load (a 12RM lateral raise inside a strength day needs only ~1 min). Frequency rises with training status (beginner 2-3, intermediate 3-4, advanced 4-7/wk) and drops through the season (off 4-6 → in 1-3). At equated weekly volume-load, strength and hypertrophy outcomes are similar regardless of frequency or split vs total-body. Order: power → other core → assistance; alternate upper/lower or push/pull to cut fatigue.
key_facts: Strength: 2-5 min rest; Power: 2-5 min
Hypertrophy: 2-3 min multijoint, 60-90 s single-joint; endurance ≤30 s
Assign rest by RELATIVE load per exercise, not one blanket goal value
Frequency by status: beginner 2-3, intermediate 3-4, advanced 4-7 (may include multiple sessions/day)
Frequency by season: off 4-6, pre 3-4, in 1-3, post/active-rest 0-3
At least 1 but not more than 3 rest days between sessions stressing the SAME muscles
Equated weekly volume-load → strength & hypertrophy similar across frequency and split-vs-total-body
Order: power → other core → assistance (fresh CNS first); alternate upper/lower or push/pull
Microdosing = splitting weekly volume across short sessions to cap fatigue
14.training_link: Your split fits the frequency table: as an advanced hybrid athlete you can run 4-7 lifting touches/wk, but in a heavy Hyrox build you deload lifting toward the in-season 1-3 range so the compromise conditioning volume doesn't blow up recovery. Put your heaviest structural lift (squat/deadlift) first while fresh, and lean on push/pull and upper/lower alternation to microdose strength between run and erg sessions without stealing rest from the same muscles.

video.title: Mike Israetel — Volume Landmarks & Mesocycle Design

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Frequency & Recovery

14.notes_5e: 5E reframes rest by relative load per-exercise (strength AND power both 2-5 min; hypertrophy split multijoint 2-3 min vs single-joint 60-90 s) and adds season-based frequency plus microdosing; stresses volume-equated parity of frequency and split-vs-total-body.

glossary.def: Recovery time between sets/exercises; scales with the relative load lifted and muscle mass involved.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#5ec8ff"><rect x="10" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="30" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="50" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/></g><g fill="#3a4a5e"><rect x="20" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/><rect x="40" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Number of sessions per time period (usually per week) for a given muscle group or the whole body.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Sequence of exercises in one session, arranged so fatigue doesn't degrade the quality of key lifts.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Different muscle groups trained on different days (e.g. upper/lower), allowing higher per-group volume and recovery.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Two back-to-back exercises for opposing muscles (agonist/antagonist), little to no rest between.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#5ec8ff"><rect x="10" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="30" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="50" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/></g><g fill="#3a4a5e"><rect x="20" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/><rect x="40" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Two back-to-back exercises for the SAME muscle group, compounding the stress.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#5ec8ff"><rect x="10" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="30" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="50" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/></g><g fill="#3a4a5e"><rect x="20" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/><rect x="40" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Subdividing weekly training volume across multiple short sessions to limit fatigue when time is constrained.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

15.title: Periodization I — models
15.page_refs: 5E Ch 22 — Models of Periodization; Periodization Periods; Undulating vs Linear (block/DUP extend 5E Ch 22)
