15.key_concept: Periodization = macro-management: sequencing training to manage fatigue and peak at target dates; programming is the micro-management underneath. 5E classifies ALL plans under three PLANNING models — parallel (all qualities at once; novices), sequential (one focused block at a time; advanced/individual sports), and emphasis (concurrent but rotating focus ~every 2 wk; congested schedules). The traditional Matveyev/Stone model shifts high-volume/low-intensity → low-volume/high-intensity across preparatory → competitive periods. 5E stresses this is NOT truly linear — volume-load undulates at micro/meso level. DUP varies load/volume/focus within the microcycle.
key_facts: 3 mechanistic bases: GAS (alarm→resistance→exhaustion), stimulus-fatigue-recovery-adaptation, fitness-fatigue paradigm
Fitness-fatigue: preparedness = fitness − fatigue; fatigue dissipates ~2/3 faster than fitness → why tapers work
Peak holds only ~7-14 days; maintainable duration ∝ inversely to avg training intensity
Hierarchy: multiyear (2-4 yr) → annual → macrocycle → mesocycle (2-6 wk, 4 wk typical) → microcycle (~1 wk) → day → session
Traditional resistance sequence: hypertrophy/strength-endurance → basic strength → strength/power → peaking → maintenance
Hypertrophy: 50-75% 1RM, 3-6×8-20; Basic strength: 80-95%, 2-6×2-6; Strength/power: 30-95% (exercise-dependent), 2-5×2-5
Peaking: very high-to-low int, 1-3×1-3, only 1-2 wk; Maintenance (team in-season): ~85-93%, 2-5×3-6
5E: 'linear periodization' is a misnomer — traditional model is nonlinear (volume-load undulates); DUP evidence mixed, not uniformly superior
Second transition (active rest) ≤4 wk, else detraining → longer re-prep needed
15.training_link: Map your Hyrox build like the traditional sequence: an off-season hypertrophy/strength-endurance base, a basic-strength meso, then a strength/power + race-pace conversion, tapering into a peak for race day. Your 4-week phased blocks with a Phase-0 readiness gate are essentially SEQUENTIAL planning with an unloading microcycle — but the fitness-fatigue rule (fatigue clears ~2/3 faster than fitness) is why your race-week taper drops volume hard while keeping a few sharp, high-intensity efforts.

video.title: Mike Israetel — Mesocycle Design & Volume Landmarks

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Periodization for Hypertrophy

15.notes_5e: 5E reframes periodization under three PLANNING models (parallel/sequential/emphasis) and explicitly rejects 'linear' as a misnomer; block (Issurin) and conjugate (Westside) terminology are NOT in this chapter, so treat them as extensions.

glossary.def: All training qualities developed simultaneously in the same microcycle. Suits novices/youth but total load grows unsustainably for advanced athletes.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Qualities trained in successive focused blocks, each building on the last's residual effect. Suits advanced athletes and individual sports needing high stimulus.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Hybrid: multiple qualities trained concurrently but with the primary focus rotated (~every 2 wk); non-focus qualities held at maintenance. Good for congested schedules.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Load, volume, and training focus vary from session to session within the microcycle (e.g., 6RM / 10RM / 3RM days), rather than only across weeks.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Every bout produces both a fitness and a fatigue aftereffect; preparedness is their summation. Reducing fatigue while retaining fitness raises preparedness.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Rebound above baseline performance after adequate recovery from a training stress (GAS resistance phase); overtraining if stress persists into exhaustion.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Progressive taper of intensity/volume to shed fatigue and maximize performance for a ~1-2 wk window before key competition.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Nested planning units: macro = months-to-year; meso = 2-6 wk block (4 wk typical); micro = ~1 wk of sessions.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

16.title: Periodization II — macro, meso, micro
16.page_refs: 5E Ch 22 — Periodization Hierarchy; Periodization Periods; Sport Seasons
16.key_concept: Periodization is the macro-management scaffold that sequences training to peak at set dates; programming is the micro-management fill (modes, loads, sets). Hierarchy nests: multiyear → annual plan → macrocycle (months–year) → mesocycle (2–6 wk, 4 common) → microcycle (days–2 wk, 1 wk common) → day → session. The traditional model runs 4 periods — preparatory (general→specific prep: hypertrophy/strength-endurance then basic strength), first transition (strength/power), competitive (peaking or maintenance), second transition (active rest). Volume ↓ and intensity ↑ across the plan; peak holds only ~7–14 days.
key_facts: Cycles: macro = months–year; meso = 2–6 wk (4 wk most common); micro = days–2 wk (1 wk / 7 days common)
Periodization = macro-mgmt scaffold; programming = micro-mgmt (modes, methods, loads)
4 periods: preparatory → first transition → competitive → second transition (active rest)
Prep splits general prep (hi vol, lo int, hypertrophy) then specific prep (basic strength, ↑int)
RT phases/loads: hypertrophy 50–75% 1RM, 3–6×8–20; basic strength 80–95% 1RM, 2–6×2–6; strength/power 30–95% 1RM, 2–5×2–5; peaking 50–≥93% 1RM, 1–3×1–3; maintenance 85–93% 1RM, 2–5×3–6
Peak performance holds only ~7–14 days; maintainable duration ↓ as avg training intensity ↑
Fatigue dissipates ~2/3 faster than fitness → preparedness rises when fitness kept, fatigue shed
3 mechanistic theories: GAS (alarm→resistance→exhaustion), stimulus–fatigue–recovery–adaptation, fitness–fatigue paradigm
Second transition (active rest) 1–4 wk max; longer → detraining → needs longer prep to rebuild
"Linear" is a misnomer — volume-load undulates at micro/meso level; core tenet is removing linearity
16.training_link: Map your Hyrox build to this: an off-season prep period (hypertrophy/strength-endurance base + long Zone-2 volume), then a strength/power first transition (heavy compounds, ski/sled power), then a competitive peak — cut volume ~40–50%, hold intensity, and time race day inside that 7–14 day window. Because fatigue clears faster than fitness, your taper unmasks fitness rather than losing it. Run 3–4 wk mesocycles with an unloading final microcycle, and keep active rest under 4 weeks so you don't need to rebuild your aerobic base.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 201: Past, Present & Future

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán: Zone 2 (block periodization)

16.notes_5e: 5E (Haff) reframes periodization as the macro-scaffold vs programming, formalizes the emphasis model alongside parallel/sequential, and explicitly rejects the 'linear periodization' label older editions implied.

glossary.def: Largest practical training block, several months to a year, containing preparatory, competitive, and transition periods; usually one per competitive season.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Medium block of linked microcycles, 2–6 weeks (4 most common), each with a single training focus like hypertrophy or basic strength.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Smallest cycle of several workouts, days to 2 weeks, most often 1 week; where daily load undulation manages fatigue.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Adaptive rebound above baseline after a stress is recovered from; the GAS resistance-phase overshoot that periodization times to competition.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Preparedness = fitness aftereffect minus fatigue aftereffect; since fatigue clears ~2/3 faster than fitness, reducing load unmasks fitness and peaks preparedness.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Competitive-period strategy shifting toward very high intensity and very low volume to shed fatigue and hold peak form for ~1–2 weeks.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: 1–4 week restorative period of low-load, non-specific activity between macrocycles to recover physically and mentally without full detraining.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#5ec8ff"><rect x="10" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="30" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/><rect x="50" y="10" width="7" height="20" rx="2"/></g><g fill="#3a4a5e"><rect x="20" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/><rect x="40" y="22" width="8" height="8" rx="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Hybrid planning model targeting several factors at once but rotating the primary emphasis (~every 2 wk); some qualities are trained, others maintained or allowed slight detraining.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

17.title: Plyometric program design
17.page_refs: 5E Ch 19 — Mechanics & Physiology of Plyometrics; SSC; Program Design (intensity, frequency, recovery, volume, progression); Safety
17.key_concept: Plyometrics reach max force in min time via the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC). Two models: mechanical (rapid eccentric stretch loads the series elastic component—mostly tendon—storing elastic energy) and neurophysiological (muscle-spindle stretch reflex potentiates the concentric action). Three SSC phases: eccentric (load) → amortization (transition) → concentric (release). Short amortization is critical—delay leaks energy as heat and kills the reflex. Drills classified ballistic (squat jump, throws) vs rebound (depth/drop jumps, higher output). Program variables mirror resistance training: mode, intensity, frequency, recovery, volume (foot contacts), progression.
key_facts: SSC phases: eccentric → amortization → concentric; short amortization = keep elastic energy + stretch reflex
Mechanical model = SEC (mostly tendon) stores/returns elastic energy; neuro model = muscle-spindle stretch reflex potentiates
Ballistic (min SSC neuro contribution: squat jump, MB throw) vs rebound (depth/drop jumps); rebound → ↑ muscle activation, power, impulse
Fast SSC <0.25 s ground contact; slow SSC >0.25 s (Schmidtbleicher)
Volume = foot contacts/session: beginner 80-100, intermediate 100-120, advanced 120-200 (intensity-dependent; ↑intensity → ↓volume)
Frequency 1-4×/wk; 48-72 h recovery between sessions → usually 2-3×/wk; DOMS/power dip up to 72 h
Depth-jump recovery: ~5-10 s between reps, 2-5 min between sets; work-to-rest ratio around 1:10 (set by drill volume/type)
Strength guide: repeated jumps ~1× BW squat; bounds/depth off ≤12 in box ~1.5× BW; depth off >12 in box ~2× BW (RFD matters, not just magnitude)
Depth-jump box: 16-42 in (41-107 cm), norm 30-32 in; >220 lb (100 kg) athletes cap ≤18 in (46 cm); >48 in defeats the purpose
Surface: grass/suspended floor/rubber mat OK; concrete/tile/hardwood NO; thick mats (≥6 in) & mini-tramps extend amortization
Land credit-card cue: active dorsiflex→plantarflex, midfoot/forefoot, heels off, shoulders over knees over toes; avoid dynamic valgus (ACL risk)
Program length ~6-10 wk; jump gains as early as 4 wk; do plyos BEFORE aerobic work (aerobic interferes with power)
Depth jumps contraindicated in prepubescents (open epiphyseal plates); caution in masters
17.training_link: For Hyrox, plyos build the reactive stiffness that lowers ground-contact time and boosts running economy—train the fast SSC (pogos, low hops, short bounds) rather than max-height jumps. Bank foot contacts as budgeted volume (start ~80-100/session) and keep 48-72 h between hard plyo days so DOMS doesn't blunt your run splits. Put plyos before Zone-2 or long runs, and cap depth-jump box height given your bodyweight and run-loaded joints.

video.title: Galpin — Optimal Protocols (plyometric programming)

audio.title: Iron Culture — Hypertrophy & Power Adaptations

glossary.def: Rapid eccentric pre-stretch → short amortization → concentric action. Combines stored elastic energy (SEC) with the muscle-spindle stretch reflex to amplify concentric force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="none" stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="2"><path d="M 12 32 L 20 20 L 28 32 L 36 12 L 44 32 L 52 8 L 60 32 L 68 4"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: The transition/coupling phase between eccentric and concentric actions. Must stay short—a long delay dissipates stored elastic energy as heat and negates the stretch reflex.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 30 L 30 6" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 30 6 L 32 6" stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="4"/><path d="M 32 6 L 70 30" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Elastic structures in series with the contractile machinery—mostly tendon plus crossbridges. Stretches like a spring during the eccentric phase, storing and returning elastic energy.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Ballistic drills use minimal SSC neuro contribution (squat jump, MB throw); rebound drills require physiomechanical SSC (depth/drop jumps) and produce higher activation, power, and impulse.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Each time a foot (or both feet) strikes the surface. The unit for prescribing lower-body plyometric volume per session.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Classified by ground-contact time: fast SSC <0.25 s, slow SSC >0.25 s (Schmidtbleicher). Reactive/rebound drills target the fast SSC.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A 'shock' drill: step off a box, land, and immediately rebound. Box height sets intensity; recommended 16-42 in (norm 30-32 in), capped low for heavy athletes.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Inward collapse of the knees on landing. A major risk factor for ACL and patellofemoral injury—coach knees-over-toes alignment.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

17.notes_5e: 5E softens the old absolute 1.5× BW squat rule into a strength-scaled continuum (~1×/1.5×/2× BW by drill), foregrounds RFD over peak-strength magnitude, and adds the ballistic-vs-rebound classification and fast/slow SSC framing.

18.title: Aerobic endurance training adaptations
18.page_refs: 5E Ch 6 — Chronic Adaptations to Aerobic Exercise; Adaptations to Aerobic Endurance Training; Detraining
18.key_concept: Chronic aerobic training reshapes every system. Central: LV chamber + wall grow, contractility ↑ → ↑max SV → ↑max Q (max HR flat/slightly ↓), resting bradycardia (40-60) from parasympathetic tone. Peripheral: ↑capillary density, ↑mitochondrial size/number, ↑myoglobin, ↑oxidative enzymes, ↑glycogen/triglyceride stores, more fat oxidation → glycogen sparing. Blood/plasma volume ↑ and hemoglobin mass ↑ (but hematocrit may dip from dilution). VO₂max = max Q × max a-vO₂ diff; it plateaus in ~6-12 mo, after which economy and lactate threshold carry further gains.
key_facts: VO₂max = max Q × (a-vO₂ diff); Q = HR × SV
VO₂max ↑ 5-30% depending on start point/genetics; elite show minimal change
Most VO₂max gains achieved in 6-12 mo; then economy + LT drive performance
↑max SV is the main lever for ↑max Q; max HR unchanged or slightly ↓
Resting HR drops to 40-60 (parasympathetic tone ↑) + faster HR recovery
Capillary density ↑ (fn of volume+intensity), mitochondria ↑ size & number, myoglobin ↑
Fiber SIZE can ↓ ~20% with marathon training while function maintained; Type IIx→IIa shift
Plasma/blood volume ↑, hemoglobin mass ↑, but hematocrit may ↓ (dilution)
Detraining: VO₂max ↓ 4-14% short-term (<4wk), 6-20% long-term — driven by ↓blood vol, ↓SV, ↓max Q, ↑submax HR
Running (impact) builds bone; cycling/swimming (weight-supported) do not
18.training_link: Your Jan→May trajectory (HRV +59%, RHR -9) is this central+peripheral remodeling in real time — bigger SV, fuller plasma volume, more mitochondria. With base solidified, your next gains come from LT and economy, not VO₂max: sub-threshold 10×1000m and short-recovery 4×8 above MLSS push the OBLA point up. For Hyrox, remember fiber SIZE may shrink under high aerobic volume — keep concurrent strength in to defend the sled/carry stations.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 101

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — Zone 2 Deep Dive (Part 2)

glossary.def: Max O₂ uptake = max cardiac output × max a-vO₂ difference. Criterion index of cardiovascular fitness; plateaus ~6-12 mo of good training.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="32" x2="70" y2="32" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="10" y1="32" x2="10" y2="6" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><path d="M 10 30 Q 18 22 30 14 Q 50 8 70 8" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intensity where lactate production outpaces clearance. Aerobic training raises the absolute and %VO₂max at which it occurs; keeps improving after VO₂max plateaus. Yours: 147/158 indoor.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 32 L 22 30 L 35 26 L 45 22 L 55 12 L 70 4" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="35" y1="6" x2="35" y2="32" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/><line x1="55" y1="6" x2="55" y2="32" stroke="#ff7a7a" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Aerobic training increases mitochondrial size and number plus oxidative enzymes, boosting O₂ extraction. Why Z2 volume matters even when easy.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><ellipse cx="20" cy="20" rx="10" ry="6" fill="#67e8b0" opacity="0.5"/><ellipse cx="45" cy="20" rx="10" ry="6" fill="#67e8b0" opacity="0.7"/><ellipse cx="65" cy="20" rx="10" ry="6" fill="#67e8b0"/></svg>

glossary.def: Blood ejected per beat. Rises via bigger LV chamber + wall + contractility; the primary driver of ↑max cardiac output and resting bradycardia.
O₂ extracted between arterial and venous blood. Widens with ↑capillary density, mitochondria, and myoglobin — the peripheral half of VO₂max.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Partial/complete loss of adaptations from insufficient stimulus (training reversibility). Distinct from tapering, which is a planned volume cut that preserves fitness.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: O₂ (energy) cost of a fixed pace/power. Improves via biomechanics + neuromuscular efficiency; can separate two athletes with equal VO₂max and LT.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

18.notes_5e: 5E adds neural adaptations and multisport interference, hemoglobin-mass-vs-hematocrit dilution, and explicit fiber-size shrinkage under aerobic volume; widens VO₂max gain range to 5-30% and quantifies detraining (4-14% short / 6-20% long).

19.title: Aerobic endurance program design
19.page_refs: 5E Ch 21 — Designing an Aerobic Endurance Program (5 design variables) + Types of Aerobic Endurance Training + Training Seasons; polarized/Zone-2 extend 5E Ch 21
19.key_concept: 5E builds every aerobic program from 5 ordered variables: mode → frequency → intensity → duration → progression, tuned to the athlete's own strengths/weaknesses (not a copied elite plan). Intensity and duration trade off inversely and drive adaptation. Four session types map to intensity zones: extensive endurance (5E's preferred name for LSD; ≤65% VO2max, ~30–120 min, builds mitochondria/fat use/LT), pace/tempo (at MLSS, steady or intermittent 'cruise' intervals, ~20–30 min), interval (near VO2max, 3–5 min, 1:1 W:R), HIIT (>MLSS/critical VO2, short bouts), and fartlek (unstructured speed play mixing them). Progress one variable ≤10%/wk. Modern polarized/Zone-2 refines the intensity distribution.
key_facts: 5 design variables IN ORDER: mode → frequency → intensity → duration → progression
Mode = specificity: train the competition movement; cross-train only to maintain
Extensive endurance (LSD): ≤65% VO2max, ~30–120 min, 'conversational' → ↑mitochondria, fat use, LT, type IIx→I shift
Pace/tempo: at MLSS, ~20–30 min, steady OR intermittent (cruise/tempo intervals) → ↑economy, LT, MLSS
Interval: near VO2max, 3–5 min work, 1:1 W:R → ↑VO2max; needs aerobic base first, use sparingly
HIIT: >MLSS/critical VO2, short bouts (Tabata = 8×20s work/10s rest); need several min >90% VO2max per session
Fartlek: 'speed play', ~20–60 min mixing LSD + tempo + interval intensities
Progression: ↑ only ONE variable, ≤10%/wk (beginners from low base may exceed); prefer time over distance
Intensity ↔ duration inverse: higher intensity → shorter duration
Fox APMHR = 220 − age (SD 10–12 bpm); Karvonen THR = HRR×%intensity + RHR (HRR = APMHR − RHR)
Taper: 7–28 days, ↓volume while holding intensity/frequency (linear, step, or progressive)
19.training_link: For Hyrox, your 'mode' is the compromised-running + station transitions the race actually demands — pure treadmill work under-specifies it. Anchor ~80% of your weekly sessions in extensive-endurance Zone 2 (conversational, fat-burning base), and reserve your 4×8 at HR 170–175 as the ~20% supra-MLSS pole per Seiler's session-count 80/20. Progress one variable ≤10%/wk, prefer time over distance, and taper 7–14 days into a race by cutting volume while keeping a few sharp intervals to stay race-crisp.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training in Practice

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán: Zone 2 Programming

19.notes_5e: 5E rebrands LSD as 'extensive endurance', foregrounds MLSS/critical power alongside LT, and adds MED/MTD dosing plus concurrent-strength and heat/altitude sections; polarized and Zone-2 concepts extend beyond the chapter.

glossary.def: 5E's preferred name for long slow distance: prolonged low-intensity work (≤65% VO2max, ~30–120 min) building aerobic base, mitochondrial density, and fat oxidation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Training at ~MLSS to develop race-pace feel; run steady (~20–30 min) or as intermittent cruise/tempo intervals. Improves LT, MLSS, and economy.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Maximal lactate steady state — highest intensity where lactate production still equals clearance; the heavy/severe boundary, synonymous with critical power/speed.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Swedish 'speed play' — unstructured session mixing easy running with faster hill or burst efforts, blending LSD, tempo, and interval intensities.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: THR = (HRR × intensity%) + RHR, where HRR = APMHR − RHR. Reserve-based, so it individualizes target HR to resting HR.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Least training stimulus that still maximizes adaptation while minimizing injury/fatigue; paired with maximum tolerated dose (MTD), the upper end still yielding benefit.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intensity distribution putting ~80% of sessions easy (below LT1) and ~20% hard (above LT2), avoiding the middle 'gray zone'. Extends 5E's zone framework.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Systematic pre-race ↓ of volume (7–28 days) while holding intensity/frequency, restoring glycogen and freshness for peak performance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

20.title: Biomechanics II — Force-velocity & length-tension
20.page_refs: 5E Ch 2 — Muscle Length, Joint Angle, Muscle Contraction Velocity, Levers of the Musculoskeletal System
20.key_concept: Muscle force depends on length, velocity, and leverage. Peak force sits near resting length, where actin–myosin overlap gives max potential crossbridges; too stretched → fewer aligned sites, too short → excessive overlap, both ↓ force. As concentric velocity ↑, force ↓ (steepest drop at slow speeds, per A.V. Hill), so eccentric > isometric > concentric. Because movement is rotation, muscle force acts as torque (force × moment arm). Most limb muscles work at a mechanical disadvantage (MA < 1.0), so internal muscle forces far exceed the external load.
key_facts: Force order: eccentric > isometric > concentric
F–V (Hill): concentric torque ↓ as angular velocity ↑; decline steepest at slow speeds (non-linear)
Eccentric torque ↑ with velocity up to ~90°/s (~1.57 rad/s), then gradual ↓
Length–tension peak = resting length (max actin–myosin overlap); too long or too short ↓ force
Titin: mechanosensing 3rd filament; tightens with Ca²⁺ in eccentrics → aids force + hypertrophy signaling
Torque = force × moment arm; MA = muscle moment arm ÷ resistive moment arm
MA > 1.0 → muscle force < load; MA < 1.0 → muscle force > load
Levers: 1st-class = forces on opposite sides of fulcrum; 2nd = same side, MM > MR (advantage); 3rd = same side, MM < MR (disadvantage, most limb muscles)
Free-weight resistive moment arm = horizontal distance from load to joint → resistive torque varies through ROM (sticking points)
20.training_link: Your PM heavy squats/deadlifts train the high-force, low-velocity left side of the F–V curve; sled pushes and box jumps develop the right side. Program eccentric-emphasis lowers to exploit that eccentric force ceiling and drive titin-linked adaptation. For Hyrox, recognize that a wall-ball or lunge sticking point is a moment-arm problem — the resistive torque peaks mid-range — so cue athletes to accelerate hardest where leverage is worst.

video.title: Dr. Andy Galpin: Muscle Fiber Types and Muscle Growth (force-velocity context)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: How to Build Strength, Muscle Size & Endurance

glossary.def: Force ↓ as concentric velocity ↑; eccentric force rises with speed to ~90°/s then plateaus. Order: eccentric > isometric > concentric.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 8 Q 25 12 40 22 Q 55 32 70 36" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 10 12 Q 25 4 40 8 Q 55 18 70 36" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Force is maximal at resting length (optimal actin–myosin overlap); falls when the muscle is stretched (fewer aligned sites) or overly shortened (excessive overlap).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 32 Q 25 28 40 8 Q 55 28 70 32" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="40" y1="6" x2="40" y2="34" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Perpendicular distance from a force's line of action to the fulcrum. Longer moment arm → more torque per unit of force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Rotational effect of a force about a joint = force × moment arm. Muscle force must be expressed as torque because movement is joint rotation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Ratio of muscle moment arm to resistive moment arm. >1.0 lets muscle force be less than the load; <1.0 forces it to exceed the load.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Elastic third filament with mechanosensing properties; tightens under eccentric load and calcium influx, contributing to force and hypertrophy signaling.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Preferred over 'contraction': concentric (shortening, force > load), eccentric (lengthening, force < load), isometric (no length change, force = load).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="12" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="10" y="22" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><g stroke="#0b1220" stroke-width="1"><line x1="22" y1="12" x2="22" y2="28"/><line x1="40" y1="12" x2="40" y2="28"/><line x1="58" y1="12" x2="58" y2="28"/></g></svg>

20.notes_5e: 5E expands the length–tension mechanism to feature titin as a mechanosensing third filament driving eccentric force and hypertrophy signaling — new emphasis vs older editions.

21.title: Performance nutrition — timing & supplements
21.page_refs: 5E Ch 11 — Pre/During/Post-Competition Nutrition, CHO Loading, Hydration, Daily Protein, RED-S
21.key_concept: Nutrient timing scaffolds performance around the session. Pre: 1-4 g/kg CHO ≥4 h out (or ~1 g/kg at 2 h), low-fat/low-fiber, familiar. During: fluid + 28-144 g/hr CHO for efforts >45 min, using multiple sugar types (glucose+fructose/maltodextrin) so absorption isn't capped by one transporter; single-sugar exogenous CHO oxidation tops out ~1.0-1.1 g/min. Post: 1.0-1.85 g/kg/hr CHO to refill glycogen (fast insulin-independent phase 30-60 min) plus 20-40 g high-leucine protein. The old narrow 'anabolic window' is really a wider 'garage door,' but urgency rises when the next session is <24 h away. Total daily CHO/protein matter more than timing precision.
key_facts: Pre (≥4 h): 1-4 g/kg CHO + 0.15-0.25 g/kg PRO; at 2 h ≈1 g/kg CHO; smaller/liquid CHO closer in
During-event CHO for efforts >45 min; 5E band is 28-144 g/hr; higher rates need mixed sugars (glucose+fructose/maltodextrin, separate gut transporters)
Single-sugar exogenous CHO oxidation caps ~1.0-1.1 g/min; fructose/galactose oxidize 25-50% slower
Sport drink: 5-10% CHO (6-8% ideal), Na⁺ 20-30 mEq/L, K⁺ 2-5 mEq/L; >8% CHO delays gastric emptying
Limit sweat loss to <2% body weight; mouth-rinse CHO alone ↑ ~1 h performance 2-3% (CNS effect)
Post glycogen: 1.0-1.85 g/kg/hr CHO; fast insulin-independent phase 30-60 min; adding PRO ↑ synthesis if CHO <1.2 g/kg/hr
Post protein: 20-40 g high-quality, high-leucine (2-3 g leucine); older adults ≥40 g; 20-50 g resistance range
CHO loading: 8-10 g/kg/day (up to 10-12 pre-marathon) → glycogen ↑ 20-40%; benefits events >90 min
Daily protein: endurance 1.4-2.0, strength/power 1.4-2.0, hypertrophy/weight-gain 1.6-2.2 g/kg/day; 20-40 g/meal every 3-4 h
Energy availability = (intake − exercise EE)/kg FFM; optimal ~45 kcal/kg FFM/day, LEA/RED-S risk <30
21.training_link: For your long Zone-2 blocks and Hyrox race sim (>90 min), practice a high hourly CHO intake (well up the 28-144 g/hr band) with a glucose+fructose gel/drink in training so your gut is trained race-day — 2% dehydration is enough to cost you. After hard doubles or a race sim with <24 h before the next session, hit CHO hard early (~1 g/kg/hr) plus 30-40 g whey; on normal days total daily CHO and protein (spread 20-40 g every 3-4 h) matter more than obsessing over the post-workout clock. Watch your EA in heavy blocks — chronic under-fueling tanks recovery and raises injury risk.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Resistance Training & Time Efficiency

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition #123 — Leucine Kinetics & mTOR (Layman PhD)

glossary.def: Combining glucose + fructose (or maltodextrin) uses separate intestinal transporters, raising exogenous CHO oxidation and absorption above the ~1 g/min single-sugar ceiling — letting the athlete reach the upper end of the 5E's 28-144 g/hr during-event band.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Post-exercise period of heightened nutrient sensitivity. Wider than once believed ('garage door,' not a small window), but timing matters most when the next session is <24 h away.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: 8-10 g/kg/day CHO for ~3 days pre-event (with taper) to supercompensate muscle glycogen ~20-40% above normal. Benefits continuous efforts >90 min.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Dietary energy intake minus exercise energy expenditure, divided by kg fat-free mass. ~45 kcal/kg FFM/day is optimal; <30 signals low EA and RED-S risk.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport — dysfunction across bone, menstrual, metabolic, immune, and cardiovascular systems from sustained low energy availability. Strong predictor of injury.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: ~2-3 g leucine (or ~0.05 g/kg) per serving maximally triggers muscle protein synthesis; leucine content, not total grams, drives the acute response.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

21.notes_5e: 5E reframes the post-exercise 'anabolic window' as a wider 'garage door,' adds a dedicated RED-S/energy-availability section (EA = intake−EEE per kg FFM; optimal ~45, LEA <30), and stresses total daily intake and sex-specific CHO-loading caveats; detailed ergogenic-supplement dosing (creatine/caffeine/beta-alanine/bicarbonate/nitrate) lives in the separate supplements chapter, not Ch 11.

22.title: Basic nutrition — macronutrients
22.page_refs: 5E Ch 10 — Macronutrients, Vitamins, Minerals, Fluid & Electrolytes
22.key_concept: Three macronutrients fuel and build the athlete: CHO and PRO yield ~4 kcal/g, fat ~9 kcal/g. Protein supplies amino acids (9 essential, 4 non, 7 conditional) for repair, enzymes, hormones — RDA 0.8 g/kg, but athletes need 1.2-2.0 g/kg. CHO is the main energy substrate, stored as glycogen (~15 g/kg BW; ¾ muscle, ¼ liver); GI/GL rank blood-glucose response. Fat carries vitamins A/D/E/K, makes hormones, and is a near-limitless fuel store. Vitamins/minerals are micronutrient cofactors; water (AI men 3.7 L, women 2.7 L) plus electrolytes govern thermoregulation and performance.
key_facts: Energy: CHO 4 · PRO 4 · FAT 9 kcal/g; alcohol 7
AAs: 9 essential, 4 nonessential, 7 conditionally essential (20 total)
PRO RDA 0.8 g/kg; endurance 1.2-2.0, strength 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day
20-40 g quality PRO (0.25-0.40 g/kg) every 3-4 hr; total daily intake > timing
Glycogen ~15 g/kg BW; ¾ muscle, ¼ liver; CHO not strictly essential (gluconeogenesis)
GL = GI × g CHO per serving ÷ 100; ref food GI = 100
Sat fat <10% kcal, added sugar ≤10% kcal; omega-3/6 are essential FAs
Water AI: men 3.7 L, women 2.7 L/day; >2% BW loss impairs performance
Rehydrate ~1.5 L fluid per kg BW lost; sport drink 20-30 mEq Na/L, 5-10% CHO
22.training_link: Your Z2 + interval + strength + Hyrox load sits you at the top of every range: CHO ~6-10 g/kg on big days, protein 1.6-2.0 g/kg split into 4-5 feeds of 20-40 g so MPS stays topped up around double sessions. Nail a personal sweat-rate test — losing >2% bodyweight in a Hyrox sim tanks power and pace, and chasing thirst with plain water risks hyponatremia, so build sodium (20-30 mEq/L) into your intra-race drink.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Resistance Training, Body Composition & Hypertrophy

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition #123 — Donald Layman PhD: Leucine, mTOR & Anabolic Response

glossary.def: ~2.5-3 g leucine per meal (≈20-40 g quality protein) needed to maximally switch on muscle protein synthesis.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="14" fill="#7ed68a" opacity="0.8"/><text x="40" y="24" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0a2008" font-size="10" font-weight="700">L</text></svg>

glossary.def: Storage polysaccharide of glucose, ~15 g/kg body weight — three-quarters in skeletal muscle, one-quarter in liver. Depletion limits high-intensity endurance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#ffb84a"><circle cx="25" cy="20" r="5"/><circle cx="35" cy="14" r="5"/><circle cx="45" cy="20" r="5"/><circle cx="35" cy="26" r="5"/><circle cx="55" cy="14" r="5"/><circle cx="55" cy="26" r="5"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Muscle protein synthesis. Rises after training + protein; net balance stays negative if trained fasted. Sensitivity persists ~24 hr post-lift.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 8 32 L 18 30 L 26 12 L 38 8 L 48 20 L 60 28 L 72 30" fill="none" stroke="#7ed68a" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: GI × grams of carbohydrate per serving ÷ 100. Accounts for portion size, so a more realistic gauge of blood-glucose response than GI alone.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: One of 7 amino acids normally made by the body but that must come from diet during illness or stress (e.g., glutamine, arginine, cysteine).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Blood sodium <130 mmol/L from over-drinking low-sodium fluid; causes swelling, cramps, and at <120 mmol/L seizures/coma.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Heme (animal) iron ~15-35% absorbed and unaffected by meal content; nonheme (plant) iron only 2-20% absorbed, blunted by phytates/tannins/calcium, boosted by vitamin C.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

22.notes_5e: 5E moves basic nutrition to Ch 10 with a new RD author, adds glycemic load, expanded vitamin/mineral DRI-UL tables, and detailed sweat-rate/hyponatremia and rehydration (1.5 L/kg) guidance.

23.title: Endocrine I — Anabolic hormones
23.page_refs: 5E Ch 4 — Primary Anabolic Hormones (Testosterone, GH, IGF); Manipulating the Endocrine System
23.key_concept: The main anabolic hormones are testosterone (androgen; testes + adrenal cortex; genomic + rapid non-genomic action; ↑ protein synthesis, ↓ breakdown, ↑ neural drive), growth hormone (anterior-pituitary somatotrophs; the 22 kD isoform is exercise-sensitive; drives liver IGF-I, lipolysis, collagen), and IGF-I (liver-made from GH stimulation, plus local muscle production from mechanical load). Acute exercise-induced spikes — not resting levels — track with remodeling, and the effect ultimately depends on receptor availability/binding. Response magnitude scales with large-muscle exercises, heavy-to-moderate load, high total work, and short rest.
key_facts: Testosterone: testes (Leydig) + adrenal cortex; women ~5-10% of male levels; free T only ~0.5-2% of total
T acute-spike protocol: large muscles (squat/DL/clean), 85-95% 1RM, mod-high volume, short rest 30-60s
GH = 191-aa 22 kD isoform (exercise-sensitive) + larger aggregate storage forms; secreted by somatotrophs
GH release driven by metabolic stress — ↑lactate + ↓pH; short rest (~1 min) + high total work maximize it
IGF-I: liver-synthesized from 22 kD GH; ALSO produced locally in muscle from mechanical load (MGF splice variant)
Local (autocrine/paracrine) muscle IGF-I often matters more than circulating IGF-I, which may not change
Acute hormonal rise correlates with hypertrophy/remodeling; resting levels change little with training
Heavy 1-3RM + long rest (>3 min) → little hormonal rise but max motor-unit recruitment; still builds strength
Effect gated by receptors: overstimulation → downregulation (e.g., insulin resistance)
Pre/post carb+protein blunts measured T (greater muscle uptake) but upregulates androgen-receptor content
23.training_link: Your big multi-joint Hyrox work — heavy sled + squat + wall-ball complexes with tight rest — is exactly the large-muscle, high-total-work, short-rest recipe that maximizes the acute T/GH/IGF-I environment. But the payoff is gated downstream: sleep is your limiter (median 5.5h vs ~9.8h need), and GH pulses in slow-wave sleep, so short nights blunt the anabolic window your sessions create. Feed pre/post carb+protein — it upregulates androgen-receptor content even though it lowers measured blood T.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Updated View on Mechanisms of Muscle Hypertrophy (Iron Culture 181)

audio.title: Stronger By Science Podcast — Episode 9: Hormones & Training Adaptations
audio.credibility: Greg Nuckols MSc + Eric Trexler PhD

glossary.def: Primary androgen from Leydig cells (testes) + adrenal cortex. Anabolic + anticatabolic: ↑ amino-acid incorporation, ↓ protein breakdown; acts via genomic androgen receptors and rapid non-genomic membrane receptors; also boosts neural drive.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="30" cy="20" r="10" fill="none" stroke="#ffb84a" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="20" r="10" fill="none" stroke="#ffb84a" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="3" fill="#ffb84a"/></svg>

glossary.def: Polypeptide from anterior-pituitary somatotrophs. Exercise-sensitive 22 kD isoform plus larger aggregate stores; ↑ liver IGF-I, protein synthesis, lipolysis (glycogen sparing), collagen. Metabolic-stress driven (↓pH).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g stroke="#ffb84a" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"><path d="M 10 30 L 18 30 L 18 12 L 22 12 L 22 30 L 32 30"/><path d="M 38 30 L 46 30 L 46 18 L 50 18 L 50 30 L 60 30"/><path d="M 65 30 L 70 30"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Insulin-like growth factor-I. Made in liver via 22 kD GH stimulation and locally in muscle from mechanical load. ↑ protein synthesis + amino-acid transport; ~98% bound to IGFBPs, which gate its activity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Only the unbound (free) fraction of a hormone can interact with target tissue; binding proteins (SHBG, albumin, IGFBPs) act as reservoirs that protect and extend half-life.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Chronic overstimulation desensitizes or reduces the number of receptors, blunting cellular response. Classic example: insulin resistance from sustained high insulin.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Splice variant of IGF-I that muscle fibers produce in response to mechanical stress; acts directly on the muscle cell (autocrine/paracrine) rather than via the bloodstream.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

23.notes_5e: 5E stresses that receptor availability/binding and local (autocrine/paracrine) muscle IGF-I gate the response — the acute blood spike is only part of the story.

24.title: Endocrine II — Catabolic hormones & stress
24.page_refs: 5E Ch 4 — Adrenal Gland/Cortisol; Catecholamines in Resistance Exercise
24.key_concept: Two adrenal stress systems drive the catabolic side of the endocrine response. The cortex, cued by ACTH via the HPA axis, secretes cortisol (chief glucocorticoid): it spares glucose, fuels gluconeogenesis, and mobilizes fat — adaptive acutely. If it fails to return to baseline within ~24 h it turns catabolic/anti-anabolic: ↓protein synthesis, ↑protein breakdown, blunted anabolic signaling, immune suppression. The medulla fires catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine) via sympathetic drive — the fight-or-flight arm: ↑force, ↑contraction rate, ↑HR/BP, glycogenolysis, and it primes testosterone release.
key_facts: Adrenal cortex → cortisol (glucocorticoid), driven by ACTH via HPA axis
Adrenal medulla → catecholamines (epi, norepi, dopamine) via direct sympathetic stimulation
Cortisol acute = adaptive (glucose sparing, gluconeogenesis, fat oxidation); chronic >24h = catabolic/anti-anabolic
Chronic cortisol ↓protein synthesis, ↑protein degradation, suppresses immune function
Cortisol ↑ with high-volume, large-muscle, short-rest resistance work
Trained athletes convert cortisol→cortisone more efficiently (protective adaptation)
Catecholamines ↑ force, contraction rate, BP, blood flow (vasodilation); prime testosterone secretion
Epinephrine correlates with blood lactate (metabolic stress marker)
Overtraining → adrenal exhaustion (↓catecholamine output) + β2-receptor downregulation → ↓contractile force
24.training_link: Your HRV climb (118→187 ms) is the autonomic read on this — high HRV = parasympathetic dominance = the catecholamine/cortisol axis has stood down between sessions. Stack heat (33°C), sleep debt, and underfueling on top of dense Hyrox blocks and you keep cortisol above baseline past 24 h, where it goes catabolic. Build in true rest days and lower-volume weeks so the adrenal glands recover before you chase adaptation again.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Mechanisms of Muscle Hypertrophy (Iron Culture 181)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Recovery & Stress

24.notes_5e: 5E frames cortisol's acute rise as adaptive (glucose sparing, remodeling) and stresses the ~24 h return-to-baseline threshold, plus the trained cortisol→cortisone conversion adaptation, rather than treating cortisol as purely catabolic.

glossary.def: Primary glucocorticoid from the adrenal cortex; raises glucose availability under stress but is catabolic and anti-anabolic when chronically elevated.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine from the adrenal medulla; the fight-or-flight hormones that boost force, contraction speed, and cardiovascular output.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal pathway; hypothalamus → ACTH from anterior pituitary → cortisol from adrenal cortex, the systemic regulator of the stress response.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources (amino acids, glycerol); a cortisol-driven route to spare/supply glucose during prolonged stress.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Reduced medullary capacity to secrete catecholamines effectively after continuous high stress without varied training or rest.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Loss of receptor number or affinity from chronic overstimulation; e.g., overtraining downregulates β2-receptors, blunting epinephrine's effect on force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

25.title: Warm-up & flexibility
25.page_refs: 5E Ch 15 — Performance Preparation, Mobility, and Flexibility
25.key_concept: A warm-up primes the body via temperature effects (↑ muscle/core temp, ↓ tissue viscosity, disrupted connective-tissue bonds, Bohr-effect O2 release) and non-temperature effects (↑ blood flow, ↑ baseline VO2, potentiation). 5E frames it with the RAMP model — Raise, Activate & Mobilize, Potentiate — replacing the old general/specific + static-stretch template. Flexibility is ROM about a joint; the chapter reframes it as mobility (ROM under motor control). Stretch types: static, dynamic, precontraction (PNF). Dynamic stretching is preferred pre-performance; static held >60 s can transiently blunt force/power.
key_facts: RAMP = Raise → Activate & Mobilize → Potentiate (Jeffreys); supersedes general/specific-only structure
Warm-up ~10-20 min; effects dissipate if >15 min gap before activity
>80% of studies show warm-up ↑ performance (jumps, sprints, endurance)
Static stretch harmless if <60 s; decrement grows past that, worst >60 s; <45 s = small but real dip
Potentiate exploits treppe/staircase + PAPE — the more speed/power the sport needs, the more critical this phase
PAPE (postactivation performance enhancement): 60-90% 1RM heavy resistance, jumps, resisted sprints; 3-12 min rest; highly individual, fatigue trade-off
Static stretch hold 15-30 s (30 s optimal, diminishing returns beyond); mild discomfort not pain
Weekly stretch VOLUME (frequency) > single-session duration for ROM gains; PNF exception = ~1 session/wk
PNF: hold-relax-with-agonist-contraction most effective (reciprocal + autogenic inhibition); isometric 3-6 s at ≤20% MVC
No consistent stretching–injury-prevention link; both hypo- and hyper-flexibility ↑ injury risk
25.training_link: For your Hyrox and 4x8 strength days, build the warm-up as RAMP, not stretching: Raise with sled-mimic or run drills, Activate & Mobilize through squat/lunge/hinge patterns, then Potentiate with build-up runs and ramping loads (50/70/90%) so rep-1 or the first station feels primed, not flat. Skip long static holds before power work — save PNF/static ROM for post-session or separate recovery days.

video.title: Squat University — How To Squat Correctly (FIX MISTAKES) — includes warm-up

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Optimal Protocols (warm-up & potentiation)

25.notes_5e: 5E (Jeffreys) reframes warm-up around the RAMP model and mobility/PAPE, moving past the older general/specific + mandatory static-stretch template and raising the static-stretch caution threshold to >60 s.

glossary.def: Warm-up framework: Raise physiological params via sport-pattern movement, Activate & Mobilize key movement patterns, Potentiate with progressive high-intensity efforts to peak the athlete.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Final RAMP stage: staged increase to session intensity, exploiting contractile history (treppe) so muscle activates with less neural drive.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Postactivation performance enhancement — a low-volume high-intensity conditioning stimulus transiently boosts subsequent force/power; balanced against fatigue.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Capacity to move freely and effectively through a movement — flexibility plus motor control, stability and coordination; more sport-relevant than static ROM.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Controlled, active movement through a large ROM (mobility drills) that mimics sport patterns; preferred warm-up method, avoids the bounce of ballistic stretching.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Slow move to end ROM held 15-30 s; slow speed avoids the stretch reflex; effective for ROM but can transiently cut force if held long pre-performance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: GTO-mediated reflexive relaxation of a muscle after its own active contraction, enabling a deeper passive stretch (basis of PNF).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Reflexive relaxation of a muscle when its antagonist contracts; contributes to the extra ROM in hold-relax-with-agonist-contraction PNF.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

26.title: Skeletal muscle architecture & fiber types
26.page_refs: 5E Ch 1 — Skeletal Muscle Microstructure, Contraction, Motor Units & Muscle Fiber Types (Tables 1.1–1.2)
26.key_concept: Skeletal muscle nests hierarchically: muscle → fasciculus → fiber (cell) → myofibril → sarcomere, the contractile unit spanning Z-line to Z-line (~2.5 µm relaxed, ~4,500/cm). Thick myosin (~16 nm) and thin actin (~6 nm) interdigitate; 6 actin surround each myosin. Ca²⁺ frees actin's binding sites, then cross-bridge cycling — ATP hydrolysis cocks the head, Pi release drives the power stroke, ADP release + new ATP binding detaches it — slides actin inward, shrinking the H-zone and I-band. Fibers grade by myosin-heavy-chain isoform: Type I (slow, oxidative, fatigue-resistant), IIa (fast, intermediate), IIx (fast, high force, low endurance). Motor units recruit by the size principle.
key_facts: Hierarchy: muscle → fasciculus → fiber → myofibril → sarcomere; sarcomere = Z-line to Z-line (~2.5 µm relaxed)
Filament geometry: 6 actin around each myosin; myosin ~16 nm / up to 200 molecules, actin ~6 nm double helix
Cross-bridge cycle: ATP hydrolysis (→ADP+Pi) cocks head → binds actin → Pi release = power stroke → ADP off + new ATP binds → detach; no ATP = rigor
Contraction shrinks H-zone + I-band; force ∝ number of actin–myosin cross-bridges formed
3 fiber/motor-unit types: I (slow-twitch), IIa (fast-twitch, fatigue-resistant), IIx (fast-twitch, fatigable)
Type I: high mitochondria, capillaries, myoglobin, aerobic enzymes; small diameter, low force, high fatigue resistance, red
Type IIx: large diameter, highest force/power/velocity, high anaerobic enzymes, low endurance, white; IIa is intermediate
Size principle: low-threshold slow units recruited first; heavier/explosive loads add high-threshold fast units
Force graded 2 ways: motor-unit recruitment + rate coding (firing frequency → twitch summation → tetanus)
Heavy resistance training shifts IIx → IIa (not Type II → Type I); slow/fast proportion largely genetic
26.training_link: Your aerobic base (indoor Z2 cap HR 132, LT1 147) is built on Type I oxidative capacity — the fatigue-resistant fibers carrying easy running and the back half of a Hyrox. Your 4×8 threshold sessions (HR 170–175) lean on the Type IIa pool: fast fibers you must make fatigue-resistant to survive repeated station blocks. Program heavy compounds and explosive work too — that's how you recruit and grow the high-threshold IIx/IIa units that drive sled push, wall balls, and the sprint finish.

glossary.def: Smallest contractile unit, bounded Z-line to Z-line (~2.5 µm relaxed). Shortens when actin slides inward over myosin, shrinking the H-zone and I-band.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#ff5e5e" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="70" y1="6" x2="70" y2="34" stroke="#ff5e5e" stroke-width="3"/><rect x="10" y="14" width="40" height="2" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="10" y="24" width="40" height="2" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="30" y="14" width="40" height="2" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="30" y="24" width="40" height="2" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="28" y="12" width="24" height="16" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity="0.7" rx="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Thick filament (~16 nm); each molecule has 2 heavy + 4 light chains and globular heads that form cross-bridges and carry the ATPase driving the power stroke.
Thin filament (~6 nm double helix) anchored at the Z-line; its myosin-binding sites are exposed when Ca²⁺ shifts tropomyosin off them.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="10" cy="20" r="4"/><circle cx="22" cy="20" r="4"/><circle cx="34" cy="20" r="4"/><circle cx="46" cy="20" r="4"/><circle cx="58" cy="20" r="4"/><circle cx="70" cy="20" r="4"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Repeating attach–pull–detach of myosin heads on actin. Needs Ca²⁺ (to expose sites) and ATP (to power the stroke and detach); absent ATP → rigor.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Small, red, mitochondria/capillary/myoglobin-rich, low-threshold, fatigue-resistant, low force/RFD. Postural + endurance work.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="16" fill="#a04040" opacity="0.8"/><circle cx="32" cy="14" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="44" cy="12" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="48" cy="24" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="30" cy="26" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Fast, intermediate diameter and fatigue resistance; blends aerobic + anaerobic capacity. The trainable middle of the fast pool.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Large, white, high anaerobic enzymes; highest force, power, and contraction speed but fatigues fastest. Sprint/max-effort fibers.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="16" fill="#f0d8a0" opacity="0.85"/><g stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" fill="none"><path d="M 30 14 L 50 14 M 28 20 L 52 20 M 30 26 L 50 26"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Motor units recruit low→high threshold: slow units first, fast units added only as force demand rises (heavy or explosive efforts).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="16" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M22 20h10M22 20l10-7M22 20l10 7" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="13" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="3"/></g></svg>

26.notes_5e: 5E classifies motor units/fibers by myosin heavy-chain isoform (I/IIa/IIx) and cites 2021 proteomics showing hundreds of proteins differ across the three fiber types; sarcomere language uses 'Z-line' (not 'Z-disc').

27.title: Motor units & the size principle
27.page_refs: 5E Ch 1 — Neuromuscular System, Activation of Muscles, Motor Unit Recruitment Patterns
27.key_concept: A motor unit = one motor neuron + all the fibers it innervates; those fibers are metabolically alike, so a unit is slow-twitch (Type I), fast-twitch/fatigue-resistant (IIa), or fast-twitch/fatigable (IIx). By the all-or-none principle, firing a motor neuron contracts every fiber in its unit. Muscle grades force two ways: recruitment (number of active units) and rate coding (firing frequency). The size principle orders recruitment small→large — low-threshold Type I first, high-threshold Type II only as demand rises — so heavier or more explosive efforts pull in the fast units.
key_facts: Motor unit = 1 motor neuron + fibers it innervates; all same type
All-or-none: fire the neuron → every fiber in that unit contracts
Force graded by recruitment (# units) + rate coding (firing frequency)
Size principle: low-threshold Type I → higher-threshold IIa → IIx as demand ↑
Weighted curl recruits BOTH low-threshold slow + high-threshold fast units (unweighted = low only)
Small muscles (eye/hand) mostly rely on rate coding; large (thigh) also need heavy recruitment
Maximal high-intensity weightlifting EMG ≈ ≥90% of available MUs activated
Twitch summation → unfused → fused tetanus = max tetanic force of a unit
IIx: largest neuron, highest threshold, fastest, most force/RFD, least fatigue-resistant
Full motor-neuron-pool activation likely NOT achievable in untrained people
27.training_link: Your strength blocks must reach true high-intensity or explosive effort — that's the only way to drive recruitment up the size principle into Type IIx. Zone-2 bike work sits far below threshold and only cycles low-threshold Type I units, so it can't substitute for heavy or maximal-intent lifting. Program heavy loads AND fast concentric intent so both recruitment and rate coding get trained for Hyrox's sled and wall-ball demands.

video.title: Motor unit recruitment & Henneman's size principle

glossary.def: One motor neuron plus all muscle fibers it innervates. Every fiber in the unit is the same type and contracts together.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="15" cy="25" r="8" fill="#5ec8ff"/><line x1="23" y1="25" x2="60" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="60" y1="25" x2="80" y2="15" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="60" y1="25" x2="80" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="60" y1="25" x2="80" y2="35" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5"/><rect x="80" y="12" width="14" height="6" fill="#a04040" rx="2"/><rect x="80" y="22" width="14" height="6" fill="#a04040" rx="2"/><rect x="80" y="32" width="14" height="6" fill="#a04040" rx="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Motor units are recruited smallest to largest: low-threshold Type I first, higher-threshold Type IIa then IIx as force demand rises.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="35" r="6" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="40" cy="32" r="9" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="65" cy="28" r="13" fill="#ff7a7a"/><line x1="10" y1="45" x2="90" y2="45" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><text x="50" y="49" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="6" text-anchor="middle">Force demand →</text></svg>

glossary.def: Grading force by the firing frequency of a motor neuron; faster firing makes twitches summate into greater, then tetanic, force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"><path d="M 10 25 L 18 25 L 18 12 L 22 12 L 22 25 L 30 25"/><path d="M 35 25 L 40 25 L 40 12 L 43 12 L 43 25 L 48 25 L 48 12 L 51 12 L 51 25 L 56 25"/><path d="M 60 25 L 64 25 L 64 12 L 66 12 L 66 25 L 70 25 L 70 12 L 72 12 L 72 25 L 76 25 L 76 12 L 78 12 L 78 25 L 85 25"/></g><text x="20" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">low</text><text x="48" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">moderate</text><text x="73" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">high</text></svg>

glossary.def: Increasing force by activating additional motor units; dominant force mechanism in large muscles.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="16" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M22 20h10M22 20l10-7M22 20l10 7" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="13" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="3"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: An action potential in a motor neuron activates every fiber of its unit, or none — there is no partial activation of a single unit.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Fusion of overlapping twitches at high firing frequency into a sustained maximal force; unfused (partial) then fused (smooth).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The force/effort level at which a given motor unit turns on. Low for Type I, high for Type II.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

27.notes_5e: 5E frames the size principle through the weighted-vs-unweighted arm-curl figure and does not attribute it to Henneman by name; adds the ~90% MU-activation EMG figure and the untrained-pool caveat.

28.title: Neuromuscular junction & excitation-contraction coupling
28.page_refs: 5E Ch 1 — Sliding-Filament Theory; Excitation-Contraction Coupling; Neuromuscular System
28.key_concept: A motor-neuron action potential reaches the axon terminals → releases acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction → ACh binds sarcolemmal receptors → an AP fires along the sarcolemma and down the T-tubules → the sarcoplasmic reticulum dumps Ca²⁺ into the myofibrils. Ca²⁺ binds troponin, shifting tropomyosin off actin's myosin-binding sites. Myosin heads (ATP hydrolyzed → cocked) attach, release Pi to power-stroke, then ADP release + fresh ATP detaches them — crossbridge cycling. Force ∝ number of crossbridges bound. Relaxation: stimulus stops, ATP-driven pumps re-sequester Ca²⁺ into the SR.
key_facts: NMJ neurotransmitter = acetylcholine (ACh); binds receptors on sarcolemma → sarcolemmal AP
AP travels down T-tubules → triggers Ca²⁺ release from SR (stored near Z-lines)
Ca²⁺ binds troponin → shifts tropomyosin → exposes myosin-binding sites on actin
Sliding-filament (Huxley & Hanson, 1954): actin slides over myosin → H-zone & I-band shrink; A-band unchanged
Crossbridge cycle: myosin hydrolyzes ATP → cocked; binds actin; Pi release → power stroke; ADP off + new ATP → detach
ATP needed for BOTH power stroke (via hydrolysis) AND detachment (fresh ATP binding) → no ATP = rigor
Force ∝ number of actin-myosin crossbridges bound at any instant
Relaxation: ATP-dependent pumps return Ca²⁺ to SR → crossbridges fall off
All-or-none: firing a motor neuron contracts ALL fibers it innervates; sarcomere ≈ 2.5 µm relaxed
28.training_link: Every hard rep you grind out in a 4×8 or a wall-ball set is crossbridge cycling gated by Ca²⁺ handling and ATP supply — as ATP falls late in a Hyrox station, detachment slows and force sags. Caffeine sensitizes the SR to release more Ca²⁺ per impulse, one reason a pre-session dose sharpens force output. Train tetanic force and rate of Ca²⁺ handling with heavy, fast intent, not just volume.
