video.credibility: Ninja Nerd Medical Lectures · Prof. Zach Murphy

audio.title: Huberman Lab — How to Build Strength, Muscle Size & Endurance

glossary.def: Neurotransmitter released at the neuromuscular junction. Binds sarcolemmal receptors, and once enough are activated an action potential fires along the muscle fiber.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="10" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity="0.3"/><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><g fill="#ffd84a"><circle cx="35" cy="20" r="2.5"/><circle cx="45" cy="28" r="2.5"/><circle cx="55" cy="22" r="2.5"/></g><rect x="65" y="15" width="25" height="20" fill="#a04040" opacity="0.4" rx="3"/><g fill="#a04040"><circle cx="73" cy="22" r="2"/><circle cx="82" cy="22" r="2"/><circle cx="73" cy="30" r="2"/><circle cx="82" cy="30" r="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Sarcolemmal invagination that carries the action potential from the fiber surface into its depths, so Ca²⁺ is released simultaneously throughout the cell.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="5" y="5" width="90" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" rx="2"/><line x1="30" y1="5" x2="30" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="55" y1="5" x2="55" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="80" y1="5" x2="80" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Tubule network encasing each myofibril that stores Ca²⁺ near the Z-lines and releases it on activation; ATP-dependent pumps reclaim it during relaxation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 25 Q 25 10 50 25 T 90 25" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2.5"/><g fill="#ffb86b"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="14" r="2"/><circle cx="50" cy="20" r="2"/><circle cx="65" cy="14" r="2"/><circle cx="80" cy="20" r="2"/></g><text x="50" y="45" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="6" text-anchor="middle">Ca²⁺ released</text></svg>

glossary.def: Repeating sequence: ATP-cocked myosin binds actin → Pi release drives the power stroke → ADP release + new ATP detaches the head. Needs Ca²⁺ and ATP.
Huxley & Hanson (1954): actin filaments slide inward over stationary myosin, pulling Z-lines together; H-zone and I-band shorten while the A-band stays constant.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The link from sarcolemmal excitation to mechanical contraction: AP → T-tubule → SR Ca²⁺ release → troponin/tropomyosin shift → crossbridge formation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Regulatory proteins on actin. Ca²⁺ binds troponin, which shifts tropomyosin off the myosin-binding sites, permitting crossbridge formation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="12" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="10" y="22" width="60" height="6" rx="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><g stroke="#0b1220" stroke-width="1"><line x1="22" y1="12" x2="22" y2="28"/><line x1="40" y1="12" x2="40" y2="28"/><line x1="58" y1="12" x2="58" y2="28"/></g></svg>

28.notes_5e: Stable core physiology; 5E frames it explicitly as resting → excitation-contraction coupling → contraction → relaxation phases and stresses force ∝ crossbridge number.

29.title: Cardiovascular system — heart, vessels, blood flow
29.page_refs: 5E Ch 1 — Cardiovascular System; Blood Vessels; Blood; Acute Responses to Aerobic Exercise
29.key_concept: The heart is a two-sided pump driven by its own conduction system: SA node (60-80/min) → internodal paths → AV node (delay) → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers, all modulated by sympathetic (speeds SA discharge) and parasympathetic tone. Output = cardiac output (Q = SV × HR), ~5 L/min at rest rising to 20-22 L/min maximally. HR climbs linearly with intensity; SV rises until ~40-50% VO₂max then plateaus, governed by end-diastolic volume (Frank-Starling) and catecholamine contractility. Blood flows arteries → arterioles (resistance/control) → capillaries (exchange) → venules → veins (reservoir, ~64% of volume).
key_facts: Q = SV × HR; rest ~5 L/min → max ~20-22 L/min (≈4× rest)
SV rises to ~40-50% VO₂max then plateaus; max SV ~100-120 mL untrained men, ~150-160 mL trained
Frank-Starling: ↑venous return → ↑EDV → stretched fibers → more forceful contraction → ↑SV
↑Venous return via venoconstriction + skeletal-muscle pump + respiratory pump
SA node 60-80/min > AV node 40-60 > ventricular fibers 15-40 (fastest pacemaker wins)
Resting HR 60-100 bpm; <60 = bradycardia, >100 = tachycardia
HRmax ≈ 220 − age (±12 bpm); better: 208 − 0.7 × age
Blood volume at rest: veins/venules ~64%, pulmonary ~9%, heart ~7%, arterioles+capillaries ~7%
Exercise BP: SBP → 220-260 mmHg max; DBP flat or slightly ↓ (vasodilation)
29.training_link: Your RHR falling 46→37 bpm with HRV rising 118→187 ms (Jan→May) is textbook Frank-Starling adaptation — a bigger, better-filling LV ejects more per beat, so HR drops for the same resting Q. During a Hyrox run-station block, your venous return climbs via the skeletal-muscle pump and respiratory pump, defending SV even as HR pushes toward max — which is why holding a controlled cadence keeps EDV high instead of letting a spiking HR shrink filling time.

glossary.def: Blood pumped per minute = SV × HR. ~5 L/min rest, 20-22 L/min max. Central determinant of oxygen delivery and VO₂max.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 40 32 C 28 22 22 14 28 8 C 32 4 38 6 40 12 C 42 6 48 4 52 8 C 58 14 52 22 40 32 Z" fill="#ff5e5e" stroke="#7a1a26" stroke-width="1"><animate attributeName="transform" type="scale" values="1;1.08;1" dur="0.9s" repeatCount="indefinite" additive="sum"/></path></svg>

glossary.def: Blood ejected per beat. Rises with intensity to ~40-50% VO₂max then plateaus; endurance training enlarges the LV chamber → higher SV and lower HR for the same Q.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="25" cy="20" r="14" fill="#ff5e5e" opacity="0.4"/><circle cx="55" cy="20" r="16" fill="#ff5e5e"/><text x="55" y="24" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fff" font-size="8" font-weight="700">trained</text></svg>

glossary.def: Greater end-diastolic filling stretches myocardial fibers, producing a more forceful contraction and larger SV. Force of contraction scales with fiber length.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Volume in the left ventricle at the end of filling (diastole). The preload that Frank-Starling acts on; ↑venous return raises EDV.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Fraction of EDV ejected per beat. Rises with sympathetic drive during exercise as cardiac emptying improves.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: SA node → internodal paths → AV node (delays impulse) → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers. SA node is the dominant pacemaker at 60-80/min.
Small muscular resistance vessel controlling blood flow into capillaries; can fully close or dilate many-fold to match tissue demand.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: HR × systolic BP; the double product, an index of myocardial oxygen demand (cardiac work).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

29.notes_5e: 5E adds the 208 − 0.7 × age HRmax formula alongside 220 − age and keeps the acute-response framing (Fick, rate-pressure product, MAP); core cardiovascular anatomy/physiology is stable.

30.title: Respiratory system & gas exchange
30.page_refs: 5E Ch 1 — Respiratory System; Exchange of Air; Exchange of Respiratory Gases; Respiratory & Gas Responses (extends 5E for Bohr/O₂-Hb curve & CO₂ transport split)
30.key_concept: Air travels ~23 airway generations (nose→trachea→bronchi→bronchioles) to the alveoli, where O₂ and CO₂ diffuse across the alveolar-capillary membrane down partial-pressure gradients. Minute ventilation = breathing frequency × tidal volume; at rest TV drives it, but at high intensity frequency dominates. Gas exchange is pressure-driven: alveolar PO₂ sits ~60 mmHg above pulmonary-capillary blood at rest. At the tissue, working muscle drops PO₂ and raises PCO₂, steepening gradients so O₂ unloads and CO₂ loads. In healthy non-elites the lungs rarely cap VO₂max — central (cardiac) and peripheral factors do.
key_facts: ~23 airway generations before air reaches alveoli (gas-exchange site)
Minute ventilation = freq × tidal volume; rest ~6 L/min → 90-150 L/min max (15-25× rest)
Breathing freq 12-15 → 35-45 br/min; TV 0.4-1 L → 3+ L with hard exercise
Ventilatory equivalent (V̇E/V̇O₂) ~20-25 L/L rest→moderate; ↑ to 35-40 at high intensity, paralleling the lactate rise
Low-mod intensity: ↑ventilation via ↑TV; high intensity: frequency takes over (TV plateaus)
↑TV = more efficient ventilation than ↑freq (dead space rises less than TV)
Anatomical dead space ~150 mL; not usable for gas exchange, ↑ with age & TV
Rest PO₂: alveoli ~60 mmHg > pulmonary capillary; tissue PO₂ 100→40 mmHg (rest), ~3 mmHg (hard exercise); tissue PCO₂ ~46 (rest) → ~90 mmHg (hard)
Bohr effect: ↑CO₂/↑temp/↓pH shift O₂-Hb curve RIGHT → more O₂ unloaded at active muscle
CO₂ transport: ~70% as bicarbonate, only ~5% dissolved in plasma, the remainder carried on hemoglobin (a limited amount); carbonic anhydrase in RBCs speeds HCO₃⁻ formation
Ventilation cost: 3-5% of total energy at rest → 8-15% in heavy exercise
VO₂max rarely pulmonary-limited in healthy non-elites — central + peripheral factors dominate
30.training_link: Your ventilatory pattern inflects at LT1=147 (indoor) — that's VT1, still TV-driven; above LT2=158 (VT2) ventilation climbs out of proportion to pace and breathing frequency becomes the limiter. In Hyrox, that RIGHT-shifting O₂-Hb curve from hot, acidic working muscle is helping you unload O₂ during the run-to-station transitions — so pace the runs just under VT2 and save the frequency spike for the sled and wall-ball blocks.

glossary.def: ↑CO₂, ↑temp, or ↓pH shift the O₂-Hb curve RIGHT, lowering Hb's O₂ affinity so more O₂ unloads at working muscle.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 32 Q 25 30 35 22 Q 45 14 70 10" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 16 32 Q 30 30 42 24 Q 54 18 75 14" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: First (~LT1) and second (~LT2) ventilatory thresholds where the breathing pattern inflects. Above VT2 ventilation rises disproportionately, tracking the lactate climb.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 8 32 L 25 28 L 40 20 L 55 10 L 70 5" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="25" y1="6" x2="25" y2="32" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/><line x1="55" y1="6" x2="55" y2="32" stroke="#ff7a7a" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Ratio of minute ventilation to O₂ uptake (V̇E/V̇O₂). ~20-25 L air per L O₂ at rest/moderate; climbs to 35-40 at high intensity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Air moved per breath; ~0.4-1 L at rest, up to 3+ L in hard exercise. Deeper breathing ventilates more efficiently than faster breathing.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: ~150 mL of air in conducting passages (nose, trachea, bronchi) that never reaches alveoli, so no gas exchange occurs there.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The difference in a gas's pressure across the membrane; net diffusion runs high→low, driving O₂ into blood/tissue and CO₂ out.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: % of Hb O₂-binding sites occupied (~98.5% at sea level). The S-shaped curve means a small tissue PO₂ drop unloads large amounts of O₂.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="12" fill="#ff5e5e"/><circle cx="32" cy="14" r="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="48" cy="14" r="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="32" cy="26" r="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="48" cy="26" r="3" fill="#5ec8ff"/></svg>

30.notes_5e: 5E keeps the gas-exchange physiology stable (partial-pressure gradients, dead space, ventilatory equivalent) but the Bohr effect, O₂-Hb curve %saturation, and CO₂ transport split are not detailed in Ch 1 — they extend the chapter from standard respiratory physiology.

31.title: Biomechanics I — Force, torque, levers
31.page_refs: 5E Ch 2 — Levers of the Musculoskeletal System; Mechanical Advantage; Variations in Tendon Insertion; Applications to Resistance Training
31.key_concept: Torque (moment) = force × moment arm, the perpendicular distance from the force's line of action to the joint axis. Skeletal muscles act through bony levers classed by where muscle force, resistive force, and fulcrum sit. Mechanical advantage (MA) = muscle moment arm ÷ resistive moment arm: >1 lets a small muscle force beat a big load; <1 forces the muscle to overpower the load. Most human joints run at MA <1 (3rd-class), so internal muscle/tendon forces vastly exceed external loads — the root of tissue injury. Moment arms shift continuously through ROM, so torque demand peaks where leverage is worst.
key_facts: Torque = F × moment arm (⊥ distance, line of force → axis); SI unit N·m
1st-class: muscle & load on OPPOSITE sides of fulcrum (triceps/elbow ext) — e.g. Fig 2.1 MA = 5/40 = 0.125
2nd-class: same side, muscle moment arm LONGER than load's → MA >1 (calf raise): muscle force < load
3rd-class: same side, muscle moment arm SHORTER → MA <1 (biceps curl): muscle force > load
Most joints run MA <1 → internal forces ≫ external loads → high muscle/tendon injury risk
Understanding MA matters more than naming lever class (fulcrum choice is somewhat arbitrary)
Moment arm changes through ROM; patella holds quad tendon away from axis → keeps quad MA high
Tendon inserted farther from joint → ↑torque/force but ↓speed (less rotation per unit shortening) — a trade-off
Free-weight resistive moment arm = horizontal distance weight→joint; biceps-curl torque peaks at forearm horizontal, zero when weight is directly above/below axis
31.training_link: Your squat sticking point just out of the hole is where knee/hip moment arms peak and leverage is worst — pause squats and tempo work overload exactly that angle. In Hyrox, bar and sled path dictate resistive torque: keep the load's horizontal distance to your hips short (chest up, bar close) on wall balls, cleans, and farmer carries to cut spinal and hip torque and save your back over a long race.

video.title: How To Squat Correctly (FIX MISTAKES!) — Squat University

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Optimal Protocols to Build Strength & Grow Muscles

glossary.def: Rotational effect of a force about a joint axis: F × moment arm. Long limbs = bigger moment arms = more torque needed for the same load.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="20" x2="70" y2="20" stroke="#7fc4ff" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="15" cy="20" r="4" fill="#ffcc4a"/><rect x="58" y="12" width="14" height="16" fill="none" stroke="#7fc4ff" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="6" stroke="#a8e1ff" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Perpendicular distance from a force's line of action to the axis of rotation. It varies through the ROM and sets how much torque a given force produces.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Muscle moment arm ÷ resistive moment arm. >1 = the muscle force can be smaller than the load; <1 = the muscle must exceed the load to balance torque.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Muscle and load on the same side of the fulcrum, muscle moment arm shorter. MA <1 — most human joints. Force disadvantage, speed/ROM advantage.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="20" x2="70" y2="20" stroke="#7fc4ff" stroke-width="3"/><circle cx="15" cy="20" r="3.5" fill="#ffcc4a"/><polygon points="40,20 36,10 44,10" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="60" y="14" width="10" height="12" fill="#ff5e5e"/></svg>

glossary.def: Muscle force and resistive force act on opposite sides of the fulcrum (e.g. triceps in elbow extension).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Muscle and load on the same side, muscle moment arm longer than the load's, giving MA >1 (e.g. standing calf raise).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: ROM region where torque demand outstrips torque capacity, marked by a drop in bar acceleration — the worst-leverage part of a lift.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

31.notes_5e: 5E stresses that mastering mechanical advantage matters more than memorizing lever classes, and adds titin/force-velocity and work-power detail absent from older editions.

32.title: Age and sex differences in training response
32.page_refs: 5E Ch 7 — Youth/Older Adults; Ch 8 — Sex-Related Differences
32.key_concept: Trainability persists across the lifespan, but the mechanism shifts. Preadolescents gain strength mainly via NEURAL adaptation (↑motor-unit activation/recruitment/firing), not hypertrophy — low androgens cap muscle growth; resistance training is safe with supervision and does NOT harm growth plates. Post-puberty, boys' testosterone (~600 vs ~20-60 ng/100mL) drives hypertrophy and diverges strength from girls. Older adults face sarcopenia/dynapenia (power falls faster than strength) yet respond strongly to progressive RT. Females show similar RELATIVE strength/hypertrophy gains; absolute gaps trace to lean-mass distribution and hormones, not trainability.
key_facts: Preadolescent strength gains = mostly NEURAL; hypertrophy limited by low circulating androgens
RT is SAFE with supervision; epiphyseal-plate fractures only in unsupervised heavy overhead lifts — none in guideline-adhering studies
PHV (peak height velocity): ~11.9 yr girls, ~13.8 yr boys; injury risk ↑ around PHV
Testosterone: preadolescent ~20-60 ng/100mL both sexes → adolescent males ~600; females stay unchanged
Untrained gains ~30-40% over 8-20 wk; adolescents attain ~50% larger absolute strength gains than children
Sarcopenia = age-related muscle loss (starts ~30s); dynapenia = strength loss; power declines FASTER than strength
Osteopenia = BMD −1 to −2.5 SD; osteoporosis = below −2.5 SD of young-adult mean
Female strength: relative to fat-free mass, sex differences largely DISAPPEAR; lower body closer to males than upper body
Current 5E evidence does NOT support modifying RT prescription by menstrual-cycle phase
32.training_link: Not your demographic directly, but the absolute-vs-relative-strength lens is exactly how you should read crew data — normalize Hyrox strength loads to bodyweight/fat-free mass before comparing your female athletes to yourself, or you'll misjudge trainability. For older or youth clients, program power explicitly (it decays first) and treat RT as a lifelong dose: gains detrain fast in both youth and seniors, so keep a minimum maintenance stimulus year-round.

32.notes_5e: 5E splits the former single age-and-sex chapter into two standalone chapters (Ch 7 age, Ch 8 sex), reframes strength differences around absolute-vs-relative (fat-free-mass) comparisons, adds dynapenia and detailed female injury/health sections, moves RED-S to Ch 11, and concludes current evidence does NOT support menstrual-cycle-phase-based RT prescription.

glossary.def: Age of maximum growth rate during the pubertal spurt (~11.9 yr girls, ~13.8 yr boys). A key maturity marker; injury risk rises around it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Progressive, generalized loss of skeletal muscle mass and function with aging, linked to falls, fractures, disability, and mortality.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Age-related loss of muscular strength, distinct from the loss of muscle mass (sarcopenia).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Low bone mineral density below −2.5 SD of the young-adult mean, with heightened fracture risk; osteopenia is the −1 to −2.5 SD range.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Progress toward mature state (skeletal, sexual, or somatic age); more accurate than chronological age for grouping/testing youth.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Length of time an athlete has consistently followed a formal, supervised resistance program — separate from chronological/biological age.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Primary estrogen in menstruating females; protects skeletal muscle, promotes protein synthesis over degradation, and enhances RT sensitivity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Absolute = total force (males higher via larger fibers); relative = normalized to body weight or fat-free mass, where sex differences shrink or vanish.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

33.title: Sport psychology — arousal, anxiety, motivation
33.page_refs: 5E Ch 9 — Energy Management (Arousal/Anxiety/Stress); Theoretical Tenets; Motivation; Attention & Focus; Psychological Techniques (Imagery, Self-Efficacy, Self-Talk, Goal Setting)
33.key_concept: Arousal = physiological + psychological activation on a sleep→excitement continuum; it indexes the intensity of motivation, not its valence. Anxiety is negatively-perceived arousal with cognitive (worry) and somatic (bodily) parts; trait anxiety (a stable disposition to feel threatened) primes state anxiety (moment-to-moment). Drive theory says arousal↑→performance↑ linearly; inverted-U refines this to peak at moderate arousal, modified by skill, task complexity and personality. IZOF, catastrophe and reversal theories individualize it further. Motivation, selective attention, imagery, self-efficacy, self-talk and process-focused goal setting are the trainable levers to reach the ideal performance state.
key_facts: Arousal = activation continuum (deep sleep→intense excitement); indexed by HR, BP, EEG, EMG, catecholamines — neutral, not inherently good/bad
Anxiety = negatively-perceived arousal → cognitive (worry) + somatic (tense muscles, tachycardia, GI) components
Trait anxiety = stable disposition to perceive threat; primes STATE anxiety = transient apprehension
Distress → arousal + anxiety; eustress → arousal + positive energy (no anxiety)
Drive theory (Hull): linear arousal→performance. Inverted-U (Yerkes-Dodson): peak at MODERATE arousal
Optimal arousal ↑ with skill; ↓ for novices/complex-decision tasks; high arousal helps simple/well-learned skills
IZOF (Hanin): optimal is a BANDWIDTH, not a fixed midpoint; positive AND negative emotions can help or hurt per-athlete
Catastrophe (Hardy): somatic arousal = inverted-U, but with HIGH cognitive anxiety → sudden crash, not gradual decline; calming doesn't fully restore
Reversal (Kerr): interpretation of arousal (excitement vs dread), not just amount, drives its effect
MAS (approach success, seek ~50% odds) vs MAF (avoid shame, pick very easy/very hard tasks)
Fitts & Posner stages: cognitive → associative → autonomous (automaticity); process goals > outcome goals for skill & anxiety
33.training_link: Your race-week arousal target is personal — find your Hyrox IZOF, not a generic peak. If you run flat, pump arousal with music/self-talk before the start; if you spiral, use diaphragmatic breathing to drop somatic arousal and dodge the catastrophe crash mid-race. Anchor to process goals (split pace, transition routine, wall-ball rhythm) instead of a finish time, and run instructional self-talk on the compromised stations while trusting automaticity on the run.

video.title: Yerkes-Dodson Law — Inverted U Theory | Performance vs. Stress

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: How to Assess & Improve All Aspects of Fitness

glossary.def: Yerkes-Dodson: performance rises with arousal to an optimal midpoint, then falls as arousal climbs further. Optimal point shifts with skill, task complexity and personality.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 32 Q 25 30 40 8 Q 55 30 70 32" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="40" cy="9" r="3" fill="#67e8b0"/></svg>

glossary.def: Individual Zones of Optimal Functioning (Hanin). Each athlete performs best within a personal arousal bandwidth, not a universal midpoint; specific emotions help one athlete yet hinder another.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="20" y="12" width="40" height="16" fill="rgba(127, 196, 142, 0.3)" stroke="#7fc48e" stroke-width="1.5" rx="3"/><line x1="40" y1="6" x2="40" y2="34" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: State = transient, situational apprehension with elevated autonomic outflow. Trait = a stable personality disposition to read situations as threatening, which primes state anxiety.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Cognitive = the mental worry/negative-thought component. Somatic = the perceived physical arousal (tense muscles, racing heart, upset gut).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Hardy: when somatic arousal rises in the presence of high cognitive anxiety, performance drops sharply rather than gradually, and lowering arousal alone doesn't restore it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Bandura: task-specific belief you can execute a given action in a given situation. Sourced from past accomplishments, modeling, verbal persuasion, imagery, and physiological/emotional states; better predictor of performance than arousal or anxiety.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Process goals target controllable execution (technique, cadence); outcome goals target results (winning, medals) that depend on others. Process focus best builds skill and curbs anxiety.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Suppressing task-irrelevant cues to process task-relevant ones — what athletes call 'focus'. Nideffer maps focus on two axes: direction (internal-external) × width (broad-narrow).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

33.notes_5e: 5E adds a modern 'Yips, Choking, Flow, Clutch' section (focal dystonia; Biles' twisties) and a mental-health-in-S&C section with signs/symptoms (Table 9.1) — largely stable arousal/anxiety theory otherwise.

34.title: Performance-enhancing substances & methods
34.page_refs: 5E Ch 12 — Hormones & drugs that mimic them; prohibited methods; adverse effects
34.key_concept: Ergogenic drugs mimic or manipulate endogenous hormones. Anabolic-androgenic steroids (synthetic testosterone) ↑ MPS via androgen-receptor binding → ↑ lean mass/strength, dose-dependent (Forbes: logarithmic dose→LBM); athletes stack, cycle, and pyramid at 5-29× replacement doses. SARMs seek tissue-selective anabolism with fewer androgenic effects; peptides/prohormones weakly mimic. GH → acromegaly; EPO/blood doping ↑ RBC mass → ↑ VO₂max but ↑ viscosity/clotting. WADA (via USADA etc.) sets the annual prohibited list; NCAA runs its own. Coaches must know mechanisms, side effects, and legal/ethical context.
key_facts: AAS = synthetic testosterone derivatives; bind androgen receptor → ↑ protein synthesis → ↑ muscle size/strength; androgenic effects inseparable
Oral 17α-alkylated steroids resist hepatic breakdown → hepatotoxic (cholestasis, peliosis hepatis, liver tumors); injectables bypass first-pass, more potent
Dosing: stacking (multiple drugs), cycling (on/off wks), pyramiding (step-up then taper); ~3 agents, 5-10 wk cycles, 5-29× replacement dose

Forbes dose-response: total AAS dose has a logarithmic relationship to ↑ lean body mass (low dose = small effect, LBM keeps climbing with dose); separately, experienced lifters who use AAS gain 2-3× the strength of similarly trained non-users
SARMs = tissue-selective AR agonists (muscle/bone), fewer prostate/liver effects intended; all investigational, WADA-banned at all times; still ↓ HDL, ↓ testosterone, ↑ ALT
GH: ↑ bone/muscle growth via IGF-I; abuse → acromegaly, insulin resistance/diabetes, CV dysfunction, organ enlargement; injectable only, urine-undetectable (blood test since 2004)
EPO/blood doping ↑ RBC mass → Hb ↑5-17%, hct ↑8-19%, aerobic power ↑7-10%; risk = ↑ viscosity → clotting, ↑ systolic BP, stroke, cerebral/pulmonary embolism, cyclist deaths
β2-agonists (clenbuterol) = 'partitioning agents' ↑ lean mass, ↓ fat; β-blockers steady aim (archery/shooting) but ergolytic for endurance (↓ HRmax, VO₂)
Prohormones (androstenedione, DHEA) weak (1/5-1/10 T activity); no proven anabolic effect, ↑ estradiol, ↓ HDL; controlled by 2004 Act & DASCA 2014
Insulin abuse (bodybuilders) → severe hypoglycemia → coma/death; HCG mimics LH → restart endogenous T after a cycle
34.training_link: You'll never program these, but as a coach you're expected to spot the pattern and steer athletes back to periodized training + real nutrition. Know which categories are legal ergogenics (caffeine, creatine, β-alanine, HMB, bicarb) versus banned hormones/methods, because contaminated supplements can trip a WADA test — for a Hyrox athlete chasing PBs, a batch-tested (Informed-Sport) product is the safe call. Recognize the tells too: rapid mass + acne + mood swings (AAS), or unexplained aerobic jumps with high hct (EPO).

34.notes_5e: 5E adds substantial SARM and peptide/GH-secretagogue coverage (LGD-4033, MK-677, DASCA 2014) reflecting the modern doping frontier, alongside stable AAS/EPO/GH material.

glossary.def: Synthetic derivative of testosterone that promotes muscle/protein anabolism while carrying inseparable androgenic (masculinizing) effects.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Chemical modification that lets an oral steroid survive first-pass liver metabolism; the tradeoff is marked hepatotoxicity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Using several agents at once (stacking) in on/off blocks (cycling), with doses ramped up then tapered (pyramiding) to limit rebound side effects.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Selective androgen receptor modulator — a drug designed to agonize AR mainly in muscle and bone with fewer effects on prostate/liver; all currently investigational and WADA-banned.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Artificially raising red-cell mass via autologous/homologous transfusion or EPO to boost O₂ delivery and VO₂max.
Kidney hormone that stimulates erythropoiesis; recombinant EPO raises hematocrit and aerobic power but also blood viscosity and clot risk.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Post-pubertal GH excess causing bone widening, organ enlargement, arthritis, and metabolic abnormalities — a hallmark risk of GH abuse.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A β2-agonist (e.g., clenbuterol) that shifts body composition toward lean mass and away from stored fat.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: World Anti-Doping Agency, which sets the annually updated international banned-substance list enforced via national bodies (USADA, UKAD).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

35.title: Test selection principles — validity, reliability, specificity
35.page_refs: 5E Ch 13 — Reasons for Testing; Testing Terminology; Evaluation of Test Quality (Validity/Reliability); Test Selection; Sequence of Tests
35.key_concept: A test earns value only when it is BOTH valid (measures the intended attribute) and reliable (repeatable). Validity is umbrella'd by construct validity, supported by face, content, and criterion (concurrent + predictive) evidence. Reliability = consistency; sources of error are intrasubject, interrater (objectivity), intrarater, and the test itself. A test must be reliable to be valid, but reliability alone doesn't grant validity. Selection weighs sport specificity (energy system + biomechanics), athlete experience, training status, age, sex, and environment. Sequence tests least→most fatiguing so one never confounds the next.
key_facts: Validity = measures what it claims; reliability = consistency on repeat. Reliable ≠ valid (60m sprint reliable but invalid for aerobic fitness)
Construct validity = overall; face (looks right to non-experts), content (experts confirm all components covered), criterion (concurrent + predictive) provide evidence for it
Convergent validity = high correlation w/ gold standard; discriminant validity = LOW correlation w/ a different construct
Objectivity = interrater reliability (agreement between different scorers)
Reliability error sources: intrasubject, interrater, intrarater, test-itself; quantified via intraclass correlation (ICC) + typical error (TE)
Valid test must emulate sport's energy system (phosphagen/glycolytic/oxidative) AND key movement patterns
Rest between trials: 2 min sub-max, 3 min near-max; ≥5 min between different tests in a battery
Recovery by system: phosphagen 3-5 min; glycolytic ≥1 hr — drives ordering
Test order: 1) non-fatiguing 2) agility 3) max power/strength 4) sprints 5) muscular endurance 6) anaerobic capacity 7) aerobic (last, ideally separate day)
Aerobic perf impaired >~80°F (27°C) if humidity >50%; VO2max ↓~5% per 3,000 ft above ~1,900 ft
35.training_link: Treat your own block testing like a CSCS test battery: standardize the venue (indoor, same time of day, same rower/skierg) to protect reliability, and pick metrics that actually map to Hyrox — a runnable LT/decoupling test has content validity your 1RM back squat does not. Sequence your test days least→most fatiguing (jumps and 1RM before any sustained runs), and never wedge a threshold run before a strength test — the fatigue confounds both, exactly the error the chapter warns against.

video.title: Andy Galpin — How to Assess & Improve All Aspects of Your Fitness

audio.title: Iron Culture — Brad Schoenfeld: Hypertrophy Research Methods

35.notes_5e: 5E reorganizes validity around construct validity as the umbrella (adding convergent/discriminant subtypes) and formalizes a scientific-process framework (Fig 13.1) for choosing tests; core sequencing/specificity content is stable.

glossary.def: Degree to which a test measures what it is intended to measure. The single most important test property; nested types provide evidence for overall construct validity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Consistency or repeatability of a test across administrations. A prerequisite for validity — variable results carry little meaning.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Degree to which different scorers produce the same result for the same performance. Improved by clear scoring systems and trained, experienced testers.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overall extent to which a test measures the theoretical construct it targets. Face, content, and criterion validity are subordinate evidence for it.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Convergent = high correlation with the gold-standard measure of the same construct; discriminant = low correlation with a test of a different construct.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Association of test scores with another measure of the same ability — concurrent (vs an accepted current test) or predictive (vs future performance).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Reliability statistic capturing combined equipment error and biological variation between repeat measures.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: How closely a test mirrors the sport's metabolic energy demands and biomechanical movement patterns; the more similar, the more valid.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

36.title: Test administration — strength & power
36.page_refs: 5E Ch 14 — Maximum Muscular Strength, Muscular Power, Anaerobic Capacity; Load-Velocity Profile
36.key_concept: Low-speed strength = 1RM of slow grinds (bench, back squat, bench pull); reach true 1RM in 3-5 attempts after a light warm-up and 2+ heavier sets, or fatigue skews it. High-speed power = 1RM explosive lifts (power clean), IMTP peak force/RFD, and jumps (CMJ, squat jump, drop/rebound jump → RSI = jump ht ÷ contact time). Margaria-Kalamen stair sprint gives P = (w×h)/t. Anaerobic capacity (30-90 s) uses the 300-yd shuttle or Wingate. Express strength relative to body mass via ratio or allometric scaling.
key_facts: 1RM: light warm-up 5-10 reps → 2+ heavier sets of 2-5 → true 1RM within 3-5 attempts
Bench = 1 spotter (head end); back squat = 2 spotters (one each end) + rack safety bars
5-lb (2.3 kg) plate gradations to dial in the 1RM
IMTP: mid-thigh 2nd-pull position, knee ~120-145°, hip ~125-150°; ≥3 max trials of 3-5 s (peak force) or ~1 s (RFD); redo if trials differ >15%
Vertical jump: hands-on-hips CMJ (arm swing = Abalakov); best of 3; squat jump = no countermovement, held 2-3 s at ~110° knee
RSI = jump height ÷ ground contact time; short contact = fast SSC / high leg stiffness
Eccentric utilization ratio = CMJ height ÷ squat-jump height
Margaria-Kalamen: ≥9 steps (~7 in / 18 cm), 6 m run-up, 3 steps at a time, time from step 3→9; P(W)=(w×h)/t
300-yd shuttle: 6 round trips of 25 yd, 2 trials, 5-min rest, average; Wingate = 30 s, resistance ~7.5% BM
Load-velocity profile should NOT be used to estimate 1RM — it overestimates; test 1RM directly
36.training_link: For your Hyrox crew, split the assessment: a directly tested back-squat and power-clean 1RM for max strength, then CMJ + drop-jump RSI to track the reactive, stiff-ankle quality that carries wall balls and sled work. Standardize the warm-up and venue every retest — that consistency is what makes a change trustworthy rather than noise. Skip using bar velocity to guess your 1RM; it overestimates, so test the top single directly.

video.title: Squat University — How to Squat Correctly (1RM testing context)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Assessment of Fitness

36.notes_5e: 5E broadens power/strength testing well beyond older editions — force-plate CMJ/squat-jump/drop-jump with RSI and eccentric utilization ratio, the IMTP, and velocity-based testing, while explicitly warning the load-velocity profile should not be used to estimate 1RM.

glossary.def: Heaviest load lifted once with correct technique; the standard low-speed maximal strength score.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Isometric mid-thigh pull — a multijoint max isometric test on a force plate yielding peak force and rate of force development from the second-pull position.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Jump height divided by ground contact time in a drop/rebound jump; indexes fast stretch-shortening-cycle ability.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Vertical jump with a rapid dip before takeoff; arms-on-hips isolates legs, arm-swing version = Abalakov.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Max energy output from phosphagen + glycolytic systems over ~30-90 s efforts (300-yd shuttle, Wingate).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Individual line relating bar velocity to load across ~20-90% 1RM; useful for intensity monitoring but unreliable for predicting 1RM.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Dividing strength by body mass raised to an exponent to remove body-size bias when comparing athletes, vs simple ratio scaling.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: CMJ height ÷ squat-jump height; a marker of how much the athlete gains from the countermovement/SSC.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

37.title: Test administration — endurance & body comp
37.page_refs: 5E Ch 14 — Aerobic Capacity, Anaerobic Capacity, Change of Direction/Agility, Speed, Body Composition
37.key_concept: Aerobic capacity (mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹) is rarely measured directly, so field tests estimate it: 1.5-mi run, 12-min run, Yo-Yo IR1/IR2, MAS (VO₂max ≈ 3.5 × MAS km/h). Anaerobic capacity → 300-yd shuttle (6×50 yd, avg of 2 trials, 5-min rest). Agility = change-of-direction tests (T-test, 5-10-5/pro-agility, 5-0-5, Illinois, hexagon). Speed = straight-line sprints; electronic gates beat stopwatch (hand-timing reads ~0.24 s fast). Body comp uses the 2-component model: skinfolds (r=0.99, best field tool), BIA, DXA (gold standard). Report %BF as a range (SEE ≥ ±5%).
key_facts: VO₂max ≈ 3.5 × MAS (speed in km/h); MAS = last speed held ≥2 min
Yo-Yo IR: 2×20 m shuttles, 10 s recovery; IR1 starts 10 km/h, IR2 starts 13 km/h; use IR1
300-yd shuttle: 6 round trips of 50 yd (2 lines 25 yd apart), avg 2 trials, 5-min rest
Agility tests: T-test, 5-10-5 (pro agility/20-yd shuttle), 5-0-5, Illinois, hexagon (18 hops), L-drill
Hand stopwatch reads ~0.24 s FASTER than electronic gates → prefer timing gates + split times
Skinfold = most valid/reliable field method (r=0.99); DXA = gold standard; measure dry skin, pre-exercise, right side
Skinfold reads to nearest 0.5 mm, prongs 1-2 cm from pinch; two trials avg if within 10%
%BF: pick population-specific Db equation → convert to %BF; report a RANGE, SEE ≥ ±5%
BIA sensitive to hydration → test AM, fasted, post-void, pre-exercise; standardize conditions
37.training_link: For your Hyrox athletes, the Yo-Yo IR1 and MAS tests are far more specific than a flat 12-min run — MAS gives you a km/h anchor to set interval and compromised-run paces directly. Track %BF as a range, not a point, and always retest skinfolds on the same side, dry, and pre-session so a 0.5 mm drift isn't mistaken for real change. Use timing gates for the 5-10-5 and sprints; hand-timing's ~0.24 s bias will swamp true week-to-week progress in trained legs.

video.title: Seiler — Polarized Training 101 (testing context)

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Energy Systems & Testing

37.notes_5e: 5E broadens field aerobic testing (adds MAS with the VO₂max ≈ 3.5×MAS rule, Yo-Yo IR1/IR2, 30-15 IFT) and modern change-of-direction batteries (5-0-5, 5-10-5), while keeping skinfold/BIA/DXA body-comp methods stable.

glossary.def: Max rate of ATP production via oxidation of fuels, expressed as mL O₂·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹; usually estimated by run/field tests rather than measured directly.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Maximal aerobic speed — lowest running speed that elicits VO₂max; VO₂max ≈ 3.5 × MAS (km/h).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intermittent 2×20 m shuttles at rising speed with 10 s recovery each rep; IR1/IR2 estimate aerobic fitness for team sports.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="14" y="8" width="34" height="26" rx="2" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M20 16h22M20 22h22M20 28h14" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M50 24l5 5 9-11" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Rapid whole-body change of direction or speed in response to a sport-specific stimulus (perception + change-of-direction).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Body composition split into fat mass and lean mass; the basis for skinfold and BIA %BF estimates.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Prediction error band of an estimate; skinfold %BF has a minimum SEE of ±5%, so results are reported as a range.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Anaerobic-capacity test: six 50-yd round trips between lines 25 yd apart; average of two trials after 5-min rest.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Dual X-ray absorptiometry; multi-compartment scan often labeled the gold standard for body composition.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

38.title: Speed & agility training
38.page_refs: 5E Ch 20 — Speed & Agility Mechanics; Sprint Technique; COD & Agility; Program Design; Monitoring
38.key_concept: 5E splits three qualities: speed (reach high velocity), change of direction (COD, pre-planned deceleration→reaccel), and agility (COD in response to a stimulus, adding perceptual-cognitive load). All are limited by net impulse — force × ground-contact time — not maximal strength, since actions occur in 0-200 ms while max force needs 300+ ms; hence RFD and impulse dominate. Sprint speed = step length × step frequency, both driven by high vertical force in brief contacts via the SSC. COD adds eccentric braking impulse; agility adds visual scanning, anticipation, and decision-making. Program via periodized needs-analysis, not random small-sided games.
key_facts: Speed vs COD vs agility: agility = COD + perception-action coupling (react to stimulus)
Max voluntary force needs ~300ms+; sport actions last 0-200ms → RFD & impulse > max strength
Sprint speed = step length × step frequency; top-speed limiter = vertical GRF in short contact
Impulse = area under force-time curve; net impulse = Δmomentum (mass constant)
GCT: max-velocity 0.09-0.11s, acceleration 0.17-0.2s, COD ~0.15-0.6s (↑ with angle)
Cut angle ≥75° → GCT >250ms, high braking → prioritize eccentric strength
Accel: forward lean, COM ahead of foot, long piston contacts; Max-V: upright, cyclic, brief contacts, foot under COM
Elite sprinters produce most vertical force in first half of stance (deviate from spring-mass model)
COD deficit = COD-test time − equal-length linear sprint time; isolates true COD ability
Agility develops best periodized: COD (closed) → +generic stimulus → sport-specific/small-sided
38.training_link: For Hyrox your running is sub-maximal aerobic pacing, so true max-velocity sprint work barely transfers — skip it unless chasing a standalone 5K PR. But the eccentric braking and COD mechanics DO matter: every sled-pull turnaround, wall-ball reset, and burpee-broad-jump landing is a deceleration→reacceleration you can train with drop landings and short start-stop drills. As a coach, use the RFD/impulse framing to justify plyos and low-volume eccentric work for your athletes without cannibalizing their engine sessions.

video.title: Galpin — Assess & Improve All Aspects of Fitness (speed work)

audio.title: Iron Culture 236 — Galpin: Fiber Types & Training Specificity

38.notes_5e: 5E rewrites this chapter to sharply separate speed/COD/agility, centers everything on net impulse & RFD over max strength, and adds modern monitoring (COD deficit, exit velocity, decision-making time).

glossary.def: Rapid whole-body change of direction, velocity, or mode in response to a stimulus. Combines COD ability with perceptual-cognitive processing.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Pre-planned, closed-loop ability to decelerate, deflect the center of mass, and reaccelerate. No reactive/stimulus component.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Force × time it is applied — area under the force-time curve. Net impulse equals the change in momentum, so it drives acceleration and braking.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: How fast force is produced (ΔF/Δt). More decisive than max strength when contact times are 0-200 ms.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Models sprint stance as a compressing/extending leg-spring storing and returning elastic energy. Describes non-elite well; elite deviate by front-loading vertical force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: COD-test time minus a straight-line sprint time of equal length. Removes linear-speed bias to isolate genuine change-of-direction ability.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Linking a perceived stimulus (defender, ball, light) to the motor response. The perceptual-cognitive layer that separates agility from pure COD.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Bending or curving around obstacles while keeping velocity, as opposed to a sharp deceleration-and-cut. Longer tests (Illinois) bias toward it and metabolic capacity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

39.title: Rehabilitation & reconditioning principles
39.page_refs: 5E Ch 23 — Tissue Healing, Goals of Rehab & Reconditioning, Program Design, Reinjury Risk
39.key_concept: Injury classes: macrotrauma (sudden overload) vs microtrauma (overuse). All tissues heal in 3 overlapping phases: inflammation (~72h, ↑vascularity, phagocytosis, growth-factor release), fibroblastic repair (~72h to ~8 wk, weak randomly-laid type-III collagen), maturation-remodeling (weeks to years, type-III→type-I collagen aligning to stress lines). Load must match phase — controlled therapeutic stress builds collagen, too much re-injures. The CSCS is the return-to-competition specialist within the allied-health team (works WITH, not instead of, MD/ATC/PT), maintaining fitness of uninjured regions and progressing sport-specific loading toward clearance.
key_facts: Macrotrauma = sudden overload (fracture, sprain, strain, contusion, dislocation/subluxation); microtrauma = overuse (stress fracture, tendinopathy)
Sprain = ligament (1st/2nd/3rd degree); strain = muscle/tendon (1st/2nd/3rd degree)
Inflammation ~72h; repair ~72h→8 wk; remodeling weeks→years — phases OVERLAP, no fixed timeline
Type-III collagen (weak, random) in repair → type-I (strong, aligned to stress) in remodeling
5E shifted PRICE → POLICE: Protection, Optimal Loading, Compression, Ice, Elevation (5E's own ordering)
Cryotherapy: 15 min ice pack/massage; cold-water immersion <59°F (15°C); analgesic + ↓swelling, but past acute phase may ↓IGF/protein synthesis
Loading progression: isometric (angle-specific) → isotonic (concentric/eccentric) → isokinetic → sport-specific/plyometric
Eccentric loading → mechanotransduction → ↑collagen synthesis; eccentrics ↓hamstring-strain risk
CKC (foot fixed, e.g. squat) = functional/multi-joint stability; OKC (foot free, e.g. leg ext) = isolate one joint
RTS: side-to-side deficit <10% (LSI ≥90%) in strength/function; prior injury = top reinjury risk factor
DAPRE: 4 sets off 6RM (10×50%, 6×75%, max×100%, then set-3 reps set the set-4 & next-session load)
39.training_link: Treat these load-vs-phase rules as your everyday progression logic, not just injury care — any new Hyrox stimulus (first sled-heavy block, adding eccentrics, ramping wall-ball volume) is 'healing tissue' that wants controlled overload, not a spike. If you tweak a hamstring on a run, POLICE it early, keep the uninjured half of your engine training (upper body, opposite leg, bike), then rebuild eccentric → concentric → speed before you sprint again. Coach your athletes to an LSI ≥90% and pain-free sport-specific testing before RTS, not just to 'pain gone' — pain resolves well before the tissue does.

video.title: Squat University — How to Deadlift Properly (return-to-lifting form)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Recovery Protocols

39.notes_5e: 5E updates acute care from PRICE to POLICE (early optimal loading) and expands medical-issues coverage (rhabdomyolysis, sickle cell trait, heat-illness continuum, hypothermia); healing-phase framework is stable.

glossary.def: Single sudden overload disrupting tissue integrity — fracture, dislocation, sprain, strain, contusion.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overuse injury from repeated abnormal stress with too little recovery, e.g. stress fracture, tendinopathy.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

glossary.def: Acute-injury protocol — Protection, Optimal Loading, Compression, Ice, Elevation (as ordered in the 5E); replaced PRICE by adding early controlled loading.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overload signal passed from outside to inside the cell that upregulates DNA and drives collagen synthesis — why loading (esp. eccentric) aids repair.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Distal segment fixed (e.g. squat, push-up); multi-joint, higher joint stability, more sport-functional.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Terminal joint free to move (e.g. leg extension, biceps curl); isolates one joint/muscle.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Joint swelling reflexively inhibits the alpha motor neuron, weakening muscle activation after injury.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Daily Adjustable Progressive Resistive Exercise — 4-set protocol off a 6RM where set-3 reps dictate the next load adjustment.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Injured-limb strength/function as a % of the uninjured limb; ≥90% (deficit <10%) is a common RTS benchmark.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

40.title: Facility design & layout
40.page_refs: 5E Ch 25 — Facility Design, Layout & Organization (design features, spacing, flooring, supervision)
40.key_concept: Facility design runs 4 phases: predesign (needs analysis, SWOT feasibility, master plan, hire architect) → design → construction → preoperation. Plan traffic flow so a central supervisor sees the whole floor. Space ≥100 sq ft (9.3 m²) per person; ceiling 12-14 ft; off-ground floors bear ≥100 ft-lb/sq ft. Group equipment by pattern along walls with 5-7 ft main walkways. Lighting 20-30 fc general (up to 50 fc lifting), temp 68-74°F, humidity <60%, air exchanged 8-10×/hr. Rubber flooring for free weights; mirrors 6 in off equipment, 20 in above floor.
key_facts: 4 phases: predesign (needs analysis→SWOT feasibility→master plan→hire architect) → design → construction → preoperation
Space ≥100 sq ft (9.3 m²) per person incl. staff; off-ground floor bears ≥100 ft-lb/sq ft
Ceiling 12-14 ft; lighting 20-30 fc general, up to 50 fc resistance/group areas; 4000-5000 K LED
Main walkways 5-7 ft; racks/barbells/dumbbells ≥3 ft spacing; circuit machines 30 in min (36 in preferred)
Temp 68-78°F (optimal 68-74°F); humidity <60%; air exchange 8-10×/hr min (12-15 optimal); sound <90 dB
Rubber flooring = premier (free weights); inlaid platforms > raised (no trip hazard); turf for sled/plyo/agility
Mirrors ≥6 in from equipment, ≥20 in above floor (weights won't hit glass; 18-in plate → 2-in clearance)
Supervision: central office, elevated, clear line of sight; shorter equipment mid-room, tall machines bolted down
Suspension anchor 7-9 ft high; fall-rated carabiners ≥5000 lb (OSHA), secondary screw-lock
40.training_link: Your Hyrox/hybrid sessions demand mixed zones — a rack for strength, open turf for sled push/burpee broad jumps, and a rower/ski/bike aisle. Apply the 5-7 ft walkway and ≥3 ft rack cushion rules when you set up a garage or crew space so athletes cycling stations don't collide, and keep the ski/rower on a wall run (electrical + 40-60 sq ft each). If you ever build a satellite crew gym, bolt equipment around the perimeter and keep the center open for transitions.

video.title: Galpin — Assess & Improve Fitness (facility considerations)

audio.title: Iron Culture — Hypertrophy & Training Environment

40.notes_5e: 5E revised the numbers older editions used: lighting is 20-30 fc general / up to 50 fc lifting (not 50-100), main walkways are 5-7 ft (not 36 in), and it adds detailed per-exercise space-need formulas (Table 25.2), outdoor/satellite spaces, and suspension-trainer anchoring.

glossary.def: Predesign step where designers and S&C experts define program space, equipment, and activity requirements the facility must serve.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Predesign analysis of strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats to confirm the investment yields a viable, sustainable return.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Overarching plan covering building/construction, facility design, budget, and the post-opening operational plan (short- and long-term goals).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Buffer of clear space around a lift/station so a falling athlete, spotter, or dropped bar injures no one; walkways and cushions must never be compromised.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: One meeting mandated codes: ADA access, HVAC, electrical, lighting, flooring, storage, square footage, safety spacing, overhead clearance, and maintained equipment.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Illuminance unit = 1 lumen/ft²; general gym areas 20-30 fc, dedicated lifting/group areas up to 50 fc.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Lifting platform flush with the floor (vs. raised); safer in high-traffic rooms because it presents no tripping hazard.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

41.title: Facility policies, procedures & legal
41.page_refs: 5E Ch 26 — Legal & Ethical Issues; Preparticipation Screening; Instruction & Supervision; Emergency Planning & Response
41.key_concept: Negligence = failure to act as a reasonable, prudent professional would; 4 elements must ALL be proven (by preponderance of evidence) — duty, breach of duty, proximate cause, damages. Standard of care is set by your education, training, and certification (CSCS/CPR/AED/first aid), so credentials both establish and raise it. Risk management stacks defenses: preparticipation medical clearance, informed consent + assumption-of-risk waivers, direct supervision, EAP, equipment inspection, and indefinite record-keeping. Waivers don't shield you if you're negligent. Supervision was cited in ~80% of athletic-injury court cases — presence + vigilance is the biggest lever.
key_facts: Negligence = duty + breach + proximate cause + damages (ALL four, preponderance of evidence)
Standard of care = reasonable prudent pro w/ same education, training, certification (CSCS, CPR, AED, first aid)
CSCS from accredited body ESTABLISHES a standard of care; NCAA D-I mandates it (Aug 1 2015)
Assumption-of-risk waiver ≠ protection if you're negligent
Supervision cited in ~80% of athletic-injury court cases; constant physical presence required
Supervision ratios: 1:10 junior high, 1:15 high school, 1:20 college; ≥100 ft² floor space/participant
Med clearance BEFORE access; only sports-med staff (AT/physician/PT) clears — outside S&C scope of practice
EAP = written, posted, practiced ≥ quarterly; staff maintain current first aid/CPR/AED
Exercise/conditioning as punishment is prohibited (hazing/abuse, sudden-death risk)
Keep records (injury reports, waivers, maintenance logs) indefinitely — statute of limitations varies
41.training_link: As a coach running Hyrox group sessions, your credentials set the bar you'll be judged against — carry current CPR/AED/first aid and coach to it, not below it. Screen and clear athletes before high-intensity work-to-rest conditioning (sickle-cell trait, asthma, prior heat episodes flag modified ratios), keep signed waivers and incident logs, and never use extra reps or runs as punishment. Post an EAP for your venue and rehearse it — under-supervision drives ~80% of injury suits.

video.title: USADA Coach's Advantage — NSCA Continuing Education
