41.notes_5e: 5E adds explicit negligence case law (exertional rhabdomyolysis, sickle-cell-trait litigation) and codifies the NCAA D-I 2015 certification mandate, framing the CSCS itself as establishing standard of care.

glossary.def: Failing to act as a reasonable, prudent professional would in similar circumstances; requires duty, breach, proximate cause, and damages all proven.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The degree of caution a reasonable professional with your education, training, and certification would exercise in the same situation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A legal obligation to act — S&C pros owe athletes a duty both to respond to injury and to prevent it, by virtue of employment.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The breach must be the natural, continuous, reasonably foreseeable cause of the harm for negligence to hold.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Athlete knowingly accepts inherent activity risks and participates anyway; a signed waiver does not shield a negligent professional.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Explaining an activity's inherent risks and benefits so a participant can voluntarily decide whether to take part.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: The legal/professional limits of one's duties; S&C pros may not diagnose or clear medical conditions — only sports-med staff can.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: A written, venue-specific plan for EMS activation, injury care, evacuation, and communication; posted and rehearsed at least quarterly.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Proactive strategies (screening, supervision, documentation, maintenance, EAP) that reduce injury and thus liability exposure.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

42.title: Phase 1 integration review
42.page_refs: 5E Ch 1-6 — Structure/Function, Biomechanics, Bioenergetics, Endocrine, Anaerobic & Aerobic Adaptations
42.key_concept: Phase 1 builds the exercise-science spine: muscle architecture and motor units generate force, levers and moment arms transmit it, three energy systems fuel it, hormones signal the remodel, and adaptations bank the gains. The single through-line is stress → recovery → adaptation, expressed system by system. Force = fiber cross-section × neural drive × mechanical leverage. Fuel shifts phosphagen → fast glycolysis → oxidative as effort lengthens. Anaerobic gains run neural-first then structural; aerobic gains run central-first (Q, SV) then peripheral (mitochondria, capillaries). Every downstream method rests on these six chapters.
key_facts: Force output = muscle CSA × neural drive (recruitment via Henneman size principle, rate coding) × leverage (moment arms)
Levers: 1st-class (fulcrum between forces), 2nd-class (resistance between), 3rd-class (muscle force between — most limbs, MA<1, favors ROM/speed)
Force-velocity: concentric force ↓ as velocity ↑; eccentric force > isometric > concentric at speed
Energy systems: phosphagen (CP, single-step, highest rate, ~sec) → fast glycolysis (→lactate, higher capacity, ~sec-min) → oxidative (mitochondria, O2, highest capacity, sustained)
CP stores ~4-6× resting ATP; creatine kinase reaction replenishes ATP fastest
Endocrine: testosterone, GH, IGF-I = anabolic; cortisol = catabolic (chronic elevation is detrimental); adaptation tracks the anabolic:catabolic balance, which follows a circadian rhythm
Anaerobic adaptation: neural dominates early (~6-10 wk, Sale); hypertrophy contributes more >10 wk
Aerobic adaptation: central first — ↑ max Q via ↑ SV (max HR ~unchanged); then peripheral — ↑ mitochondria, capillary density, a-vO2 diff
VO2max = max cardiac output × max a-vO2 difference (Fick)
42.training_link: Every RoxLive metric maps to one of these chapters: your HR/pace pairs read central aerobic capacity, DFA-a1 and decoupling read the oxidative-vs-glycolytic crossover, and velocity loss on the sled reads neural fatigue. When you program a Hyrox block, sequence it the way adaptation actually stacks — neural and central changes come first, structural and peripheral gains bank later — so early sessions chase drive and efficiency before you load pure hypertrophy or capacity volume.

video.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: How to Build Strength, Muscle Size & Endurance (synthesis)

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Energy Systems Overview

42.notes_5e: 5E frames adaptation explicitly as an integrated stress→recovery→adaptation model across systems and reinforces neural-first (anaerobic) vs central-first (aerobic) timelines; energy-system and lever fundamentals are stable versus prior editions.

glossary.def: Motor units are recruited low-to-high threshold as force demand rises; small (Type I) first, large (Type II) as load or velocity climbs.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="16" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M22 20h10M22 20l10-7M22 20l10 7" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="13" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="3"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Perpendicular distance from the joint axis to the line of force. Longer moment arm → more torque for the same force.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Ratio of muscle-force moment arm to resistive-force moment arm. <1.0 means the muscle must exceed the load (favoring range/speed over force).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M10 30 C24 30 40 24 66 8" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="30" x2="70" y2="30" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="10" y1="6" x2="10" y2="34" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1"/></svg>

glossary.def: Anaerobic, single-step ATP resynthesis via creatine kinase; highest power/rate, smallest capacity — dominant for ~first seconds of maximal effort.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="7" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".8"/><circle cx="40" cy="14" r="5" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="5" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="58" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M27 20h7M45 16l8 3M45 25l8-3" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Anaerobic breakdown of glucose/glycogen to pyruvate→lactate; faster ATP rate than oxidative, larger capacity than phosphagen.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Arterial minus venous O2 content — how much O2 the tissues extract; widens with aerobic training (peripheral adaptation).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Net signaling from anabolic hormones (T, GH, IGF-I) vs catabolic (cortisol); adaptation favors the anabolic side within recovery windows.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="6" fill="#ffb86b"/><path d="M26 20h14" stroke="#9fb2c8" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="14" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><circle cx="50" cy="26" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="12" height="20" rx="6" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

glossary.def: Early strength gain from ↑ recruitment, rate coding, and synchronization — precedes measurable hypertrophy.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="16" cy="20" r="6" fill="#5ec8ff"/><path d="M22 20h10M22 20l10-7M22 20l10 7" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"/><g fill="#67e8b0"><circle cx="40" cy="13" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3"/><circle cx="40" cy="27" r="3"/></g></svg>

43.title: Polarized training & intensity distribution (Seiler)
43.page_refs: Extends 5E Ch 21 — Training Intensity / Training Zones (three- & five-zone models); polarized model from Seiler 2010, Stöggl & Sperlich 2014
43.key_concept: 5E prescribes aerobic intensity by zones anchored to thresholds: a three-zone VT model (Z1 < LT1/VT1, Z2 between, Z3 > LT2/MLSS) or the Norwegian five-zone lactate scale (Seiler-derived). Intensity is monitored via %VO2max, %HRR/%MHR, RPE, or blood lactate. The modern polarized extension asks how those zones are DISTRIBUTED: elites cluster ~75-85% of SESSIONS in easy Z1 and ~15-20% in hard Z3, with little in the between 'grey' zone. Defined by session count, not minutes. Keeping easy days truly easy preserves the capacity to make hard days genuinely hard.
key_facts: 5E three-zone model: Z1 < LT1/VT1, Z2 = LT1→LT2 (2-4 mmol/L), Z3 > LT2/MLSS
Norwegian 5-zone lactate scale (Seiler): Z1 0.8-1.5 mM (1-6h) up to Z5 6-10 mM (15-30 min)
Extensive-endurance day = 'conversational', ~zone 2 (5-zone) / zone 1 (3-zone), talk-test
Polarization = SESSION-COUNT ratio ~80/20, NOT a minute-share
Grey-zone (Z2) drift: too hard to recover, too easy to drive top-end → junk miles
Monitor intensity via %VO2max, %HRR/%MHR, RPE, or blood lactate; LT1/LT2 are anchors
Easy truly easy → hard truly hard; grey-zone chronicity blunts both ends
Competing distributions: pyramidal (mostly easy, some threshold) vs threshold/sweet-spot vs polarized
Polarization Index = log10(Z1%/Z3%) style summary of a block's distribution
43.training_link: Your Seiler 80/20 is computed on session count, not minutes, and you track a Polarization Index across the block. Your indoor Z2 HR cap of 132 sits below LT1≈147, so easy runs stay genuinely conversational and never drift into the grey zone — that discipline is exactly what protects the quality of your hard 4×8 threshold/VO2 days. Watch that treadmill HR reads lower than road at matched effort, so verify cap by talk-test, not the number alone.

glossary.def: Intensity distribution where ~80% of sessions are easy (below LT1) and ~20% hard (above LT2), with minimal middle. Judged by session count, not total minutes.

glossary.def: The Z2 band between LT1 and LT2 — too hard to allow full recovery, too easy to maximize top-end adaptation. Where junk miles accumulate.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 6 34 C 30 34 40 30 50 18 C 58 9 66 7 74 6' stroke='#ffb86b' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><line x1='6' y1='34' x2='74' y2='34' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

glossary.def: A single number (log10 of Z1%/Z3% weighting) summarizing how polarized a block's intensity distribution is; higher = more polarized.

glossary.def: Lactate/ventilatory threshold 1 (~2 mmol/L, first rise, top of easy Z1) and threshold 2 (~4 mmol/L / MLSS, bottom of hard Z3). The two anchors that bracket the grey zone.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Norwegian Olympic Federation lactate-based scale (Seiler) from Z1 (0.8-1.5 mM, 1-6 h) to Z5 (6-10 mM, 15-30 min); a finer monitoring tool than the three-zone VT model.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: 5E's preferred term for long, low-intensity 'conversational' aerobic work (zone 2 of the five-zone scale); builds mitochondrial density, fat use, and lactate clearance. Prioritize duration, not speed.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

43.notes_5e: 5E formalizes zone-based prescription (three-zone VT model + Seiler's Norwegian five-zone lactate scale) but does not name the 80/20 polarized-distribution concept — that is the modern Seiler extension layered on top of the textbook's threshold anchors.

44.title: Lactate thresholds & zone anchoring (LT1, LT2, MLSS)
44.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — Lactate Threshold & OBLA; Ch 6 — Aerobic Adaptations; Ch 21 — Aerobic Program Design (intensity zones). Modern Z2/polarized layer extends 5E.
44.key_concept: As intensity climbs, blood lactate breaks upward at two inflection points. LT1 (lactate threshold) is the first abrupt rise above baseline — signaling growing glycolytic reliance; it aligns with the ventilatory threshold and marks the top of easy/aerobic work (~50-60% VO2max untrained, 70-80% trained). LT2/OBLA is the second inflection, occurring roughly at 4 mmol/L, and closely tracks MLSS — the highest intensity where lactate appearance equals clearance (heavy-to-severe boundary, synonymous with critical power/speed). Training shifts both thresholds rightward, letting an athlete hold a higher %VO2max without acidosis.
key_facts: LT1 = first lactate break above baseline; ↑ glycolytic reliance; ≈ ventilatory threshold; top of aerobic zone
LT1 onset: ~50-60% VO2max untrained, ~70-80% VO2max in trained athletes
LT2 = OBLA = second inflection, ~4 mmol/L; often called anaerobic threshold
MLSS = highest intensity where lactate production = clearance; heavy→severe transition; ≈ critical power/critical speed
MLSS often a better endurance predictor than VO2max or LT alone
Training near/above LT/OBLA shifts them RIGHT → higher %VO2max without acidosis (↑mito, ↓catecholamines, more FFA use)
Zone models anchor to lactate/ventilatory thresholds: Z1 <2 mmol/L, threshold work 2-4 mmol/L, Z3 >4 mmol/L
Above-MLSS work (~85% VO2max) is short (20-30 min) as lactate accumulates to failure
As VO2max/LT/MLSS improve, a fixed absolute intensity becomes a lower relative stress → must re-anchor zones
44.training_link: Your indoor LT1=147 and LT2=158 are the anchors for every zone you train — a Z2 cap of 132 sits safely under LT1 so easy work stays truly aerobic. Your 4×8 at HR 170-175 is deliberately supra-MLSS, above LT2, where lactate accumulates and forces the threshold-shift adaptation. As your engine improves, re-test: the same watts will drift to a lower relative stress, so re-anchor rather than chasing old HR numbers.

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán: Deep Dive into Zone 2 (Part 2)

glossary.def: Lactate threshold — first abrupt rise of blood lactate above baseline as glycolytic reliance grows. Corresponds to the ventilatory threshold and the top of the aerobic (easy) zone.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 6 34 C 30 34 40 30 50 18 C 58 9 66 7 74 6' stroke='#5ec8ff' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><line x1='6' y1='34' x2='74' y2='34' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

glossary.def: Second lactate inflection (OBLA, ~4 mmol/L), closely tracking the maximal lactate steady state — the highest intensity where lactate appearance still equals clearance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 6 34 C 30 34 40 30 50 18 C 58 9 66 7 74 6' stroke='#ff7a7a' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><line x1='6' y1='34' x2='74' y2='34' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

glossary.def: Onset of blood lactate accumulation — the intensity at which blood lactate reaches ~4 mmol/L; used as the LT2/anaerobic-threshold marker.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Maximal lactate steady state — highest workload where production equals clearance so lactate plateaus; marks the heavy-to-severe boundary and is synonymous with critical power/speed.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: The breakpoint in the ventilation-vs-VO2 relationship; tracks LT1 and is used as a non-invasive threshold marker.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Training-induced rightward move of LT/OBLA (↑mitochondria, ↓catecholamine release, more fat use) letting an athlete hold a higher %VO2max before lactate accumulates.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Setting HR/power zones from measured thresholds (lactate or ventilatory) rather than generic %HRmax tables, and re-anchoring as fitness improves.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='6' y='8' width='14' height='26' rx='2' fill='#5ec8ff'/><rect x='33' y='20' width='14' height='14' rx='2' fill='#ffb86b'/><rect x='60' y='14' width='14' height='20' rx='2' fill='#ff7a7a'/></svg>

44.notes_5e: 5E adds LT1/LT2 nomenclature and explicitly aligns OBLA/LT2 with MLSS, critical power/speed, and the heavy-to-severe boundary; older editions leaned on a single 'anaerobic threshold' framing.

45.title: Zone 2, mitochondrial biogenesis & fat oxidation (San-Millán)
45.page_refs: 5E Ch 3 — Oxidative System, Fat Oxidation, LT/OBLA; Ch 6 — Muscular Adaptations to Aerobic Training. Zone-2/San-Millán extends 5E.
45.key_concept: The oxidative system rephosphorylates ATP in the mitochondria via the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain, burning both carbohydrate (~32 ATP/glucose) and fat, where beta-oxidation of one triglyceride yields >300 ATP. Aerobic training enlarges and multiplies mitochondria, raises oxidative-enzyme and capillary density, and increases stored triglyceride — so muscle burns more fat, spares glycogen, and shifts LT/OBLA rightward. Zone-2 (just below LT1) is the modern extension: the intensity that maximally drives Type-I fat oxidation with minimal lactate, chronically upregulating PGC-1α, mitochondrial biogenesis, and MCT1 clearance.
key_facts: Oxidative system = primary ATP source at rest + low intensity; uses CHO + fat (protein minor, rises >90 min work)
At rest ~70% ATP from fat, ~30% CHO; as intensity ↑, substrate shifts fat→CHO (near-100% CHO at high intensity)
1 glucose → ~32 ATP oxidatively (~34 from glycogen); glucose oxidation nets >90% of ATP via oxidative phosphorylation
Beta-oxidation: FFAs → acetyl-CoA into Krebs; 1 triglyceride (3× palmitic) yields >300 ATP → huge fat capacity
Krebs rate-limiter = isocitrate dehydrogenase (isocitrate→α-ketoglutarate); ↑ by ADP, inhibited by ATP
Aerobic training: ↑ mitochondrial size + number, ↑ myoglobin, ↑ oxidative enzymes, ↑ capillary density, ↑ stored triglyceride → fat use ↑, glycogen spared
LT1 ~50-60% VO2max untrained, ~70-80% trained; OBLA/LT2 ≈ 4 mmol/L blood lactate
Training shifts LT/OBLA right → hold higher %VO2max before lactate rises (↓ catecholamines, ↑ mito content)
Zone-2 (extension): just below LT1 = max fat-ox stimulus, near-baseline lactate; drives PGC-1α, biogenesis, MCT1 — dose-dependent on easy volume
45.training_link: Your Z2 cap at HR 132 indoor keeps you just under LT1, in the fat-ox sweet spot instead of leaking into the grey zone that stalls both endurance and recovery. The RHR 46→37 and HRV 118→187 (Jan→May) are the visible face of exactly this mitochondrial/autonomic remodeling — more mitochondria and MCT1 mean you clear the running-station lactate faster and hold sled/row pace with less glycogen cost. Bank the easy volume: the biogenesis signal is dose-dependent, so most Hyrox base weeks should be Z2, not threshold grinds.

glossary.def: Exercise intensity where blood lactate first rises abruptly above baseline (~50-60% VO2max untrained, 70-80% trained).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Onset of blood lactate accumulation; a second inflection where lactate ≈ 4 mmol/L, marking a higher sustainable intensity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M8 32 C30 30 40 26 54 12 L72 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="50" y1="6" x2="50" y2="34" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="50" cy="15" r="2.5" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Training-driven increase in mitochondrial size and number, raising oxidative ATP capacity and fat oxidation.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="26" cy="20" r="11" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M26 12v16M18 20h16" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="1.6"/><path d="M44 20h20M56 14l8 6-8 6" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Rate-limiting Krebs enzyme (isocitrate→α-ketoglutarate); stimulated by ADP, inhibited by ATP — links flux to energy demand.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

45.notes_5e: 5E keeps oxidative ATP yields modernized (~32/glucose, NADH≈2.5 / FADH2≈1.5 ATP rather than the older 3/2) and frames LT1 vs OBLA/LT2 explicitly; Zone-2/San-Millán is a post-textbook extension not in the 5E.

46.title: Concurrent training: the molecular interference effect
46.page_refs: 5E Ch 5 — Compatibility of Aerobic and Anaerobic Modes of Training (extends with molecular AMPK/mTOR model, Coffey & Hawley 2017)
46.key_concept: Training strength/power and aerobic endurance together creates a programming conflict: concurrent work can blunt strength and especially power gains — the interference effect. 5E frames it mainly as a fatigue problem — high-intensity, high-volume, or high-frequency aerobic work degrades the QUALITY of the resistance stimulus. The molecular model adds the mechanism: endurance activates AMPK (energy-sensing, mitochondrial, catabolic), resistance activates Akt/mTOR (protein synthesis, hypertrophy), and AMPK can suppress mTOR. Critically it is ASYMMETRIC — heavy lifting barely dents aerobic power and can improve economy, while endurance load impairs strength/RFD.
key_facts: Interference scales with aerobic INTENSITY, VOLUME, FREQUENCY — not low easy volume
ASYMMETRIC: endurance ↓ strength/power; heavy RT does NOT meaningfully ↓ V̇O₂max (can ↑ economy)
POWER/RFD hit hardest — more than slow-velocity max strength (Häkkinen: RT-only ↑RFD, concurrent didn't)
5E mechanism = fatigue degrading RT quality; molecular add-on = AMPK ↑ suppresses Akt/mTOR
Resistance → Akt/mTOR (+MAPK) drives MPS; endurance → AMPK favors mitochondrial biogenesis
Kraemer 5-group: combined grew only type IIa area; RT-only grew type I+IIa → cellular antagonism
↑ Recovery/separation ↓ interference: same-session 3×/wk shows less than 6 consecutive days
Sale: 4 days (2 RT + 2 aerobic) beat 2 days combined for leg-press 1RM (25% vs 13%)
Sequence matters — do the priority quality (usually lifting) fresh; separate hard modes
46.training_link: This is the load-bearing rule of your Hyrox week. Your hardest aerobic sessions — VO₂ intervals, mixed-modality, threshold running — are exactly what blunt strength and power, so keep your heaviest lifting (Lower-Heavy / power work) maximally separated from them, ideally on different days or AM/PM with hours between. Because the effect is asymmetric, you pay almost nothing for keeping heavy lifting in — it protects RFD and even sharpens running economy. Priority quality goes first, fresh.

46.notes_5e: 5E adds a full 'Compatibility of Aerobic and Anaerobic Modes' section framing interference as fatigue degrading RT quality (intensity/volume/frequency + recovery), stressing power/RFD is hardest hit and the effect is asymmetric; the AMPK/mTOR molecular model is a modern extension layered on the chapter's Akt/mTOR and AMPK references.

47.title: What limits endurance: VO₂max, threshold & economy
47.page_refs: 5E Ch 6 (Aerobic Power, Lactate Threshold, Economy) & Ch 21 (Factors Related to Aerobic Endurance Performance) — extends 5E with Joyner & Coyle determinants model
47.key_concept: Endurance performance rides on three semi-independent determinants. VO₂max sets the aerobic-power ceiling — the product of maximal cardiac output and a-vO₂ difference; in trained athletes it plateaus after ~6-12 months. Lactate/anaerobic threshold (LT, MLSS/critical power) fixes the % of that ceiling you can hold before lactate outpaces clearance — the strongest single predictor of race result. Economy is the O₂/energy cost of a set pace or power. Two athletes with identical VO₂max diverge on whoever holds the higher threshold or moves more economically, so mature athletes gain mainly from LT and economy, not the ceiling.
key_facts: Performance ≈ VO₂max × fractional utilization (%VO₂max at threshold) × economy
VO₂max = max cardiac output × a-vO₂ diff; mostly central (↑stroke volume, HRmax ~unchanged)
Untrained VO₂max ↑ roughly 5-30% (5E cites ~20% in the untrained); most gains within 6-12 mo, then plateaus → elites barely move it
LT/MLSS often a BETTER predictor of endurance result than VO₂max; OBLA classically ~4 mmol/L
Same VO₂max 50: LT at 80% (40) beats LT at 70% (35) — 5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ more usable power
Economy = O₂ cost per pace/power; biomechanics + neuromuscular efficiency (leg stiffness, elastic return)
MLSS ≡ critical power/critical speed = lactate production = clearance; heavy→severe boundary
Late gains come from LT + economy; HIIT + strength drive economy, tempo/threshold work drives LT
↑Economy can raise both usable VO₂max and LT for the same effort
47.training_link: Your VO₂max is already deep, so your marginal gains now live in threshold and economy, not the ceiling. Push LT2 up with tempo/threshold blocks and short HIIT above 90% VO₂max, and defend economy with run skill, leg stiffness, and heavy strength work — which is exactly why your strength block is structurally protected, not optional. For Hyrox, better running economy also buys you cheaper watts on the ergs and sled, so the same fitness carries you further into the back half.

47.notes_5e: 5E keeps the classic VO₂max/LT/economy triad but leans harder on MLSS = critical power as the heavy-to-severe boundary and often a better predictor than either VO₂max or LT.

48.title: Durability & aerobic decoupling (modern endurance)
48.page_refs: Extends 5E Ch 6 — Chronic Adaptations (cardiovascular, muscular, economy, lactate threshold) + Detraining
48.key_concept: 5E Ch 6 explains the aerobic base: chronic endurance training ↑ stroke volume, capillary + mitochondrial density, oxidative enzymes, glycogen/triglyceride stores and fat oxidation, so lactate threshold shifts to a higher %VO₂max and economy improves. But these markers are measured fresh, whereas races are decided tired. Durability (modern extension) is how well threshold, economy and power resist drift after hours of work. Aerobic decoupling quantifies it — the drift of HR relative to pace/power across a steady effort. Low decoupling (<~5%) on a long Z2 session signals a well-built base; higher flags a base gap or a pace above true Z2.
key_facts: 5E base adaptations: ↑ stroke volume, ↑ capillary + mitochondrial density, ↑ oxidative enzymes → ↑ a-vO₂ diff
Trained muscle spares glycogen + ↑ fat oxidation → prolongs same-intensity work
Lactate threshold shifts to higher %VO₂max; economy keeps improving after VO₂max plateaus (~6-12 mo)
Lab VO₂max/threshold measured FRESH → underpredicts fatigued race performance
Durability = resistance of threshold/economy/power to fatigue over hours (modern extension, not in 5E)
Aerobic decoupling = HR drift relative to pace/power on a steady effort; lower is better
<~5% decoupling ≈ solid base; >5% = under-built base OR effort exceeded true Zone 2
Built by accumulated Z2 volume + long steady sessions (same enzymatic/mitochondrial base 5E describes)
Aerobic enzymes are the most detraining-sensitive; VO₂max ↓4-14% short-term detraining
48.training_link: Your base training is exactly the 5E machinery — Z2 volume builds mitochondria, capillaries and fat oxidation that let threshold and economy hold late in a Hyrox. Your progress chart tracks within-session decoupling: Apr-May ran +7.66% (42% of sessions overreached) vs Aug-Nov +9.86% (65%), so you're measurably more durable now. Chase sub-5% on long steady Z2, and treat a decoupling spike on an easy day as a flag you drifted above true Zone 2.

glossary.def: Oxygen/energy cost of a given pace or power; better economy means a faster pace for the same VO₂ and keeps improving after VO₂max plateaus.

glossary.def: Trained muscle's shift toward greater fat oxidation, reducing glycogen use at a given intensity and extending time before fatigue.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

48.notes_5e: 5E covers the aerobic base (economy, threshold shift, fat oxidation, detraining sensitivity) but durability and aerobic decoupling are a modern endurance framework layered on top, not textbook terms.

49.title: HIIT & interval prescription (long intervals, supra-MLSS)
49.page_refs: 5E Ch 21 — Interval / HIIT / Pace-Tempo training (extends 5E with Seiler & Buchheit-Laursen)
49.key_concept: Interval methods sit at the top of the aerobic-intensity ladder. Classic interval work runs 3-5 min close to VO₂max at a 1:1 work:rest ratio; HIIT pushes above MLSS/critical VO₂ with brief recoveries. Every prescription is set by four dials — work duration, work intensity, recovery duration, recovery intensity — tuned to a target system. For threshold/VO₂max gains, longer HIIT reps (2-4 min at/above ~90% VO₂max) with incomplete recovery accumulate the most total time near VO₂max while staying repeatable. Pacing is the skill: hold an even supra-MLSS effort across the whole series rather than a heroic first rep that fades.
key_facts: 4 dials: work duration, work intensity, recovery duration, recovery intensity → set to target a system
Interval training: 3-5 min (down to 30 s) close to VO₂max; W:R ≈ 1:1 for 3-5 min reps
HIIT: bouts above MLSS / critical VO₂ + brief recovery; goal = several min >90% VO₂max/session
Long-interval HIIT example: 2-3 min ≥90% VO₂max, ~2 min passive/low active recovery
Short (<45 s) reps → neuromuscular power + economy; long (2-4 min) → ↑glycolysis, lactate, LT
Tabata: 20 s work / 10 s rest × 7-8 → exhaustion
Pace/tempo = at/slightly above MLSS (threshold); steady 20-30 min OR intermittent 'cruise intervals'
Recovery too short → poor quality reps + injury risk; too long → lose glycolytic stimulus
Interval work is very stressful → use sparingly, only on a firm aerobic base
49.training_link: Your data shows 4×8 chronically under-paced (HR 146-151 vs ~170 target) while 6- and 7-min reps are your sweet spots — so anchor on 6×5 + 7×4 and hold the 4×8 at ~170-175 mean HR, 7-10 mmol lactate, 2-min recoveries, supra-MLSS. Treat recovery as series maintenance, not full reset: keep it short enough to stay >90% VO₂max but long enough to protect rep quality. For Hyrox, this is your run-station transfer — even pacing under fatigue beats a fast opener that dies.

glossary.def: Highest intensity where lactate production = clearance and blood lactate holds steady; the heavy→severe boundary. Synonymous with critical power/speed.

49.notes_5e: 5E frames HIIT via MLSS/critical VO₂ and cites Buchheit-Laursen's 'several minutes >90% VO₂max' dose; this lesson extends that base with Seiler polarized/pacing concepts not in the text.

50.title: Hyrox race demands & compromised-running pacing
50.page_refs: Extends 5E Ch 21 — Training Zones; Types of Aerobic Training (LT/MLSS/economy)
50.key_concept: Hyrox interleaves 8 × 1 km runs with 8 functional stations (ski-erg, sled push, sled pull, burpee broad jumps, row, farmers carry, sandbag lunges, wall balls). The defining demand is 'compromised running' — sustaining run economy on pre-fatigued, lactate-loaded legs with HR already near threshold off the prior station. Ch 21 physiology explains why: MLSS (≈ critical power/speed, the heavy→severe boundary) caps sustainable pace, and once effort exceeds it lactate climbs continually toward failure. With 8 km of running, fatigued economy dominates the result; the sleds and grip stations spike lactate above MLSS, so even-to-negative pacing beats redlining early.
key_facts: Format: 8 × 1 km runs alternating with 8 stations (ski, sled push, sled pull, burpee broad jump, row, farmers carry, sandbag lunge, wall balls)
Compromised running = holding economy on lactate-loaded, pre-fatigued legs (the core skill)
MLSS = heavy→severe boundary; = critical power/critical speed; above it lactate rises continually → failure
Zone ref points = LT/ventilatory threshold; low zone <2 mmol/L, threshold 2-4 mmol/L, high >4 mmol/L
8 km total run volume → fatigued run economy dominates the overall time
Sled push/pull + grip = biggest elite-to-average gap; they push effort above MLSS
Pace/tempo (threshold) work sits ≈ MLSS, ~20-30 min, drives LT + economy — the key Hyrox engine builder
Even/negative pacing wins; redlining early sleds detonates back-half runs
Elites run ~80% of volume at extensive-endurance (easy) pace — polarized base
50.training_link: Your Mixed-Modality Engine sessions train exactly the station→run transition — practice re-establishing turnover while sitting right at MLSS, not above it. Anchor most run volume to easy extensive-endurance pace and build the ceiling with pace/tempo work at threshold; sled pull + grip is your flagged gap, the highest-ROI station to drill. Heat blunts this MLSS-anchored work and pushes you severe early, hence your cool-venue race-specificity rule.

glossary.def: Speed or %VO2max where blood lactate rises above resting (~4 mmol/L reference); a key anchor for setting training zones.

50.notes_5e: 5E frames zones around LT/ventilatory thresholds and equates MLSS with critical power/critical speed (heavy→severe boundary) — a more explicit CP/CS framing than earlier editions; Hyrox pacing here is an applied extension.

51.title: Sled push/pull mechanics & the force-velocity profile
51.page_refs: 5E Ch 17 — Nontraditional Implements (sled push/pull); Ch 2 — Force–Velocity Curve & Strength-to-Mass Ratio
51.key_concept: Sled work is horizontal-force production: you accelerate an implement forward via ground reaction, so load selection slides you along the force–velocity relationship. The classic 5E curve (Fig 2.13) is torque vs joint angular velocity — concentric torque falls as speed ↑, eccentric torque exceeds concentric. Applied to sleds, heavy loads (the 5E cites ~80% body mass for sprint studies and 85% 1RM back squat for heavy pulls) sit at the high-force/low-velocity end and build acceleration mechanics; lighter loads shift toward high velocity/turnover. Technique: hands low → near-horizontal trunk and shin, feet together to break a heavy sled inertia, short fast steps first, then lengthen stride with shorter ground contacts; front foot stays behind the hip.
key_facts: Sled = horizontal (propulsive) force task, driven by ground reaction force
Force–velocity curve (Fig 2.13): concentric torque ↓ as angular velocity ↑; eccentric > concentric torque
Eccentric torque rises until ~90°/s then declines → greatest force is eccentric
Heavy sled (↑load) → high-force/low-velocity end; light sled → high-velocity/turnover
Heavy sled sprint studies used ~80% body mass → ↑ horizontal force + 5 m sprint
Heavy sled pull example: 85% 1RM back squat, 3×25 m
PAPE sled loads ≈ 66–75% body mass primed subsequent sprints
Lower hand position → more horizontal trunk/shin → more propulsive force
Break heavy sled with feet together; use split stance for lighter/sprint-like starts
Push cue: front foot behind the hip; short fast steps → longer strides, shorter contacts
Strength-to-mass ratio governs acceleration; ↑mass must not outpace ↑force
51.training_link: Sled push/pull is your flagged biggest Hyrox gap, so program both ends of the curve: heavy sleds (the 5E research spans ~80% body mass for sprint work up to 85% 1RM back squat for pulls) to build the acceleration/horizontal-force mechanics the race demands, plus lighter loads for turnover on tired legs. Drill low hands and a near-horizontal shin so force goes forward, not down, and rehearse the feet-together break under fatigue since the Hyrox sled is a dead-stop, high-inertia start. Keep sessions in cool conditions so technique quality isn't heat-capped.

glossary.def: During shortening actions, peak torque declines as angular velocity increases; you cannot be maximally fast and maximally forceful at once.

glossary.def: Lengthening muscle actions can produce greater torque than concentric, rising until ~90°/s — why heavier loads can be lowered than lifted.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Force capability relative to the mass being accelerated; higher ratio = better acceleration in sprinting, jumping, and sled starts.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

51.notes_5e: 5E adds a full nontraditional-implements section with dedicated sled push/pull/drag technique and load-specific horizontal-force research not present in older editions; the force–velocity curve itself is stable.

52.title: Periodization for hybrid athletes (concurrent load)
52.page_refs: 5E Ch 22 — Models of Periodization (parallel/emphasis/sequential); Fitness–Fatigue; DUP vs linear. Modern concurrent/hybrid framing extends 5E Ch 22.
52.key_concept: 5E frames planning as three models: parallel (all qualities at once — best for novices, load ceiling limits advanced athletes), sequential (concentrate one quality per block), and emphasis (target several simultaneously but rotate focus ~every 2 weeks, some qualities developing, some maintained, some allowed to detrain). Hybrid athletes are a concurrent-load problem: strength and endurance advanced together, so the emphasis model fits best — lean each mesocycle toward the limiting quality while holding the other. Sequencing is governed by fitness–fatigue: fatigue clears ~two-thirds faster than fitness, so hard priority work goes in the freshest slots and volume/emphasis is periodized while intensity distribution stays roughly fixed.
key_facts: 3 planning models: parallel (concurrent, all factors together), sequential (one factor/block), emphasis (rotate focus)
Parallel = best for novices/developmental; advanced athletes exceed training tolerance as total load must keep rising
Emphasis model: multiple qualities at once, focus rotated ~every 2 wks; some qualities ↑, some maintained, some detrain slightly
Fitness–fatigue: prepared­ness = fitness − fatigue; fatigue dissipates ~2/3 faster than fitness → taper reveals fitness
DUP = within-microcycle swings in load/volume/focus (e.g., 6RM, then 10RM, then 3RM days)
"Linear" is a misnomer — traditional model is nonlinear at micro/meso level (volume-load undulates even at fixed reps)
Give the limiting quality's hard session the freshest slot; periodize volume/emphasis, hold the polarized distribution ~constant
Mesocycle typically 2–6 wks (4 most common); microcycle usually 1 wk
52.training_link: You are the emphasis model in the flesh — strength and endurance advanced together, so lean each mesocycle toward the limiting quality (threshold → VO₂max → consolidation) while holding the other. Run your DUP rotation (Lower / Upper-Grip / Power / Upper-Hyper) inside the ~88-day macro, and put the limiting quality's hard session in the freshest slot so interference and fatigue don't blunt it. Periodize volume and emphasis at the block level, but keep the polarized ~80/20 distribution roughly constant — don't periodize that.

video.title: Huberman Lab — Andy Galpin: Optimal Protocols to Build Strength

audio.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 101

glossary.def: Targets all training factors within one session/day/microcycle. Suits novices; advanced athletes outgrow it as total load must keep climbing past tolerance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Hybrid of parallel + sequential — several qualities trained at once but the focus rotates (~2 wk); some are pushed, some maintained, some allowed to detrain. Best fit for concurrent/hybrid athletes.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Concentrates one (or few) quality per block, sequenced so residual effects carry into the next. Needs longer prep runways; poor fit for dense schedules.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Every bout produces both fitness and fatigue aftereffects that summate into preparedness; because fatigue clears faster than fitness, unloading raises preparedness.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Daily undulating periodization — load, volume, and focus swing session-to-session within the microcycle rather than block-to-block.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 6 34 C 30 34 40 30 50 18 C 58 9 66 7 74 6' stroke='#ffb86b' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><line x1='6' y1='34' x2='74' y2='34' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

glossary.def: Ordering days/sessions so hard endurance is kept away from heavy lifting and the priority quality gets fresh slots, limiting the interference effect.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='16' cy='20' r='9' fill='#5ec8ff'/><line x1='28' y1='20' x2='52' y2='20' stroke='#ffd84a' stroke-width='2.5'/><polygon points='52,15 62,20 52,25' fill='#ffd84a'/><circle cx='70' cy='20' r='9' fill='#a33344'/></svg>

glossary.def: Block of linked microcycles, 2–6 wks (4 most common), carrying a single dominant training goal.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="8" y="22" width="16" height="10" fill="#5ec8ff"/><rect x="26" y="16" width="16" height="16" fill="#67e8b0"/><rect x="44" y="10" width="16" height="22" fill="#ffb86b"/><rect x="62" y="20" width="10" height="12" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".6"/></svg>

52.notes_5e: 5E (Haff) reorganizes planning into three explicit models — parallel/emphasis/sequential — and repeatedly stresses that 'linear periodization' is a false label, giving hybrid/concurrent load a cleaner home in the emphasis model than older editions.

53.title: Heat acclimatization & thermoregulation
53.page_refs: 5E Ch 6 — Heat Acclimatization; Ch 10 — Fluid & Electrolytes; extends with Périard 2015
53.key_concept: In heat, blood is split between skin (cooling) and working muscle (metabolic demand): skin blood flow rises, stroke volume falls, HR climbs and performance drops — so HR at a given pace lies. Sweat losses >fluid intake drive hypohydration: ↑core temp, ↓plasma volume, ↑HR, ↑RPE, and impaired power/strength once ≥2% body mass is lost. Repeated heat exposure (~10-14 d, ~75% of adaptation in 4-6 d) yields acclimatization — earlier/greater sweating, lower sweat-onset and core/skin temps, expanded plasma volume, lower HR — restoring much lost capacity but decaying ~2.5%/day once heat exposure stops.
key_facts: Heat → skin blood flow competes with muscle → ↓SV, ↑HR, ↓performance
≥2% body-mass fluid loss impairs power, strength, endurance; ↑core temp, ↓SV/CO
Acclimatization: ↑sweat rate + earlier onset, ↓sweat-onset temp, ↓core/skin temp, ↑plasma volume, ↓HR
~75% of adaptation within 4-6 d; full picture ~10-14 d
Decay ~2.5% per day without heat; largely lost in ~1 month; keep training to retain
Heat acclimation expands plasma volume → ↑SV (unlike altitude, which acutely ↓plasma volume)
Sweat electrolytes: mostly Na+/Cl-, then K+, Mg2+, Ca2+; Na loss 0.2->12.5 g/L
Post-loss rehydrate ~1.5 L per kg body-mass lost (with electrolytes) when recovery <12 h
Over-drinking plain water when sweating heavily → hyponatremia (blood Na <130 mmol/L)
53.training_link: Your 31-32°C thermo ceiling and the 'heat costs more recovery than the 4×8s do' rule come straight from this competition-for-cardiac-output physiology — cap heat-season quality on effort, not pace, and save race-specific intervals for cool venues. Weigh in/out to know your true sweat rate, then replace ~1.5 L per kg lost with sodium (not plain water) so you don't drift into hyponatremia on long Hyrox runs. If a hot race looms, bank ~10-14 days of heat exposure but keep touching heat right up to it, since the plasma-volume gains decay ~2.5%/day.

glossary.def: Core heat adaptation: aldosterone-driven rise in plasma raises blood volume, supporting both skin cooling and stroke volume.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='6' y='8' width='14' height='26' rx='2' fill='#5ec8ff'/><rect x='33' y='20' width='14' height='14' rx='2' fill='#ffb86b'/><rect x='60' y='14' width='14' height='20' rx='2' fill='#ff7a7a'/></svg>

glossary.def: Gradual HR rise at steady effort as skin blood flow and hypohydration erode stroke volume, so HR overstates true intensity.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 8 18 L 22 18 L 28 8 L 38 32 L 46 20 L 54 20 L 60 14 L 72 14' stroke='#ff7a7a' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/></svg>

glossary.def: Chronic thermoregulatory adaptation from repeated heat exposure — earlier/larger sweating, lower core/skin temps, expanded plasma, lower HR.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 6 34 C 30 34 40 30 50 18 C 58 9 66 7 74 6' stroke='#ffb86b' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><line x1='6' y1='34' x2='74' y2='34' stroke='#888' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

glossary.def: Fluid deficit from sweat exceeding intake; ≥2% body-mass loss raises core temp and cuts stroke volume, cardiac output and performance.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M40 8 C48 20 54 26 54 31 a14 14 0 0 1-28 0 C26 26 32 20 40 8Z" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".85"/></svg>

glossary.def: Fluid lost per hour = (pre-weight − post-weight + fluid drunk − urine) / time; guides individual fluid needs.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M40 8 C48 20 54 26 54 31 a14 14 0 0 1-28 0 C26 26 32 20 40 8Z" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity=".85"/></svg>

glossary.def: Dangerously low blood sodium (<130 mmol/L) from over-drinking low-sodium fluid during long sweaty efforts; risks cerebral edema.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

53.notes_5e: 5E adds a dedicated Heat Acclimatization section (Ch 6) with a mechanism figure comparing heat vs altitude plasma-volume effects, and expands Ch 10 hydration/electrolyte guidance beyond older editions.

54.title: Recovery, HRV monitoring & sleep as the limiter
54.page_refs: 5E Ch 24 — Overtraining/Overreaching Defined, Allostatic Load, Assessment & Recovery; HRV/sleep monitoring extends 5E Ch 24
54.key_concept: Adaptation follows the GAS: stress → alarm → resistance → adaptation, but only if recovery matches the load. Overtraining (OT) is the process — excess volume/intensity beyond recovery capacity — that yields the overtraining syndrome (OTS), a long-term (weeks–months) performance drop plus maladaptation symptoms. Overreaching is the same but milder: functional OR super-compensates after days–weeks; nonfunctional OR (NFOR) only returns to baseline. Off-field stressors sum into the allostatic load. Speed drops first, strength last. HRV and sleep are the modern daily-readiness levers layered onto this monitoring frame.
key_facts: OT = the process (excess load > recovery); OTS = the result (long-term ↓performance + maladaptation, restore in weeks–months)
Overreaching = same but short-term; recover in days–weeks
Functional OR (FOR) → planned, super-compensates; NFOR → only back to baseline, no benefit, longer recovery
OTS subtypes: SOTS (sympathetic, ↑epinephrine → β2 down-regulation; strength/power) vs POTS (parasympathetic, ↓HR/↓epinephrine; endurance, huge volumes)
Onset order: speed ↓ first → power → maximal strength ↓ LAST (strength well-protected)
Allostatic load = cumulative wear from ALL stressors (training + sleep, travel, job, nutrition, illness)
Not all underperformance = OT; mistimed peak, undertraining, illness, RED-S all mimic it
Monitor tiers: Cat I daily/cheap/fast → Cat II every few days → Cat III occasional/intrusive; escalate only if flagged
Recovery = cut volume/intensity (mandatory); OR recovers ~1 wk, severe OT up to 4 wk (RE) or months–year (endurance volume)
Recovery-strategy evidence (Table 24.4): modifying the program is rated 'Most strong'; quality sleep and training taper are both 'Strong'; HRV/RHR are Cat-I cardiovascular readiness markers
54.training_link: Map your monitoring the way Ch 24 does: WHOOP HRV + RHR are your Category-I daily markers (HRV +59%, RHR -9 bpm Jan→May = real adaptation, resistance phase working). But watch the trend, not one noisy morning. Sleep is your genuine limiter (median ~5.5h vs ~9.8h need) — one of the strongest-evidence recovery levers in Table 24.4, behind only modifying the program itself — which is why v9.8 gates any load bump behind a 7-day-median readiness check, keeping your Hyrox blocks in functional-OR territory instead of tipping into NFOR before race day.

glossary.def: OT is the process — chronic load exceeding recovery. OTS is the result: long-term performance decrement (weeks–months) with maladaptation signs.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: FOR is planned short-term overload that super-compensates after brief rest. NFOR accumulates more fatigue, recovers slower, and only returns to baseline — no benefit.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Cumulative 'wear and tear' from all stress antecedents — training plus off-field factors (sleep, job, travel, nutrition) — that together can tip into OTS.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Sympathetic OTS (↑epinephrine, β2 down-regulation; strength/power) vs parasympathetic OTS (↓resting HR & epinephrine; high-volume endurance).
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="24" cy="20" r="9" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M40 20h22M54 13l8 7-8 7" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Heart-rate variability — beat-to-beat variation indexing parasympathetic (recovery) tone; a Category-I daily readiness marker. Track the multi-week trend, not one reading.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M 8 18 L 22 18 L 28 8 L 38 32 L 46 20 L 54 20 L 60 14 L 72 14' stroke='#67e8b0' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/></svg>

glossary.def: Adjusting the day's load to morning recovery markers (HRV, resting HR, sleep) instead of following a fixed plan.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 80 40' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='16' cy='20' r='9' fill='#5ec8ff'/><line x1='28' y1='20' x2='52' y2='20' stroke='#ffd84a' stroke-width='2.5'/><polygon points='52,15 62,20 52,25' fill='#ffd84a'/><circle cx='70' cy='20' r='9' fill='#67e8b0'/></svg>

glossary.def: Accumulated shortfall vs sleep need; erodes quality sleep, which Table 24.4 rates as a strongly-supported recovery strategy, and raises injury/illness and OTS risk.

54.notes_5e: New standalone 5E chapter (Fry & Nindl): formalizes OT-as-process vs OTS-as-result, allostatic load, SOTS/POTS, and a Cat I–III monitoring escalation with HRV, RHR and sleep as daily markers — HRV/readiness detail is the modern extension.
